topics.title: Determination of specific gravity, density and viscosity of the given liquid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL407 - FLUID MECHANICS, OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS & HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of specific gravity, density and viscosity of the given liquid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Specific Gravity (Relative Density)

**Formal Definition:** Specific Gravity (also called **Relative Density**) of a liquid is defined as the ratio of the **weight (or mass) of a given volume of the liquid** to the **weight (or mass) of an equal volume of standard liquid** (usually pure water at **4°C**, where water has its maximum density of **1000 kg/m³**).

Mathematically:
$$\text{Specific Gravity (S)} = \frac{\text{Weight of liquid}}{\text{Weight of equal volume of water at 4°C}} = \frac{\rho_{\text{liquid}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Specific Gravity is a **dimensionless quantity** (no units). It is numerically equal to the density of the liquid in the CGS system (g/cm³) and 1/1000th of the SI value (kg/m³).

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine you have two identical bottles. You fill one with the given liquid and the other with pure water. If the liquid bottle weighs twice as much as the water bottle, then the liquid's specific gravity is **2**. So specific gravity essentially tells you "**how heavy the liquid is compared to water**."

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU lab examinations, students are expected to know that for water, SG = 1, density = 1000 kg/m³, and these serve as the reference standard.

---

## 1.2 Density (Mass Density, ρ)

**Formal Definition:** Density of a fluid is defined as its **mass per unit volume**. It is a measure of how tightly packed the molecules of a fluid are.

$$\rho = \frac{\text{Mass (m)}}{\text{Volume (V)}} \quad [\text{Units: kg/m}^3 \text{ or g/cc}]$$

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a sponge and a stone of the same size. The stone is heavier because it has more **mass packed into the same volume** — so it has higher density. Similarly, **honey is denser than oil** because a cup of honey weighs more than a cup of oil.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Values:** Water at 4°C → ρ = **1000 kg/m³**, Mercury → ρ = **13,600 kg/m³**, Air at STP → ρ = **1.225 kg/m³**.

---

## 1.3 Viscosity (Dynamic Viscosity, μ)

**Formal Definition:** Viscosity is the **internal resistance of a fluid to flow** (or deformation). It arises due to **internal friction** between adjacent fluid layers moving at different velocities.

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{\left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress (N/m²)
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient (rate of shear strain, s⁻¹)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity (N·s/m² or Pa·s or Poiseuille)

### Kinematic Viscosity
$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \quad [\text{Units: m}^2/\text{s or Stokes}]$$

### Conceptual Analogy
- **Honey** is more viscous than **water** — it pours slowly because molecules resist sliding past each other.
- Imagine a deck of cards: when you push the top card, it slides easily, but the card at the bottom stays still. The **"stickiness"** between layers is viscosity.
- Viscosity is the **"thickness"** or **"stickiness"** of a fluid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** For a Newtonian fluid, shear stress is **directly proportional** to the velocity gradient. The constant of proportionality is the **dynamic viscosity (μ)**.

---

## 1.4 Significance in Engineering

| Property | Real-World Engineering Use |
|----------|---------------------------|
| Specific Gravity | Used in designing **hydrometers**, calculating **buoyancy** in ships/submarines, quality control of fuels and lubricants |
| Density | Used in calculating **hydrostatic pressure**, **Bernoulli's equation**, designing **storage tanks**, and **pipe flow** problems |
| Viscosity | Critical in **lubricant selection**, **pipe flow design** (Reynolds number), **pump performance**, and **boundary layer** analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> In a typical KTU lab exam, students are given a liquid (commonly **glycerine**, **kerosene**, **engine oil**, or **salt solution**) and must experimentally determine all three properties and compare with standard tables.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Measurement of Specific Gravity

### Method 1: Specific Gravity Bottle (Pycnometer)
- A clean, dry **specific gravity bottle** of known volume (V) is weighed empty: $W_1$
- The bottle is filled with water and weighed: $W_2$
- The bottle is emptied, dried, filled with the **test liquid** and weighed: $W_3$

$$S = \frac{\text{Weight of test liquid}}{\text{Weight of equal volume of water}} = \frac{W_3 - W_1}{W_2 - W_1}$$

### Method 2: Hydrometer
- A hydrometer is a **floating glass instrument** with a graduated stem.
- It works on **Archimedes' Principle**: A floating body displaces its own weight of the liquid.
- The **deeper** it sinks, the **lower** the specific gravity of the liquid.

### Method 3: U-Tube Manometer
- The same manometer is connected to two fluids. The pressure at the interface is equated:

$$S = \frac{h_{\text{water}}}{h_{\text{liquid}}}$$

## 2.2 Measurement of Density

Density is calculated **directly from specific gravity**:

$$\rho_{\text{liquid}} = S \times \rho_{\text{water}} = S \times 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

Or **directly from mass and volume**:

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V}$$

## 2.3 Measurement of Viscosity (Capillary Tube Viscometer Method)

The most common KTU lab method uses a **capillary viscometer** (e.g., Ostwald viscometer) based on **Hagen-Poiseuille Flow**.

### Operating Principle
- A fixed volume (V) of liquid flows through a **thin capillary tube** of radius **r** and length **L** under a **constant pressure head h**.
- The **time of flow** (t) is measured.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Compare with Water?** The exact dimensions of the capillary (r, L) are difficult to measure accurately. To eliminate these, we run **water** (a known viscosity liquid) through the same apparatus under identical conditions and use a **relative comparison method**.

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Specific Gravity | $S = \dfrac{W_3 - W_1}{W_2 - W_1}$ | Dimensionless | Using pycnometer |
| 2 | Specific Gravity (alt) | $S = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{liquid}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$ | Dimensionless | Direct definition |
| 3 | Density | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | kg/m³ | Direct measurement |
| 4 | Density (from SG) | $\rho_{\text{liquid}} = S \times 1000$ | kg/m³ | Quick calculation |
| 5 | Dynamic Viscosity (Absolute) | $\mu = \dfrac{\pi r^4 \rho g h t}{8 V L}$ | N·s/m² | Hagen-Poiseuille |
| 6 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | m²/s | Ratio |
| 7 | Relative Viscosity (KTU Method) | $\mu_r = \dfrac{\mu_1}{\mu_2} = \dfrac{\rho_1 t_1}{\rho_2 t_2}$ | Dimensionless | **Most used in labs** |
| 8 | Dynamic Viscosity (lab) | $\mu_1 = \mu_w \times \dfrac{\rho_1 t_1}{\rho_w t_w}$ | N·s/m² | Subscript 1 = test, w = water |
| 9 | Shear Stress | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | N/m² | Newton's law |
| 10 | Kinematic Viscosity (Stokes) | $1 \text{ Stokes} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ | — | CGS to SI conversion |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget that in the capillary viscometer equation, the **pressure head (h)** is the height difference between liquid levels — NOT the length of the capillary tube.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic machines (pumps, turbines):** Viscosity of the working fluid (often water or oil) directly affects **efficiency** and **cavitation**.
- **Lubrication engineering:** Viscosity index of oils determines their suitability at high/low temperatures.
- **Pipeline design:** Viscosity affects **head loss** (friction factor f in Darcy-Weisbach equation).
- **Hydraulic structures:** Density of seawater vs freshwater affects **hydrostatic thrust** on dams and offshore structures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Capillary Viscometer Equation (Hagen-Poiseuille Law)

### Starting Assumptions
- **Steady, laminar, incompressible flow** through a horizontal capillary tube.
- **Fully developed** velocity profile (parabolic).
- **No-slip** condition at the wall.
- Fluid is **Newtonian**.

### Step 1: Force Balance on a Fluid Cylinder
Consider a fluid cylinder of radius **r** and length **L** inside the capillary. The pressure force drives the flow; the viscous shear force resists it.

$$\text{Pressure Force} = \Delta P \times \pi r^2$$

$$\text{Shear Force} = \tau \times (2\pi r L)$$

For equilibrium (steady state):

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L$$

### Step 2: Express Shear Stress from Newton's Law

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

For a cylindrical coordinate system, $y$ becomes the radial distance from the wall, so:

$$\tau = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$$

The negative sign indicates velocity decreases as **r** increases (away from the centerline).

### Step 3: Substitute and Integrate

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = -\mu \frac{du}{dr} \cdot 2\pi r L$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{du}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 \mu L}$$

Integrating with respect to **r**:

$$u = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r^2}{4 \mu L} + C$$

### Step 4: Apply Boundary Condition
At the wall ($r = R$), $u = 0$ (no-slip condition):

$$0 = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4 \mu L} + C \implies C = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4 \mu L}$$

### Step 5: Velocity Profile Equation

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} (R^2 - r^2)$$

This is a **parabolic velocity profile**, maximum at the centerline ($r = 0$).

### Step 6: Total Volumetric Flow Rate (Q)
Integrate velocity over the circular cross-section:

$$Q = \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_0^R (R^2 - r^2) r \, dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[\frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4}\right]_0^R = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}$$

### Step 7: Final Hagen-Poiseuille Equation

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P \cdot R^4}{8 \mu L}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that **Q ∝ R⁴** — the flow rate is **extremely sensitive** to the capillary radius. A 2× increase in radius gives a **16× increase** in flow rate.

### Step 8: Express in Terms of Volume (V) and Time (t)
Since $Q = \dfrac{V}{t}$ and $\Delta P = \rho g h$ (hydrostatic pressure due to liquid column of height h):

$$\frac{V}{t} = \frac{\pi \rho g h R^4}{8 \mu L}$$

Solving for **μ**:

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{\pi R^4 \rho g h t}{8 V L}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Comparative Viscosity Formula (KTU Lab Method)

This is the **most important formula** for KTU lab exams.

### Step 1: Write the Equation for Test Liquid

$$\mu_1 = \frac{\pi R^4 \rho_1 g h_1 t_1}{8 V L}$$

### Step 2: Write the Equation for Water (Same Apparatus, Same V)

$$\mu_w = \frac{\pi R^4 \rho_w g h_w t_w}{8 V L}$$

### Step 3: Divide the Two Equations

The terms $\dfrac{\pi R^4 g}{8 V L}$ **cancel out** because the apparatus is identical:

$$\frac{\mu_1}{\mu_w} = \frac{\rho_1 h_1 t_1}{\rho_w h_w t_w}$$

### Step 4: If Pressure Heads Are Equal (Same Liquid Volume Above Capillary)
In a typical Ostwald viscometer, $h_1 = h_w$ because the same volume of liquid is used. So:

$$\frac{\mu_1}{\mu_w} = \frac{\rho_1 t_1}{\rho_w t_w}$$

### Step 5: Final Working Formula (KTU Board Standard)

$$\boxed{\mu_1 = \mu_w \times \frac{\rho_1 \times t_1}{\rho_w \times t_w}}$$

Where:
- $\mu_1$ = Dynamic viscosity of test liquid (N·s/m²)
- $\mu_w$ = Dynamic viscosity of water at experimental temperature (N·s/m²)
- $\rho_1$ = Density of test liquid (kg/m³)
- $\rho_w$ = Density of water (kg/m³)
- $t_1$ = Time of flow of test liquid (s)
- $t_w$ = Time of flow of water (s)

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A specific gravity bottle weighs 28.5 g when empty, 78.5 g when filled with water, and 85.0 g when filled with a test liquid. The same liquid takes 85 seconds to flow through a capillary viscometer, while water takes 45 seconds. Given $\mu_w = 0.001$ N·s/m² at the experimental temperature, calculate the dynamic viscosity of the liquid.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Find Specific Gravity**

$$S = \frac{W_3 - W_1}{W_2 - W_1} = \frac{85.0 - 28.5}{78.5 - 28.5} = \frac{56.5}{50.0} = 1.13$$

**Step 2: Find Density**

$$\rho_1 = S \times \rho_w = 1.13 \times 1000 = 1130 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3: Find Dynamic Viscosity**

$$\mu_1 = \mu_w \times \frac{\rho_1 \times t_1}{\rho_w \times t_w} = 0.001 \times \frac{1130 \times 85}{1000 \times 45}$$

$$\mu_1 = 0.001 \times \frac{96050}{45000} = 0.001 \times 2.134 = 0.00213 \text{ N·s/m}^2$$

**Step 4: Find Kinematic Viscosity**

$$\nu_1 = \frac{\mu_1}{\rho_1} = \frac{0.00213}{1130} = 1.886 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** Always carry the unit through every step. Examiners specifically check for correct units. Marks are often deducted for writing 0.001 instead of 1.0 × 10⁻³ N·s/m².

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Table

| Method | Property Measured | Accuracy | Equipment | Used When |
|--------|------------------|----------|-----------|-----------|
| Pycnometer | Specific Gravity | High (±0.001) | Specific gravity bottle, balance | Lab standard |
| Hydrometer | Specific Gravity | Medium (±0.005) | Hydrometer jar | Quick field check |
| U-tube manometer | Specific Gravity | High | Mercury manometer | Differential measurements |
| Capillary viscometer | Viscosity (relative) | High | Ostwald/Ubbelohde | Newtonain fluids, low viscosity |
| Redwood viscometer | Viscosity (kinematic) | Medium | Redwood No. 1 or 2 | Oils, industrial fluids |
| Falling ball | Viscosity (dynamic) | Medium | Glass tube, ball | Opaque or high-viscosity fluids |
| Rotational viscometer | Viscosity (dynamic) | Very High | Spindle + cup | Non-Newtonian fluids |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Capillary Viscometer (Ostwald Type) — Functional Flow

This Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the capillary viscometer experiment — the fluid path, the control parameters, and the measurement outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Clean and dry viscometer]) --> fillA[Fill upper bulb with test liquid up to mark M1 using pipette]
    fillA --> mountA[Mount viscometer vertically in thermostat bath at constant T]
    mountA --> suckA[Draw liquid into measuring bulb using rubber bulb suck]
    suckA --> markA{Liquid level above mark M2?}
    markA --> |No| suckA
    markA --> |Yes| releaseA[Release suction and start stopwatch when liquid crosses M2]
    releaseA --> timeA[Stop stopwatch when liquid meniscus reaches mark M3]
    timeA --> recordA[Record time t1 as average of 3 trials]
    recordA --> repeatA[Repeat same procedure with distilled water to get time tw]
    repeatA --> weighA[Weigh empty pycnometer W1, with water W2, with test liquid W3]
    weighA --> calcA[Calculate Specific Gravity S, Density rho, Dynamic Viscosity mu1]
    calcA --> endA([Result tabulated with standard deviation])

    style startA fill:#e1f5ff
    style endA fill:#d4edda
    style mountA fill:#fff3cd
    style calcA fill:#f8d7da
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Three Measurements

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT SECTION]
        liqA([Test Liquid Sample])
        waterA([Distilled Water Reference])
    end

    subgraph APPARATUS[APPARATUS SET]
        pycA[Specific Gravity Bottle / Pycnometer]
        balA[Precision Electronic Balance]
        viscoA[Capillary Viscometer Ostwald Type]
        thermoA[Thermostatic Water Bath at 25 C]
        stopA[Digital Stopwatch Resolution 0.01 s]
    end

    subgraph MEASUREMENT[MEASUREMENT BLOCK]
        sgA[Specific Gravity = W3-W1 over W2-W1]
        densA[Density = Specific Gravity times 1000]
        viscA[Dynamic Viscosity = mu_w times rho1 t1 over rho_w t_w]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT / RESULTS]
        sgOutA[Specific Gravity S dimensionless]
        densOutA[Density rho in kg per m3]
        viscOutA[Dynamic Viscosity mu in N s per m2]
        kinOutA[Kinematic Viscosity nu = mu over rho]
    end

    liqA --> pycA
    pycA --> balA
    balA --> sgA
    sgA --> densA

    liqA --> viscoA
    waterA --> viscoA
    viscoA --> thermoA
    viscoA --> stopA
    stopA --> viscA
    densA --> viscA
    viscA --> viscOutA
    viscOutA --> kinOutA
    densA --> densOutA
    sgA --> sgOutA

    style INPUT fill:#e3f2fd
    style APPARATUS fill:#fff8e1
    style MEASUREMENT fill:#fce4ec
    style OUTPUT fill:#e8f5e9
```

## 4.3 Typical Lab Tabular Observation Format (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    tabA[Observation Table] --> tabB[Row 1: Specific Gravity Measurements]
    tabA --> tabC[Row 2: Viscosity Time Measurements]
    tabA --> tabD[Row 3: Calculated Results]

    tabB --> tabB1[Weight of empty bottle W1]
    tabB --> tabB2[Weight of bottle + water W2]
    tabB --> tabB3[Weight of bottle + test liquid W3]

    tabC --> tabC1[Trial 1: time t1, time tw]
    tabC --> tabC2[Trial 2: time t1, time tw]
    tabC --> tabC3[Trial 3: time t1, time tw]
    tabC --> tabC4[Average time t1_avg, tw_avg]

    tabD --> tabD1[Specific Gravity S]
    tabD --> tabD2[Density rho = S times 1000]
    tabD --> tabD3[Viscosity mu1 = muw times rho1 t1_avg over rho_w tw_avg]
    tabD --> tabD4[Kinematic Viscosity nu1 = mu1 over rho1]

    style tabA fill:#bbdefb
    style tabD fill:#c8e6c9
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always maintain the **same order** of observations — Specific Gravity first, then Viscosity. This matches the standard KTU lab record format and earns easy marks in the **viva voce** section.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A1]**
> **Q1. Define Specific Gravity. How is it determined using a specific gravity bottle?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Definition (1 mark):** Specific gravity of a liquid is the ratio of the weight of a given volume of the liquid to the weight of an equal volume of water at 4°C. It is dimensionless.
> **Procedure (2 marks):**
> 1. A clean, dry specific gravity bottle is weighed empty: $W_1$.
> 2. The bottle is filled with distilled water and weighed: $W_2$.
> 3. The bottle is emptied, dried thoroughly, filled with the test liquid, and weighed: $W_3$.
> 4. Specific gravity is calculated as: $S = \dfrac{W_3 - W_1}{W_2 - W_1}$

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A2]**
> **Q2. What is viscosity? Differentiate between dynamic and kinematic viscosity.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Viscosity is the property of a fluid that offers resistance to deformation or flow. It arises due to internal friction between fluid layers.
> **Differentiation (1.5 marks):**
>
> | Feature | Dynamic Viscosity (μ) | Kinematic Viscosity (ν) |
> |---------|----------------------|-------------------------|
> | Definition | Resistance to flow | Resistance to flow due to inertia |
> | Formula | $\mu = \tau / (du/dy)$ | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ |
> | Units | N·s/m² or Pa·s | m²/s or Stokes |
> | Depends on | Fluid's internal friction | Both μ and density |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam — Model Question B1]**

**Determine the specific gravity, density, and dynamic viscosity of the given liquid using a specific gravity bottle and capillary viscometer. Tabulate the observations and explain the procedure in detail with the relevant formulae.**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Specific Gravity & Density Determination (7 marks)**

**Apparatus Required:** Specific gravity bottle, distilled water, test liquid, precision balance, pipette.

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. Clean the specific gravity bottle thoroughly, dry it, and weigh it empty on the precision balance. Record as $W_1$.
2. Fill the bottle with distilled water up to the brim (ensure no air bubbles). Wipe dry from outside. Weigh and record as $W_2$.
3. Empty the bottle, rinse with the test liquid, fill with the test liquid up to the brim, wipe dry, and weigh. Record as $W_3$.
4. Repeat step 3 two more times and take the average value of $W_3$.

**Calculations (3 marks):**

$$S = \frac{W_3 - W_1}{W_2 - W_1}$$

$$\rho_{\text{liquid}} = S \times 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Result:** Specific Gravity = ___ ; Density = ___ kg/m³

---

**(b) Dynamic Viscosity Determination (7 marks)**

**Apparatus Required:** Ostwald capillary viscometer, distilled water, test liquid, stopwatch, thermostatic bath, rubber bulb.

**Theory (2 marks):** The viscosity is determined by comparing the time of flow of equal volumes of the test liquid and water through the same capillary under identical head conditions, using the formula:

$$\mu_1 = \mu_w \times \frac{\rho_1 \cdot t_1}{\rho_w \cdot t_w}$$

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Clean the viscometer with chromic acid, then with distilled water, and finally with acetone. Dry it in an oven.
2. Pipette a known volume (e.g., 10 mL) of distilled water into the lower bulb through the wider limb. Mount the viscometer vertically in a thermostatic bath at a constant temperature (e.g., 25°C).
3. Using a rubber bulb, suck the water up through the capillary until it fills the upper bulb above mark M₂.
4. Release the suction. Start the stopwatch as the meniscus passes mark M₂. Stop when it reaches mark M₃. Record time $t_w$.
5. Repeat three times and take the average. Empty, clean, dry the viscometer.
6. Repeat steps 2–5 with the test liquid to get $t_1$.

**Calculations (2 marks):**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $\rho_w$ (at 25°C) | 997 kg/m³ |
| $\mu_w$ (at 25°C) | 0.891 × 10⁻³ N·s/m² |
| $t_w$ (avg) | ___ s |
| $t_1$ (avg) | ___ s |
| $\rho_1$ | from part (a) |

$$\mu_1 = 0.891 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{\rho_1 \times t_1}{997 \times t_w} \text{ N·s/m}^2$$

**Result:** Dynamic Viscosity $\mu_1$ = ___ N·s/m² ; Kinematic Viscosity $\nu_1 = \mu_1 / \rho_1$ = ___ m²/s

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam — Model Question B2]**

**State and derive Hagen-Poiseuille equation. An oil of specific gravity 0.85 takes 90 seconds to flow through a capillary viscometer, while water takes 30 seconds at the same temperature. Find the dynamic and kinematic viscosity of the oil. Given: $\mu_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m² at the test temperature.**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Hagen-Poiseuille Equation (7 marks)**

**Statement (1 mark):** For steady, laminar, incompressible flow of a Newtonian fluid through a uniform circular cross-section capillary tube of radius R and length L under a constant pressure gradient $\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$, the volumetric flow rate is given by:

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

**Derivation (6 marks):** *(Refer to the step-by-step derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes.)*

Step 1: Force balance on fluid cylinder: $\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L$

Step 2: Newton's law of viscosity: $\tau = -\mu \dfrac{du}{dr}$

Step 3: Substituting and integrating with no-slip BC: $u(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$

Step 4: Total flow rate by integration: $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P \cdot R^4}{8\mu L}$

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- Specific gravity of oil, $S_{\text{oil}} = 0.85$
- $t_{\text{oil}} = 90$ s
- $t_w = 30$ s
- $\mu_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m²
- $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³

**Step 1: Density of oil (1 mark)**

$$\rho_{\text{oil}} = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2: Dynamic viscosity of oil (3 marks)**

$$\mu_{\text{oil}} = \mu_w \times \frac{\rho_{\text{oil}} \times t_{\text{oil}}}{\rho_w \times t_w} = (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times \frac{850 \times 90}{1000 \times 30}$$

$$\mu_{\text{oil}} = (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times \frac{76500}{30000} = (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times 2.55$$

$$\boxed{\mu_{\text{oil}} = 2.55 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N·s/m}^2}$$

**Step 3: Kinematic viscosity of oil (2 marks)**

$$\nu_{\text{oil}} = \frac{\mu_{\text{oil}}}{\rho_{\text{oil}}} = \frac{2.55 \times 10^{-3}}{850} = 3.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{\nu_{\text{oil}} = 3.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 3.0 \text{ cSt}}$$

**Result:** Dynamic viscosity of oil = $2.55 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m² ; Kinematic viscosity = $3.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Mixing CGS and SI units. Always convert $\mu_w$ to SI (N·s/m²) before substitution. **1 Poise = 0.1 N·s/m²**.
> 2. **Temperature Dependence:** Viscosity of water changes with temperature. Use the value corresponding to your bath temperature. At 25°C → $\mu_w = 0.891 \times 10^{-3}$; at 30°C → $\mu_w = 0.798 \times 10^{-3}$.
> 3. **Pressure Head Assumption:** In a well-designed viscometer, $h_1 \approx h_w$ only if equal volumes of liquid are used. Always state this assumption.
> 4. **Forgetting the Density Ratio:** Many students write $\mu_1 = \mu_w \times (t_1/t_w)$ and skip the $\rho_1/\rho_w$ factor. This is incorrect.
> 5. **Averaging:** Always take at least 3 readings and report the **mean value** with the **standard deviation** for full marks.
> 6. **No-Slip Condition:** Must be explicitly mentioned in the derivation to score the boundary condition mark.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision — Memorize These Before the Exam!**

- ✅ **Specific Gravity** = $S = \dfrac{W_3 - W_1}{W_2 - W_1}$ (pycnometer method) — **dimensionless**.
- ✅ **Density** $\rho = S \times 1000$ kg/m³ (at 4°C for water).
- ✅ **Kinematic Viscosity** $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ — units: m²/s.
- ✅ **Dynamic Viscosity** (KTU comparative method): $\mu_1 = \mu_w \times \dfrac{\rho_1 t_1}{\rho_w t_w}$.
- ✅ **Hagen-Poiseuille Equation:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ — remember **R⁴** (radius to the fourth power).
- ✅ **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$.
- ✅ **Water reference values:** $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu_w \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m² (at 20°C).
- ✅ **Viscosity increases with** decrease in temperature (for liquids).
- ✅ **Parabolic velocity profile** in laminar capillary flow.
- ✅ **No-slip condition** is the key boundary condition — $u = 0$ at $r = R$.
- ✅ **Apparatus for viscosity:** Capillary viscometer (lab), Redwood/Saybolt (industrial).
- ✅ **Always state assumptions:** Steady, laminar, incompressible, Newtonian, fully developed flow.
- ✅ **Final result must include units and compare with standard table values** (e.g., glycerine μ ≈ 1.5 N·s/m², water μ ≈ 0.001 N·s/m²).
- ✅ **Viva question:** "Why use a comparative method?" → To eliminate dependence on capillary dimensions (R, L) which are hard to measure accurately.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of total pressure and centre of pressure on plane surface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL407 - FLUID MECHANICS, OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS & HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of total pressure and centre of pressure on plane surface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Aim of the Experiment
To determine the **total hydrostatic thrust (force)** acting on a submerged plane surface and to experimentally locate the **centre of pressure** on that surface, verifying these against theoretical values derived from fluid statics.

### 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Pressure (Total Hydrostatic Thrust, F):**
> It is the resultant of all the hydrostatic pressure forces acting normal to a submerged plane surface. For a plane area `A` submerged in a static liquid of specific weight `w = ρg`, the total pressure is:
>
> $$F = w \bar{h} A$$
>
> where `ħ` is the **depth of centroid (G)** of the plane area measured from the free liquid surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Centre of Pressure (CP):**
> It is the point on the submerged plane surface through which the resultant total pressure force is assumed to act. It always lies **below** the centroid of the area (for a surface having a free liquid surface above it) because pressure increases linearly with depth.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you push a square wooden board completely underwater and hold it parallel to the surface of a swimming pool. The water pushes on the board from every direction. But because the board is not infinitely thin — it has a finite area — the top edge of the board is in **shallower water** (less push), while the bottom edge is in **deeper water** (more push).

Because the bottom gets pushed harder than the top, the *average* push acts **below the geometric middle of the board**. That average point is the **centre of pressure**, and the total integrated push is the **total pressure force**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Insight:**
> - If a plane surface has its top edge exactly at the free surface, the CP lies at `2/3` of the depth (for a rectangle).
> - For a circular disc whose top edge is at the free surface, CP lies at `5/8` of the diameter.
> - CP and Centroid coincide **only** when the surface is at infinite depth (a theoretical impossibility).

### 1.4 Apparatus Used in KTU Labs

The KTU-standard **Hydrostatic Pressure Apparatus** consists of:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Water tank** with transparent side | Holds the static water body |
| **Quadrant / Sector-shaped test plate** | The plane surface whose thrust is measured |
| **Hinged counter-balance arm with weights** | Measures the hydrostatic force |
| **Pointer / Vernier scale** | Locates the centre of pressure along the quadrant arc |
| **Datum level indicator** | Provides reference for depth measurement |
| **Sliding weights on arm** | Balances the hydrostatic thrust |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Derivation Logic (Step-by-Step)

Consider a small elemental strip of area `dA` on an inclined plane surface submerged in liquid.

1. **Pressure at depth `h`:** `p = w h`
2. **Force on elemental strip:** `dF = p \, dA = w h \, dA`
3. **Total hydrostatic thrust:** Integrate over the whole area

$$F = \int_A w h \, dA = w \int_A h \, dA = w \bar{h} \int_A dA$$

4. Since `∫_A h \, dA = ħ A` (by definition of centroid), we obtain:

$$F = w \bar{h} A$$

5. **Location of CP along the inclined plane (slant direction):** Taking moments about the free-surface intersection line:

$$F \cdot \bar{h}^* = \int_A h \cdot (h \, dA) = \int_A h^2 \, dA$$

6. Using the **parallel axis theorem**, `I_O = I_G + A \bar{h}^2`, where `I_O` is the second moment of area about the free surface line and `I_G` about the centroidal axis parallel to free surface:

$$\int_A h^2 dA = I_O = I_G + A \bar{h}^2$$

7. Substituting and solving for `ħ*` (depth of CP):

$$\bar{h}^* = \frac{I_G + A \bar{h}^2}{\bar{h} A} = \frac{I_G}{\bar{h} A} + \bar{h}$$

8. For an **inclined surface** making angle `θ` with the free surface, replacing `I_G` with `I_G sin²θ` (perpendicular distance component):

$$\bar{h}^* = \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{\bar{h} A} + \bar{h}$$

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why does pressure vary linearly?** Because in a static incompressible fluid, `dp/dz = -ρg` (Euler's equation in `z` direction). Integration gives `p = ρgz`.
- **Why is CP always below the centroid?** Because pressure is *greater* at larger depths — the integrated moment shifts downward.
- **Why use `sin²θ`?** Because `I_G` is computed about the centroidal axis *in the plane of the surface*, but the perpendicular distance from the free surface uses the vertical component.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **board-exam essentials** for KTU 2024 Scheme. Master them.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$F = w \bar{h} A$$ | Total hydrostatic thrust | N |
| 2 | $$\bar{h}^* = \frac{I_G}{\bar{h} A} + \bar{h}$$ | Depth of CP (vertical surface) | m |
| 3 | $$\bar{h}^* = \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{\bar{h} A} + \bar{h}$$ | Depth of CP (inclined surface) | m |
| 4 | $$e = \bar{h}^* - \bar{h} = \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{\bar{h} A}$$ | Eccentricity (CP below centroid) | m |
| 5 | $$\frac{I_G}{A} = k^2$$ | Square of radius of gyration | m² |
| 6 | $$\bar{h}^* = \frac{k^2 \sin^2\theta}{\bar{h}} + \bar{h}$$ | CP depth using radius of gyration | m |
| 7 | $$p = w h$$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth `h` | Pa (N/m²) |

### 2.4 Standard `I_G` Values for Common Shapes

| Shape | Area `A` | `I_G` (about centroidal axis) | `k² = I_G/A` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangle (b × d) | `bd` | `bd³/12` | `d²/12` |
| Circle (diameter D) | `πD²/4` | `πD⁴/64` | `D²/16` |
| Triangle (base b, height h) | `bh/2` | `bh³/36` | `h²/18` |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dam design**: Gravity dams, buttress dams, and arch dams must resist hydrostatic thrust from reservoirs. The line of action of CP determines overturning stability.
- **Sluice gates & lock gates**: CP location dictates hinge placement to minimize opening torque.
- **Ship hulls**: Pressure distribution on flat hull plates is used to compute bending moments.
- **Submarine windows**: Designed using total pressure and CP analysis.
- **Storage tanks**: Side walls of petroleum and water tanks must withstand the lateral hydrostatic thrust.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Total Pressure and Centre of Pressure

**Setup:** Consider a plane surface of arbitrary shape, inclined at angle `θ` to the horizontal free liquid surface. Let `OX` be the line where the plane of the surface intersects the free liquid surface.

```
                  Free Liquid Surface
   ────────────────●─────────────●───────────── 
                  /             /
                 /             /
                /             /
               /  dF = p·dA   /   
              /             /    P = Total Pressure
             /   h         /     acting at CP
            /             /
           /             /  θ = angle of inclination
          ●─────────────●
          O            Element strip
                       at depth h
```

**Step 1:** Consider an elemental strip of area `dA` at perpendicular depth `h` from the free surface.

The pressure intensity at this strip is:

$$p = w h$$

where `w = ρg` is the specific weight of the liquid (for water, `w = 9810 N/m³`).

**Step 2:** The force on this elemental strip is:

$$dF = p \cdot dA = w h \, dA$$

**Step 3:** Integrating over the entire submerged area `A`:

$$F = \int_A w h \, dA$$

Since `w` is constant for an incompressible homogeneous liquid:

$$F = w \int_A h \, dA$$

**Step 4:** By definition of the centroid `G` of area `A`:

$$\int_A h \, dA = \bar{h} \cdot A$$

where `ħ` is the depth of centroid `G` from the free liquid surface.

**Therefore:**

$$\boxed{F = w \bar{h} A}$$

**Step 5:** To locate the centre of pressure `CP`, take moments of all elemental forces about the intersection line `OX` (line of the free surface on the plane).

$$\text{Moment about OX} = F \cdot \bar{h}^* = \int_A h \cdot (w h \, dA) = w \int_A h^2 \, dA$$

where `ħ*` is the perpendicular depth of CP from the free surface.

**Step 6:** The integral `∫_A h² dA` is the **second moment of area** about the line `OX`, denoted `I_O`.

$$F \cdot \bar{h}^* = w \cdot I_O$$

$$\bar{h}^* = \frac{w \cdot I_O}{F} = \frac{w \cdot I_O}{w \bar{h} A} = \frac{I_O}{\bar{h} A}$$

**Step 7:** Apply the **Parallel Axis Theorem** (also called the Theorem of Perpendicular Axes):

$$I_O = I_G + A \bar{h}^2$$

where `I_G` is the second moment of area about an axis through the centroid `G` parallel to `OX`.

**Step 8:** Substituting:

$$\bar{h}^* = \frac{I_G + A \bar{h}^2}{\bar{h} A} = \frac{I_G}{\bar{h} A} + \frac{A \bar{h}^2}{\bar{h} A} = \frac{I_G}{\bar{h} A} + \bar{h}$$

**Therefore:**

$$\boxed{\bar{h}^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G}{\bar{h} A} = \bar{h} + \frac{k^2}{\bar{h}}}$$

where `k² = I_G / A` is the **square of the radius of gyration** about the centroidal axis.

**Step 9:** For an **inclined plane surface** making angle `θ` with the horizontal free surface, the perpendicular distance from the free surface to any point on the surface is `h = y sinθ`, where `y` is the slant distance. Substituting this into the parallel axis theorem yields the general formula:

$$\bar{h}^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{\bar{h} A}$$

**Step 10:** The **eccentricity** `e` (vertical distance from centroid `G` to centre of pressure `CP` along the inclined plane) is:

$$e = \bar{h}^* - \bar{h} = \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{\bar{h} A} = \frac{k^2 \sin^2\theta}{\bar{h}}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A circular gate of diameter **2.5 m** is installed vertically in a tank. The centroid of the gate is located at a depth of **6.0 m** below the free water surface. Determine:
1. Total hydrostatic thrust on the gate.
2. Depth of centre of pressure below the free surface.
3. Position of centre of pressure relative to the centroid of the gate.

**Given:**
- Diameter `D = 2.5 m`
- Depth of centroid `ħ = 6.0 m`
- Specific weight of water `w = 9810 N/m³`
- Vertical surface, so `θ = 90°`, hence `sin²θ = 1`

**Solution:**

**(a) Total Hydrostatic Thrust:**

Area of gate:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (2.5)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 6.25}{4} = 4.9087 \text{ m}^2$$

Total thrust:

$$F = w \bar{h} A = 9810 \times 6.0 \times 4.9087$$

$$F = 288,901.6 \text{ N} \approx 288.9 \text{ kN}$$

**(b) Depth of Centre of Pressure:**

Second moment of area of a circle about its centroid:

$$I_G = \frac{\pi D^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times (2.5)^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 39.0625}{64} = 1.9175 \text{ m}^4$$

Depth of CP:

$$\bar{h}^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G}{\bar{h} A} = 6.0 + \frac{1.9175}{6.0 \times 4.9087}$$

$$\bar{h}^* = 6.0 + \frac{1.9175}{29.4522} = 6.0 + 0.0651 = 6.0651 \text{ m}$$

**(c) Eccentricity (CP relative to centroid):**

$$e = \bar{h}^* - \bar{h} = 6.0651 - 6.0 = 0.0651 \text{ m} = 65.1 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The CP lies only **6.51 cm** below the centroid because the gate is at a considerable depth (6 m). As depth increases, CP → centroid. This is a common KTU conceptual question.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Force Analysis Diagram (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Free Liquid Surface"]:::surface
    node2["Pressure p1 = w*h1 (smaller)"]:::lowP
    node3["Pressure distribution: linear increase with depth"]:::dist
    node4["Pressure p2 = w*h2 (larger)"]:::highP
    node5["Elemental Strip dA"]:::strip
    node6["Centroid G at depth h-bar"]:::centroid
    node7["Centre of Pressure CP at depth h-star (below G)"]:::cp
    node8["Total Thrust F = w * h-bar * A"]:::force

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8
    node5 -.Force dF on strip.-> node8

    classDef surface fill:#A8D5BA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lowP fill:#FFF1B0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef dist fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef highP fill:#FF8A80,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef strip fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef centroid fill:#FFE082,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cp fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef force fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Experimental Setup Block Diagram

```mermaid

    nodeB["Quadrant Test Plate (submerged)"]:::plate
    nodeC["Hinged Pivot Point at top of quadrant"]:::pivot
    nodeD["Counter-Balance Lever Arm"]:::arm
    nodeE["Sliding Weight on Arm"]:::weight
    nodeF["Vernier Scale / Pointer (locates CP)"]:::vernier
    nodeG["Datum Pointer at Free Surface"]:::datum

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB -.pivots about.-> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeD -.rotates to.-> nodeF
    nodeB -.aligned with.-> nodeG

    classDef tank fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef plate fill:#FFCC80,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    classDef arm fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef weight fill:#EF5350,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef vernier fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```
