
### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["Stage 1: Fill Tank with Water to Marked Level"]
    s2["Stage 2: Set Test Plate at Known Depth"]
    s3["Stage 3: Balance Lever Arm without Water (zero error)"]
    s4["Stage 4: Allow Water to Act on Plate"]
    s5["Stage 5: Add Weights to Lever Arm until Re-Balanced"]
    s6["Stage 6: Record Balancing Weight = Total Thrust F"]
    s7["Stage 7: Move Datum Pointer to Locate CP"]
    s8["Stage 8: Calculate Theoretical F and h-star"]
    s9["Stage 9: Compare Experimental vs Theoretical"]

    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> s3
    s3 --> s4
    s4 --> s5
    s5 --> s6
    s6 --> s7
    s7 --> s8
    s8 --> s9
```

### 4.4 Observation Table Template (Standard KTU Lab Format)

| Sl. No. | Depth of Centroid `ħ` (m) | Area of Plate `A` (m²) | Theoretical Thrust `F_th = wħA` (N) | Experimental Weight `F_exp` (N) | % Error |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.05 | 0.01 | 4.905 | 4.80 | 2.14 |
| 2 | 0.10 | 0.01 | 9.810 | 9.65 | 1.63 |
| 3 | 0.15 | 0.01 | 14.715 | 14.55 | 1.12 |

| Sl. No. | `I_G` (m⁴) | Theoretical `ħ*` (m) | Experimental `ħ*` (m) | % Error |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.0 × 10⁻⁶ | 0.067 | 0.070 | 4.48 |
| 2 | 1.0 × 10⁻⁶ | 0.133 | 0.130 | 2.26 |
| 3 | 1.0 × 10⁻⁶ | 0.200 | 0.196 | 2.00 |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Define total pressure and centre of pressure on a submerged plane surface. State the formulae for both.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Total Pressure (F):** It is the resultant of all the hydrostatic pressure forces acting normally on a submerged plane surface.

$$F = w \bar{h} A$$

where `w = ρg` = specific weight of liquid, `ħ` = depth of centroid of the area from the free liquid surface, `A` = area of the submerged plane surface.

**Centre of Pressure (CP):** It is the point on the submerged plane surface through which the resultant total pressure is assumed to act.

$$\bar{h}^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{\bar{h} A}$$

CP always lies **below** the centroid `G` of the plane area (when free liquid surface is above). *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for each formula = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Why does the centre of pressure for a submerged plane surface always lie below the centroid?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The hydrostatic pressure at any point on a submerged plane surface is given by `p = wh`, where `h` is the vertical depth below the free liquid surface. Since pressure increases **linearly** with depth, the lower portions of the submerged surface experience **greater** pressure than the upper portions.

Because the pressure distribution is not uniform but increases from top to bottom, the resultant of the distributed pressure forces acts at a point **lower** than the geometric centroid of the area. Hence, the centre of pressure always lies below the centroid.

As the depth of submersion increases infinitely, the relative variation in pressure across the surface diminishes, and CP approaches the centroid. *(3 marks for complete reasoning)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** A rectangular gate **2 m wide and 3 m deep** is installed in a vertical wall of a tank, with its top edge **4 m below the free water surface**. Determine:
1. The total hydrostatic thrust on the gate.
2. The depth of centre of pressure from the free surface.
3. The position of centre of pressure relative to the centroid of the gate.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An inclined plane surface, **1.5 m wide and 2.5 m long**, is immersed in water at an angle of **30°** to the horizontal. The top edge of the surface is **1.0 m below** the free water surface. Find the total pressure and centre of pressure.

**(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a):**

Given:
- Width `b = 2 m`, Depth `d = 3 m`, vertical wall
- Top of gate at 4 m below free surface
- Specific weight `w = 9810 N/m³`

**Depth of centroid:**

$$\bar{h} = 4 + \frac{d}{2} = 4 + \frac{3}{2} = 5.5 \text{ m}$$

**Area:**

$$A = b \times d = 2 \times 3 = 6 \text{ m}^2$$

**(1) Total Hydrostatic Thrust:**

$$F = w \bar{h} A = 9810 \times 5.5 \times 6 = 323{,}730 \text{ N} \approx 323.73 \text{ kN}$$

**(2) Depth of Centre of Pressure:**

$$I_G = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{2 \times (3)^3}{12} = \frac{54}{12} = 4.5 \text{ m}^4$$

$$\bar{h}^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G}{\bar{h} A} = 5.5 + \frac{4.5}{5.5 \times 6} = 5.5 + 0.1364 = 5.6364 \text{ m}$$

**(3) Position of CP relative to Centroid:**

$$e = \bar{h}^* - \bar{h} = 5.6364 - 5.5 = 0.1364 \text{ m} = 13.64 \text{ cm (below centroid)}$$

**Part (b):**

Given:
- `b = 1.5 m`, slant length `L = 2.5 m`, `θ = 30°`
- Top edge at `h_top = 1.0 m` below free surface

**Depth of centroid (vertical depth):**

$$\bar{h} = h_{top} + \frac{L \sin\theta}{2} = 1.0 + \frac{2.5 \times \sin 30°}{2} = 1.0 + 0.625 = 1.625 \text{ m}$$

**Area:**

$$A = b \times L = 1.5 \times 2.5 = 3.75 \text{ m}^2$$

**(1) Total Pressure:**

$$F = w \bar{h} A = 9810 \times 1.625 \times 3.75 = 59{,}784.4 \text{ N} \approx 59.78 \text{ kN}$$

**(2) Centre of Pressure:**

$$I_G = \frac{b L^3}{12} = \frac{1.5 \times (2.5)^3}{12} = \frac{23.4375}{12} = 1.9531 \text{ m}^4$$

$$\sin^2\theta = \sin^2 30° = 0.25$$

$$\bar{h}^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{\bar{h} A} = 1.625 + \frac{1.9531 \times 0.25}{1.625 \times 3.75}$$

$$\bar{h}^* = 1.625 + \frac{0.4883}{6.0938} = 1.625 + 0.0801 = 1.7051 \text{ m}$$

$$\text{Distance along the slant from free surface} = \frac{\bar{h}^*}{\sin\theta} = \frac{1.7051}{0.5} = 3.4103 \text{ m}$$

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Derive expressions for the **total hydrostatic thrust** on a submerged plane surface and the **depth of centre of pressure** using the parallel axis theorem. Explain why CP always lies below the centroid.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A circular plate of diameter **1.5 m** is placed vertically in water with its upper edge at the free water surface. Compute the total pressure acting on the plate and the depth of centre of pressure. Take `w = 9810 N/m³`.

**(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a):** *(Refer to Section 3 of these notes for the complete derivation.)*

The complete derivation is as shown:

- Start with elemental strip `dA` at depth `h`.
- Pressure `p = wh`.
- Total thrust: `F = ∫_A w h dA = w ħ A`.
- Moment about free surface: `F ħ* = ∫_A h² dA · w = w I_O`.
- By parallel axis theorem: `I_O = I_G + A ħ²`.
- Therefore: `ħ* = (I_G + A ħ²)/(ħ A) = ħ + I_G/(ħ A)`.
- For inclined surface: replace `I_G` by `I_G sin²θ`.

**CP lies below centroid** because pressure increases linearly with depth, giving greater weight to deeper portions during integration of moment. The term `I_G/(ħ A)` is always positive, so `ħ* > ħ`.

**Part (b):**

Given: Circular plate, `D = 1.5 m`, vertical, upper edge at free surface.

- Depth of centroid: `ħ = D/2 = 0.75 m`
- Area: `A = π D²/4 = π × 2.25 / 4 = 1.7671 m²`

**Total Pressure:**

$$F = w \bar{h} A = 9810 \times 0.75 \times 1.7671 = 12{,}997.6 \text{ N} \approx 13.0 \text{ kN}$$

**Centre of Pressure:**

$$I_G = \frac{\pi D^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 5.0625}{64} = 0.2485 \text{ m}^4$$

$$\bar{h}^* = \bar{h} + \frac{I_G}{\bar{h} A} = 0.75 + \frac{0.2485}{0.75 \times 1.7671} = 0.75 + 0.1875 = 0.9375 \text{ m}$$

**Position of CP relative to centroid:**

$$e = \bar{h}^* - \bar{h} = 0.9375 - 0.75 = 0.1875 \text{ m} = \frac{D}{8} = \frac{1.5}{8}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Result:** For a circular disc with its top edge at the free water surface, CP is at depth `5D/8 = 0.9375 m` (which is `D/8` below the centroid). This is a frequently tested KTU result.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert slant length to vertical depth**: When an inclined surface is given, students often use the slant length as `ħ` directly. Always compute `ħ = h_top + (L sinθ)/2`.
>
> 2. **Mixing up `sinθ` and `sin²θ`**: For inclined surfaces, the formula uses `sin²θ`, **not** `sinθ`. This is the single most common error in KTU answer sheets.
>
> 3. **Using wrong `I_G`**: For rectangles, `I_G = bd³/12` (use the dimension along the depth, not the width).
>
> 4. **Units inconsistency**: Always use SI units throughout. Mixing `cm` and `m` is a guaranteed mark-loss.
>
> 5. **Not stating the position of CP clearly**: Examiners expect both the **depth from the free surface** AND the **distance from the centroid** (eccentricity `e`). Giving only one loses 2–3 marks.
>
> 6. **Skipping the derivation explanation**: In 14-mark questions, merely writing the formula without showing the parallel axis theorem step loses 4–5 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**
>
> - ✅ **Total Pressure:** `F = w ħ A` — depends only on the depth of **centroid**, not the shape.
> - ✅ **Centre of Pressure:** `ħ* = ħ + I_G sin²θ/(ħ A)` — depends on shape (`I_G`) AND inclination (`θ`).
> - ✅ **CP lies below centroid** when free liquid surface exists above the plane.
> - ✅ **CP coincides with centroid** only when `I_G = 0` (point area) or at infinite depth.
> - ✅ **Eccentricity** `e = ħ* - ħ = I_G sin²θ/(ħ A) = k² sin²θ/ħ`.
> - ✅ For a **rectangle with top at free surface**: CP is at `2d/3` from free surface.
> - ✅ For a **circle with top at free surface**: CP is at `5D/8` from free surface.
> - ✅ For a **triangle with vertex at free surface**: CP is at `3h/4` from free surface.
> - ✅ For a **triangle with base at free surface**: CP is at `3h/8` from free surface.
> - ✅ **Radius of gyration**: `k² = I_G/A` — geometry-only parameter.
> - ✅ The **parallel axis theorem** `I_O = I_G + A ħ²` is the cornerstone of CP derivation.
> - ✅ For **vertical surfaces**, `θ = 90°`, so `sin²θ = 1`, simplifying the formula.
> - ✅ Standard **specific weight of water**: `w = 9810 N/m³` (or `9.81 kN/m³`).
> - ✅ Experimental verification uses **hydrostatic pressure apparatus** with quadrant test plate.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of metacentric height of a floating body
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL407 - FLUID MECHANICS, OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS & HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of metacentric height of a floating body

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module Experiment 2: Determination of Metacentric Height of a Floating Body

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **metacentric height (GM)** is a fundamental parameter in **fluid statics and naval architecture** that quantifies the **initial static stability** of a floating or submerged body. It is defined as the distance between the **center of gravity (G)** of the body and the **metacenter (M)** of the body.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definitions for KTU Board Exam:**
> - **Center of Gravity (G):** The point through which the total weight of the body is assumed to act vertically downward.
> - **Center of Buoyancy (B):** The centroid of the volume of fluid displaced by the floating body. It is the point through which the total buoyant force acts vertically upward.
> - **Metacenter (M):** The point at which the **vertical line** passing through the new (tilted) center of buoyancy intersects the **original vertical line** passing through the initial center of buoyancy.
> - **Metacentric Height (GM):** The distance between the center of gravity (G) and the metacenter (M). Mathematically, $GM = BM \pm BG$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on the edge of a small boat:

- If the boat is **very wide and flat** (like a raft), even when you shift your weight, the boat tilts only slightly and self-corrects. This is a **stable** floating body.
- If the boat is **very narrow and tall** (like a canoe), it can easily tip over when you shift your weight. This is an **unstable** floating body.

The **metacentric height (GM)** is the scientific measure of this "self-righting" tendency. A **larger GM** means the body has a strong tendency to return to its upright position (highly stable), while a **smaller GM** (or negative GM) means the body is prone to capsizing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Significance:** Ship designers carefully calculate the metacentric height. Passenger ships require a **moderately high GM** for comfort (not too rolly), while warships need a **high GM** for stability in rough seas. Cargo ships often have a **low GM** to prevent excessive rolling during voyages.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Density of fresh water:** $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 1 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- **Density of sea water:** $\rho_{sw} \approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Specific weight of water:** $\gamma_w = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$ (or $9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$)
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Standard tilt angle for stability test:** $\theta \leq 15°$ (small angle approximation)

### 1.4 Stability Criteria Based on Metacentric Height

The KTU board examination frequently tests the conditions of equilibrium:

| Condition | Position of M relative to G | Stability Status |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $GM > 0$ | M is **above** G | **Stable** Equilibrium |
| $GM = 0$ | M **coincides** with G | **Neutral** Equilibrium |
| $GM < 0$ | M is **below** G | **Unstable** Equilibrium |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background: Why Does a Floating Body Tilt?

When a floating body tilts by a **small angle $\theta$** about a longitudinal axis due to an external disturbance (e.g., shifting a weight):

1. The **center of gravity (G)** remains in the same position relative to the body (it moves with the body).
2. The **center of buoyancy (B)** shifts to a new position $B'$ because the shape of the submerged volume changes.
3. The buoyant force now acts vertically upward through $B'$.
4. The weight of the body acts vertically downward through G.
5. The two equal and opposite forces (Weight $W$ at G, Buoyancy $F_B$ at $B'$) form a **restoring couple** that tries to bring the body back to its original position.

The **metacenter (M)** is geometrically defined as the intersection of:
- The **new vertical line** through $B'$ (the line of action of buoyant force), and
- The **original vertical line** through B (the initial axis of symmetry).

## 2.2 The Core 'Why' and 'How' Behind Metacentric Height

**Why does GM determine stability?**

- If M lies **above G** ($GM > 0$): The couple formed by Weight and Buoyancy is a **restoring couple**. The body returns to its original position. → **STABLE**
- If M lies **below G** ($GM < 0$): The couple becomes an **overturning couple**. The body capsizes. → **UNSTABLE**
- If M **coincides with G** ($GM = 0$): The couple is **zero**. The body remains in the tilted position. → **NEUTRAL**

**How is GM measured experimentally?**

The classical experimental method uses a **rectangular floating body (pontoon)** with a **movable jockey weight** on top. By shifting the jockey weight by a known distance, the body tilts, and the angle of tilt is measured using a **plumb line** or **inclinometer**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes **all key formulas** for the KTU board examination. Note: The absolute value symbol is rendered as `\vert` for table compatibility.

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $BM = \dfrac{I}{V}$ | Distance between B and M (Metacentric Radius) | metres (m) |
| 2 | $I = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$ | Moment of inertia of waterline area (rectangular body) | $\text{m}^4$ |
| 3 | $I = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^4}{64}$ | Moment of inertia of waterline area (circular body) | $\text{m}^4$ |
| 4 | $V = A_w \cdot h$ | Volume of fluid displaced (submerged volume) | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 5 | $GM = BM - BG$ | Metacentric height (when G is above B) | metres (m) |
| 6 | $GM = BM + GB$ | Metacentric height (when G is below B) | metres (m) |
| 7 | $GM = \dfrac{w \cdot a}{W \cdot \tan\theta}$ | GM by experimental method (jockey weight shifted) | metres (m) |
| 8 | $GM = \dfrac{w \cdot a \cdot L}{W \cdot x}$ | GM using plumb line method ($x$ = bob shift, $L$ = bob length) | metres (m) |
| 9 | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{x}{L}$ | Angle of tilt from plumb line | radians (approx.) |
| 10 | $W = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V$ | Total weight of floating body = weight of displaced water | Newtons (N) |

**Where:**
- $I$ = Second moment of area of the waterline (plan) area about the longitudinal axis
- $V$ = Volume of the submerged portion of the body
- $L, B$ = Length and breadth of the rectangular waterline area
- $D$ = Diameter of the circular waterline area
- $A_w$ = Waterline (plan) area; $h$ = depth of immersion (draft)
- $w$ = Weight of the movable jockey
- $a$ = Distance through which the jockey is shifted from the center
- $W$ = Total weight of the floating body
- $\theta$ = Angle of tilt (in degrees, then converted to tan)
- $x$ = Horizontal shift of plumb bob from the original vertical
- $L$ = Length of plumb bob suspension

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Tip:** Always remember that the **small angle approximation** ($\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta$ in radians) is valid only when $\theta \leq 15°$. For larger angles, the formula is no longer accurate, and a moment-based stability analysis must be performed.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Naval Architecture:** Critical in designing ships, submarines, and offshore platforms. Determines the righting moment under wave action.
2. **Dam and Gate Design:** Floating gates and caissons must have adequate GM to remain stable.
3. **Buoy Design:** Navigation buoys and floating platforms require precise GM calculations.
4. **Aircraft and Submarine Stability:** Especially for seaplanes and submarines during surfacing/diving maneuvers.
5. **Civil Engineering:** Floating bridges, pontoon bridges, and floating dry docks depend on metacentric height analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Expression for Metacentric Radius (BM)

**Consider a floating body** initially floating in equilibrium. The center of buoyancy is at B and the weight W acts at G.

**Step 1: Tilt the body by a small angle $\theta$**

The body is tilted by a small angle $\theta$ about a longitudinal axis O-O. The new center of buoyancy is $B'$. 

**Step 2: Apply the principle of constancy of buoyancy**

By **Archimedes' principle**, the weight of fluid displaced remains equal to the weight of the body. Hence, the **volume of fluid displaced remains constant** before and after tilting. This is a crucial KTU concept.

**Step 3: Identify the wedge volumes**

When the body tilts:
- A **wedge of volume $v_1$** is submerged on one side.
- A **wedge of volume $v_2$** emerges on the other side.
- Since total displaced volume is constant, $v_1 = v_2 = v$.

**Step 4: Compute the shift of the center of buoyancy**

The shift of the center of buoyancy from B to $B'$ is given by:

$$
BB' = \frac{I \cdot \sin\theta}{V}
$$

**Step 5: Relate BB' to BM**

From the geometry of the metacenter:

$$
\sin\theta = \frac{BB'}{BM}
$$

**Step 6: Solve for BM**

Substituting the value of $BB'$:

$$
BM = \frac{BB'}{\sin\theta} = \frac{I \cdot \sin\theta}{V \cdot \sin\theta}
$$

$$
\boxed{BM = \frac{I}{V}}
$$

**Physical meaning:** The metacentric radius $BM$ is the **ratio of the second moment of area of the waterline plane to the submerged volume**. This is one of the most frequently tested KTU derivations.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Metacentric Height from Experimental Method

**Consider a floating body** of total weight $W$ with a movable jockey weight $w$ placed on top.

**Step 1: Initial equilibrium (jockey at center)**

Initially, the jockey is at the center of the body. The center of gravity G of the **entire system** (body + jockey) lies on the central vertical axis. The body floats upright.

**Step 2: Shift the jockey by distance $a$**

The jockey weight $w$ is moved horizontally by a known distance $a$ from the center. This creates an **unbalanced moment** about the original center of buoyancy.

**Step 3: The body tilts by angle $\theta$**

The body rotates by a small angle $\theta$ until equilibrium is re-established. In the new equilibrium position, the **center of gravity G' of the system** lies on the same vertical line as the **new center of buoyancy $B'$**.

**Step 4: Apply the moment equilibrium equation**

The moment of the shifted jockey about the new center of buoyancy must equal zero (equilibrium condition). Equating the overturning moment to the righting moment:

$$
w \cdot a = W \cdot GM \cdot \tan\theta
$$

**Step 5: Solve for GM**

$$
GM = \frac{w \cdot a}{W \cdot \tan\theta}
$$

$$
\boxed{GM = \frac{w \cdot a}{W \cdot \tan\theta}}
$$

**Where:**
- $w$ = weight of the movable jockey (N)
- $a$ = horizontal distance the jockey is shifted from the center (m)
- $W$ = total weight of the floating body (N)
- $\theta$ = angle of tilt (measured from plumb line or inclinometer)

## 3.3 Derivation 3: GM Using Plumb Line Method

In the experimental setup, a **plumb line** is suspended from a fixed point on the floating body. As the body tilts by $\theta$, the plumb line (which always remains vertical) shifts horizontally by a distance $x$ from the original vertical reference.

**Step 1: Geometric relation for tilt angle**

If $L$ is the length of the plumb bob from its suspension point:

$$
\tan\theta = \frac{x}{L}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the experimental GM equation**

$$
GM = \frac{w \cdot a}{W \cdot \tan\theta} = \frac{w \cdot a}{W \cdot \left(\dfrac{x}{L}\right)}
$$

$$
\boxed{GM = \frac{w \cdot a \cdot L}{W \cdot x}}
$$

This is the **final experimental formula** used in the KTU laboratory examination for Module Experiment 2.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Examples

### **Numerical Example 1: GM from Direct Experimental Data**

**Given:**
- Weight of floating body, $W = 250 \text{ N}$
- Weight of jockey, $w = 30 \text{ N}$
- Distance of jockey shift, $a = 1.2 \text{ m}$
- Angle of tilt, $\theta = 5°$

**Find:** Metacentric height (GM)

**Solution:**

Step 1: Calculate $\tan 5°$

$$
\tan 5° = 0.0875
$$

Step 2: Apply the experimental formula

$$
GM = \frac{w \cdot a}{W \cdot \tan\theta}
$$

$$
GM = \frac{30 \times 1.2}{250 \times 0.0875}
$$

$$
GM = \frac{36}{21.875}
$$

$$
\boxed{GM = 1.646 \text{ m}}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $GM > 0$, the floating body is **stable** in its equilibrium position.

---

### **Numerical Example 2: GM of a Rectangular Pontoon**

**Given:**
- Length of pontoon, $L = 6 \text{ m}$
- Breadth of pontoon, $B = 3 \text{ m}$
- Depth of pontoon, $D = 1.5 \text{ m}$
- Draft (depth of immersion), $h = 0.75 \text{ m}$
- Density of water, $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Find:** Metacentric height (GM)

**Solution:**

Step 1: Calculate the waterline (plan) area

$$
A_w = L \times B = 6 \times 3 = 18 \text{ m}^2
$$

Step 2: Calculate the second moment of area about the longitudinal axis

$$
I = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{6 \times 3^3}{12} = \frac{6 \times 27}{12} = 13.5 \text{ m}^4
$$

Step 3: Calculate the submerged volume

$$
V = A_w \times h = 18 \times 0.75 = 13.5 \text{ m}^3
$$

Step 4: Calculate the metacentric radius

$$
BM = \frac{I}{V} = \frac{13.5}{13.5} = 1.0 \text{ m}
$$

Step 5: Calculate BG

For a uniform rectangular pontoon:
- G is at the **geometric center** of the body, i.e., at depth $D/2 = 1.5/2 = 0.75 \text{ m}$ from the bottom.
- B is at the **centroid of the submerged volume**, i.e., at depth $h/2 = 0.75/2 = 0.375 \text{ m}$ from the bottom.

$$
BG = \frac{D}{2} - \frac{h}{2} = 0.75 - 0.375 = 0.375 \text{ m}
$$

Step 6: Calculate GM

$$
GM = BM - BG = 1.0 - 0.375
$$

$$
\boxed{GM = 0.625 \text{ m}}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $GM = 0.625 \text{ m} > 0$, the pontoon is **stable** when floating at the given draft.

---

### **Numerical Example 3: GM Using Plumb Line Method**

**Given:**
- Weight of floating body, $W = 400 \text{ N}$
- Weight of jockey, $w = 50 \text{ N}$
- Distance of jockey shift, $a = 0.8 \text{ m}$
- Length of plumb bob, $L = 0.5 \text{ m}$
- Horizontal shift of plumb bob, $x = 0.04 \text{ m}$

**Find:** Metacentric height (GM)

**Solution:**

Step 1: Apply the plumb line formula

$$
GM = \frac{w \cdot a \cdot L}{W \cdot x}
$$

Step 2: Substitute the values

$$
GM = \frac{50 \times 0.8 \times 0.5}{400 \times 0.04}
$$

$$
GM = \frac{20}{16}
$$

$$
\boxed{GM = 1.25 \text{ m}}
$$

**Conclusion:** The floating body is **stable** with a metacentric height of 1.25 m.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Geometric Diagram: The Metacenter Concept

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **geometric relationship** between the center of buoyancy (B), the center of gravity (G), and the metacenter (M) when a floating body tilts.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Floating Body in Equilibrium]) --> A[Initial State: Body is Upright]
    A --> B["Center of Buoyancy B is on the Central Vertical Axis"]
    B --> C["Weight W acts downward at G"]
    C --> D["External Force Causes Tilt by Angle theta"]
    D --> E["New Center of Buoyancy B prime is Formed"]
    E --> F["Vertical Line through B prime is Drawn Upward"]
    F --> G["It Intersects the Original Vertical Line at Point M"]
    G --> H["Point M is the METACENTER"]
    H --> I["Distance GM is the METACENTRIC HEIGHT"]
    I --> J{Check Stability}
    J -->|GM greater than 0| K[STABLE Body Returns to Upright]
    J -->|GM equal to 0| L[NEUTRAL Body Remains Tilted]
    J -->|GM less than 0| M[UNSTABLE Body Capsizes]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Experimental Setup

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **complete experimental apparatus** used in the KTU laboratory for Module Experiment 2.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Water Tank with Water] --> B[Floating Rectangular Body Pontoon]
    B --> C[Movable Jockey Weight on Top Deck]
    B --> D[Plumb Line Suspension from Top]
    D --> E[Plumb Bob Hanging Vertically]
    B --> F[Scale Ruler for Measuring Draft]
    C --> G[Scale for Jockey Position]
    H[External Weight Box for Calibration] --> B
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#ffe1e1
    style D fill:#e1ffe1
    style E fill:#e1ffe1
    style F fill:#f0e1ff
    style G fill:#f0e1ff
    style H fill:#ffe1f0
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following diagram represents the **functional flow of the experimental procedure** step by step.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Clean the water tank and fill with fresh water] --> P2[Step 2: Weigh the empty floating body on a weighing scale]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Float the body in water and allow it to settle]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Place the jockey weight at the center position]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Note the plumb bob reference position on the scale]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Shift the jockey by a known distance a to one side]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Wait for the body to settle in tilted position]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Measure the horizontal shift x of the plumb bob]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Measure the length L of the plumb bob]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Calculate GM using the formula]
    P10 --> P11[Step 11: Repeat with different jockey distances]
    P11 --> P12[Step 12: Tabulate results and find the average GM]
    style P1 fill:#cce5ff
    style P12 fill:#ccffcc
```

## 4.4 Force Diagram: Stability Conditions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph StableCase[STABLE CASE GM greater than 0]
        SA[Weight W at G acts Downward] --> SB[Buoyancy Fb at B prime acts Upward]
        SB --> SC[Restoring Couple is Formed]
        SC --> SD[Body Returns to Upright Position]
    end
    subgraph UnstableCase[UNSTABLE CASE GM less than 0]
        UA[Weight W at G acts Downward] --> UB[Buoyancy Fb at B prime acts Upward]
        UB --> UC[Overturning Couple is Formed]
        UC --> UD[Body Capsizes]
    end
    style StableCase fill:#e6ffe6
    style UnstableCase fill:#ffe6e6
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Metacenter and Metacentric Height** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **metacenter (M)** of a floating body is defined as the point at which the **vertical line** passing through the new (tilted) center of buoyancy intersects the **original vertical line** passing through the initial center of buoyancy.

The **metacentric height (GM)** is defined as the distance between the **center of gravity (G)** of the floating body and the **metacenter (M)**. It is a measure of the initial static stability of the floating body.

Mathematically, $GM = BM \pm BG$, where $BM = I/V$ is the metacentric radius and $BG$ is the distance between the center of gravity and the center of buoyancy.

---

### **Question 2: What is the Condition of Stability of a Floating Body?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A floating body is in **stable equilibrium** when the metacenter (M) lies **above** the center of gravity (G), i.e., when the metacentric height is **positive** ($GM > 0$).

The three conditions of equilibrium for a floating body are:
1. **Stable Equilibrium:** $GM > 0$ (M is above G). The body, if tilted, returns to its original upright position.
2. **Neutral Equilibrium:** $GM = 0$ (M coincides with G). The body remains in any tilted position without any restoring or overturning couple.
3. **Unstable Equilibrium:** $GM < 0$ (M is below G). The body capsizes and does not return to its original position.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the metacentric height of a floating body. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Initial State**

Consider a floating body in equilibrium. The center of buoyancy is at B, and the weight W of the body acts at the center of gravity G.

**Step 2: Tilting the Body**

The body is tilted by a small angle $\theta$ about a longitudinal axis. The new center of buoyancy is at $B'$.

**Step 3: Constancy of Displaced Volume**

By Archimedes' principle, the volume of fluid displaced before and after tilting must remain constant. Hence, the wedge submerged on one side ($v_1$) equals the wedge emerged on the other side ($v_2$), so $v_1 = v_2 = v$.

**Step 4: Shift of Center of Buoyancy**

The horizontal shift of the center of buoyancy is given by:

$$
BB' = \frac{I \cdot \sin\theta}{V}
$$

**Step 5: Geometric Relation**

From the geometry of the metacenter:

$$
BM = \frac{BB'}{\sin\theta} = \frac{I}{V}
$$

**Step 6: Final Expression for Metacentric Height**

$$
GM = BM - BG = \frac{I}{V} - BG
$$

$$
\boxed{GM = \frac{I}{V} - BG}
$$

---

**(b) In a metacentric height experiment on a ship model, a weight of 60 N is shifted through a distance of 1.0 m across the deck. The total weight of the model is 300 N. The angle of tilt is observed to be 6°. Determine the metacentric height. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Weight of jockey, $w = 60 \text{ N}$
- Distance shifted, $a = 1.0 \text{ m}$
- Weight of model, $W = 300 \text{ N}$
- Angle of tilt, $\theta = 6°$

**Step 1: Calculate $\tan 6°$**

$$
\tan 6° = 0.1051
$$

**Step 2: Apply the experimental formula**

$$
GM = \frac{w \cdot a}{W \cdot \tan\theta}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the values**

$$
GM = \frac{60 \times 1.0}{300 \times 0.1051}
$$

$$
GM = \frac{60}{31.53}
$$

$$
\boxed{GM = 1.903 \text{ m}}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $GM > 0$, the ship model is **stable** in its equilibrium position with a metacentric height of approximately **1.903 m**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the experimental method to determine the metacentric height of a floating body with a neat sketch. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Apparatus Required:**
1. A rectangular floating body (pontoon) made of wood or metal
2. A movable jockey weight (typically 20-50 N)
3. A water tank
4. A plumb line suspension with a plumb bob
5. A graduated scale to measure the shift of the plumb bob
6. A weighing balance to measure the weight of the body

**Experimental Procedure:**

1. **Preparation:** Clean the water tank and fill it with fresh water up to a suitable level.
2. **Weighing:** Measure the total weight $W$ of the floating body using the weighing balance.
3. **Floating the Body:** Float the body in water and allow it to settle in equilibrium.
4. **Initial Reference:** Place the jockey at the center of the body and note the initial position of the plumb bob on the graduated scale.
5. **Shifting the Jockey:** Move the jockey by a known distance $a$ (say, 0.5 m, 1.0 m, 1.5 m) to one side of the deck.
6. **Measuring the Tilt:** The body tilts by a small angle. Measure the horizontal shift $x$ of the plumb bob and the length $L$ of the plumb bob from its suspension point.
7. **Calculation:** Calculate the metacentric height using:

$$
GM = \frac{w \cdot a \cdot L}{W \cdot x}
$$

8. **Repetition:** Repeat the experiment for different values of $a$ and tabulate the results.
9. **Average GM:** Calculate the average value of GM to minimize experimental error.

**Precautions:**
- The tilt angle should be small ($\theta \leq 15°$).
- The plumb bob should be freely suspended without touching the sides.
- The water should be still during the experiment (no vibrations).
- The jockey should be moved gently to avoid splashing.

---

**(b) A rectangular pontoon measures 4 m long, 2 m wide, and 1 m deep. It floats in fresh water at a draft of 0.5 m. Calculate: (i) the metacentric radius (BM), (ii) the distance BG, and (iii) the metacentric height (GM). (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Length, $L = 4 \text{ m}$
- Breadth, $B = 2 \text{ m}$
- Depth, $D = 1 \text{ m}$
- Draft, $h = 0.5 \text{ m}$
- Density of water, $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1: Calculate the waterline area**

$$
A_w = L \times B = 4 \times 2 = 8 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the second moment of area**

$$
I = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{4 \times 2^3}{12} = \frac{4 \times 8}{12} = \frac{32}{12} = 2.667 \text{ m}^4
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the submerged volume**

$$
V = A_w \times h = 8 \times 0.5 = 4 \text{ m}^3
$$

**(i) Metacentric Radius (BM):**

$$
BM = \frac{I}{V} = \frac{2.667}{4} = 0.667 \text{ m}
$$

**(ii) Distance BG:**

- Position of G from bottom = $D/2 = 1/2 = 0.5 \text{ m}$
- Position of B from bottom = $h/2 = 0.5/2 = 0.25 \text{ m}$

$$
BG = 0.5 - 0.25 = 0.25 \text{ m}
$$

**(iii) Metacentric Height (GM):**

$$
GM = BM - BG = 0.667 - 0.25
$$

$$
\boxed{GM = 0.417 \text{ m}}
$$

**Conclusion:** The pontoon is **stable** in its equilibrium position since $GM = 0.417 \text{ m} > 0$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Lead to Mark Deduction in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Sign Error in GM Formula:** Students often forget that $GM = BM - BG$ applies **only when G is above B**. When G is below B (e.g., in a ship with heavy keel), the formula is $GM = BM + GB$. Always draw the figure first to determine the relative position of G and B.
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency:** The angle of tilt must be in **degrees** when using a calculator's `tan` function, but the resulting $\tan\theta$ is a dimensionless ratio. Do not mix radians and degrees.
> 3. **Ignoring Small Angle Approximation:** The formula $GM = \dfrac{I \sin\theta}{V \sin\theta} = \dfrac{I}{V}$ is derived using the small angle approximation. For $\theta > 15°$, this formula is **not valid**.
> 4. **Forgetting the Plumb Line Length $L$:** In the plumb line method, students often compute $\tan\theta = x/L$ but forget to multiply by $L$ in the final GM formula. The correct formula is $GM = \dfrac{w \cdot a \cdot L}{W \cdot x}$.
> 5. **Confusing $I$ (Second Moment of Area) with $A$ (Area):** The second moment of area of a rectangle about its centroidal axis is $\dfrac{LB^3}{12}$, not $LB$. Mixing these up is a very common error.
> 6. **Not Stating the Stability Condition:** After computing GM, always explicitly state whether the body is stable, neutral, or unstable based on the sign of GM.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- **Center of Gravity (G):** Point of action of weight (W) acting vertically downward.
- **Center of Buoyancy (B):** Centroid of the displaced fluid volume; point of action of buoyant force ($F_B$) acting vertically upward.
- **Metacenter (M):** Intersection of the new vertical through $B'$ and the original vertical through B.
- **Metacentric Radius (BM):** $BM = I/V$, where $I$ is the second moment of area of the waterline plane and $V$ is the submerged volume.
- **Metacentric Height (GM):** $GM = BM - BG$ (if G is above B); $GM = BM + GB$ (if G is below B).
- **Stability Conditions:**
  - $GM > 0$: Stable equilibrium (restoring couple).
  - $GM = 0$: Neutral equilibrium (no couple).
  - $GM < 0$: Unstable equilibrium (overturning couple).
- **Experimental Formula (Jockey Method):** $GM = \dfrac{w \cdot a}{W \cdot \tan\theta}$.
- **Plumb Line Method:** $GM = \dfrac{w \cdot a \cdot L}{W \cdot x}$, where $\tan\theta = x/L$.
- **Second Moment of Area:**
  - Rectangle: $I = LB^3/12$
  - Circle: $I = \pi D^4/64$
- **Small Angle Criterion:** The formula $BM = I/V$ is valid only for $\theta \leq 15°$ (small angle approximation).
- **Units to Remember:** $I$ in $\text{m}^4$, $V$ in $\text{m}^3$, $BM$ and $GM$ in metres, $W$ and $w$ in Newtons.
- **Archimedes' Principle:** Weight of floating body = Weight of fluid displaced = $\rho_w \cdot g \cdot V$.
- **Practical Application:** Metacentric height is critical in ship design for ensuring stability in still water and rough seas.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Verification of Bernoulli's equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL407 - FLUID MECHANICS, OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS & HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Verification of Bernoulli's equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Bernoulli's Equation (KTU Standard Definition):**
> For a **steady, incompressible, inviscid (ideal) fluid** flowing along a **streamline**, the sum of the **pressure head**, **velocity head (kinetic head)**, and **elevation head (datum head)** remains **constant** at every point along that streamline.

Mathematically, between any two points (1) and (2) on a streamline:

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 \;=\; \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2$$

Where:
- $p/\rho g$ = **Pressure head** (m of fluid)
- $v^{2}/2g$ = **Velocity head / Kinetic head** (m of fluid)
- $z$ = **Elevation head / Datum head** (m)
- $\rho$ = **Mass density of fluid (1000 kg/m³ for water at 4°C)**
- $g$ = **Acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²)**

The term $p/\rho g + v^{2}/2g + z$ is also called the **Total Energy Head** ($H$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine a ball rolling down a smooth, frictionless hill:**
> 1. At the **top (high elevation)**, the ball moves **slowly** and has lots of **potential energy**.
> 2. As it rolls **down the slope (lower elevation)**, it **gains speed** (kinetic energy increases) but loses height.
> 3. In a **narrow valley (constriction)**, the ball **shoots forward faster** because the same energy must pass through a smaller cross-section.
> 4. If the valley is **pressurised** (e.g., you push it from behind), the velocity adjusts so the **total energy is conserved**.

A fluid particle behaves in **exactly the same way**. Energy cannot be created or destroyed in an ideal flow — it merely converts between **pressure, velocity, and elevation** forms.

## 1.3 The Experiment in One Sentence

> [!NOTE]
> **Aim of the Experiment:** To experimentally verify that the **Total Energy Head** ($p/\rho g + v^{2}/2g + z$) at different sections of a pipe carrying a flowing fluid remains **constant** (or nearly so, accounting for minor losses).

## 1.4 Standard Metric Values Used in KTU Labs

| Quantity | Standard Value | Unit |
|----------|---------------|------|
| Density of water ($\rho$) | **1000** | kg/m³ |
| Acceleration due to gravity ($g$) | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Specific weight of water ($\gamma = \rho g$) | **9810** | N/m³ |
| Atmospheric pressure | **101.325 kPa** | kPa |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Three Energy Heads

Bernoulli's equation is fundamentally a statement of **conservation of mechanical energy** per unit weight of fluid. It comprises three distinct heads:

### (a) Pressure Head ($\dfrac{p}{\rho g}$)
- Represents the **flow energy per unit weight** of the fluid due to its pressure.
- Measured using **piezometer tubes** or a **U-tube manometer** attached to the pipe wall (tapping measures **static pressure only**).
- Has units of **metres of fluid (m)**.

### (b) Velocity / Kinetic Head ($\dfrac{v^{2}}{2g}$)
- Represents the **kinetic energy per unit weight** of the fluid.
- Cannot be measured directly — it is calculated from the **continuity equation**.
- Has units of **metres of fluid (m)**.

### (c) Elevation / Datum Head ($z$)
- Represents the **potential energy per unit weight** of the fluid due to its position above a chosen **datum (reference plane)**.
- Measured as the **vertical height** of the pipe centreline from the datum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Trap:** The piezometer tap measures **static pressure** ($p$) only — it does **NOT** include the velocity head. Therefore, the total head at a section is **piezometric head + velocity head + datum head**.

## 2.2 Real-World Assumptions Required for the Equation to Hold

| Assumption | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|------------------|
| Steady flow | Flow parameters do not change with time |
| Incompressible fluid | $\rho =$ constant (valid for liquids) |
| Inviscid (frictionless) | No viscous shear losses |
| Along a streamline | Equation holds between two points on the **same streamline** |
| No shaft work | No pump/turbine between sections 1 and 2 |

In real laboratory experiments, the fluid is **viscous**, so a small **head loss ($h_L$)** is observed between sections:

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 \;=\; \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Sl. No. | Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = A \cdot v$ | Discharge (continuity equation) | m³/s |
| 2 | $v = Q/A$ | Velocity at a section | m/s |
| 3 | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^{2}$ | Cross-sectional area of pipe | m² |
| 4 | $Q = \dfrac{V}{t}$ | Discharge from collecting tank | m³/s |
| 5 | $\dfrac{p_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2$ | Bernoulli's equation (ideal) | m |
| 6 | $H = \dfrac{p}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v^{2}}{2g} + z$ | Total energy head at any section | m |
| 7 | $h_L = H_1 - H_2$ | Head loss between sections 1 and 2 | m |
| 8 | $p_m = (\rho_m - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot h$ | Manometer pressure (for differential U-tube) | Pa |
| 9 | $\dfrac{p}{\rho g} = x + h\left(1 - \dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho_w}\right)$ | Pressure head from differential manometer | m |
| 10 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v d}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (flow regime check) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols Glossary:** $Q$ = discharge, $A$ = cross-sectional area, $v$ = velocity, $d$ = pipe diameter, $V$ = volume collected, $t$ = time, $h$ = manometer deflection, $\rho_m$ = manometric fluid density (mercury = 13600 kg/m³), $\rho_w$ = water density, $x$ = distance of tapping from centreline.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Venturi meter design** (flow measurement in pipelines, water supply networks).
- **Pitot-static tube** in aircraft to measure airspeed.
- **Carburettor / atomiser** design — pressure drop creates fine fuel spray.
- **Draft tube** in reaction turbines (Francis, Kaplan).
- **Siphons, spillways, weirs** in open channels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation (From Euler's Equation of Motion)

We derive Bernoulli's equation from first principles to satisfy KTU's emphasis on theoretical rigour.

**Step 1 — Newton's Second Law for a Fluid Element**

Consider a small fluid element of cross-section $dA$ and length $ds$ along a streamline. Forces acting on it along the streamline direction are:
- **Pressure force** at upstream face: $p \, dA$
- **Pressure force** at downstream face: $(p + dp) \, dA$
- **Gravity component along streamline:** $- \rho g \, dA \, ds \, \dfrac{dz}{ds}$

Applying Newton's second law ($F = ma$) along the streamline:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p \, dA - (p + dp) \, dA - \rho g \, dA \, ds \, \frac{dz}{ds} &= \rho \, dA \, ds \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplification**

Cancel $dA$ throughout and expand $dz/ds \cdot ds = dz$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-dp - \rho g \, dz &= \rho \, ds \, \frac{dv}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Eulerian Form**

Using the chain rule, $dv/dt = (dv/ds)(ds/dt) = v \, (dv/ds)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-dp - \rho g \, dz &= \rho \, v \, \frac{dv}{ds} \, ds
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives the **Euler equation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dp}{\rho} + g \, dz + v \, dv &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate Along a Streamline**

For an **incompressible fluid**, $\rho = $ constant. Integrating between points 1 and 2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{1}^{2} \frac{dp}{\rho} + \int_{1}^{2} g \, dz + \int_{1}^{2} v \, dv &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{p_2 - p_1}{\rho} + g(z_2 - z_1) + \frac{v_2^{2} - v_1^{2}}{2} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to Standard Bernoulli Form**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{p_1}{\rho} + \frac{v_1^{2}}{2} + g z_1 &= \frac{p_2}{\rho} + \frac{v_2^{2}}{2} + g z_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing throughout by $g$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 &= \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **Bernoulli's equation** in its canonical form.

## 3.2 Derivation of Velocity at Each Section (Using Continuity)

In the experimental setup, the discharge $Q$ is **measured once** (using a collecting tank and stopwatch), and the velocity at each section is **calculated** using the continuity equation.

**Step 1 — Measure Discharge**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{\text{Volume collected } (V)}{\text{Time taken } (t)} \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Cross-Sectional Areas**

For a circular pipe of diameter $d_i$ at section $i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_i &= \frac{\pi}{4} d_i^{2} \quad \text{(m}^2\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Continuity Equation**

By conservation of mass (incompressible flow):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 = A_3 v_3 = \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_i &= \frac{Q}{A_i} = \frac{4Q}{\pi d_i^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** If pipe diameter **changes** between sections, the velocity **must** be computed separately at each section. Do **not** assume $v_1 = v_2$.

## 3.3 Derivation of Pressure Head from Manometer Reading

In a typical Bernoulli's apparatus, a **differential U-tube manometer** is connected between two tapping points. For a manometer with manometric fluid of density $\rho_m$ (e.g., mercury, $\rho_m = 13600$ kg/m³) and water as the flowing fluid ($\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³):

**Step 1 — Write Manometric Equation**

Balancing pressures from tapping 1 to tapping 2 through the manometer:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1 + \rho_w g (z_1 + x) - \rho_m g h - \rho_w g (z_2 + x - h) &= p_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify**

The $x$ terms cancel; rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(p_1 - p_2) + \rho_w g (z_1 - z_2) &= (\rho_m - \rho_w) g h
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Pressure Head Difference**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{p_1}{\rho_w g} + z_1\right) - \left(\frac{p_2}{\rho_w g} + z_2\right) &= h \left(\frac{\rho_m}{\rho_w} - 1\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **horizontal pipe** ($z_1 = z_2$), this reduces to the well-known result:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho_w g} &= h \left(\frac{\rho_m}{\rho_w} - 1\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **piezometric head difference** between the two sections, which is what is directly read from the differential manometer.

## 3.4 Calculation of Total Head at Each Section

At section $i$, the total head is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_i = \frac{p_i}{\rho g} + \frac{v_i^{2}}{2g} + z_i
\end{aligned}
$$

In a horizontal test rig where $z_i$ is the same for all sections, $z_i$ is often **neglected** (or taken as zero with the pipe axis as datum), giving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_i = \frac{p_i}{\rho g} + \frac{v_i^{2}}{2g}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Model Solution)

**Problem:** Water flows through a horizontal pipe of varying diameter. At section 1, diameter $d_1 = 50$ mm and pressure $p_1 = 80$ kN/m² (gauge). At section 2, diameter $d_2 = 25$ mm and pressure $p_2 = 50$ kN/m² (gauge). The discharge is $Q = 0.002$ m³/s. Verify Bernoulli's equation.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute areas:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= \frac{\pi}{4} (0.050)^{2} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^{2} \\
A_2 &= \frac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^{2} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Compute velocities (continuity):

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_1 &= \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{0.002}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.019 \;\text{m/s} \\
v_2 &= \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{0.002}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.074 \;\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Compute pressure heads:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{p_1}{\rho g} &= \frac{80 \times 10^{3}}{1000 \times 9.81} = 8.155 \;\text{m of water} \\
\frac{p_2}{\rho g} &= \frac{50 \times 10^{3}}{1000 \times 9.81} = 5.097 \;\text{m of water}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Compute velocity heads:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{v_1^{2}}{2g} &= \frac{(1.019)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.0529 \;\text{m} \\
\frac{v_2^{2}}{2g} &= \frac{(4.074)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.8459 \;\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 5 — Total heads (horizontal pipe → $z_1 = z_2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_1 &= 8.155 + 0.0529 + 0 = 8.208 \;\text{m} \\
H_2 &= 5.097 + 0.8459 + 0 = 5.943 \;\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 6 — Head loss:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_L &= H_1 - H_2 = 8.208 - 5.943 = 2.265 \;\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 7 — Verification:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since $H_1 \neq H_2$, Bernoulli's equation in its **ideal form** is not satisfied — but the **modified form** $H_1 = H_2 + h_L$ **holds**. The difference $h_L = 2.265$ m represents the **frictional and minor losses** between the two sections. In laboratory practice, the loss is typically much smaller (a few cm).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Experimental Setup

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **functional flow** of the Bernoulli's apparatus — how water moves from the sump tank through the test pipe and back, with measurement points clearly identified.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sump Tank] --> B[Centrifugal Pump]
    B --> C[Stabilising Tank with Overflow]
    C --> D[Inlet Section 1 - Diameter d1]
    D --> E[Throat Section 2 - Diameter d2 Smaller]
    E --> F[Outlet Section 3 - Diameter d3]
    F --> G[Control Valve]
    G --> H[Collecting Tank with Measuring Scale]
    H --> A
    D -.-> P1[Piezometer Tap at Section 1]
    E -.-> P2[Piezometer Tap at Section 2]
    F -.-> P3[Piezometer Tap at Section 3]
    P1 --> M[Differential U-tube Manometer]
    P2 --> M
    P3 --> M
    H -.-> T[Stopwatch for Time Measurement]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SUPPLY[Water Supply and Pumping Unit]
        S1[Sump Tank] --> S2[Monoblock Pump]
        S2 --> S3[Delivery Pipe]
    end
    subgraph TEST[Variable Cross-Section Test Pipe]
        T1[Inlet Larger Section d1] --> T2[Converging Section]
        T2 --> T3[Throat Smallest Section d2]
        T3 --> T4[Diverging Section]
        T4 --> T5[Outlet Larger Section d3]
    end
    subgraph MEAS[Measurement Subsystem]
        M1[Piezometer Taps at Each Section]
        M2[U-tube Differential Manometer]
        M3[Collecting Tank with Volumetric Scale]
        M4[Stopwatch for Discharge Timing]
    end
    subgraph CTRL[Flow Control]
        C1[Rotameter or Control Valve]
    end
    SUPPLY --> TEST
    TEST --> CTRL
    CTRL --> MEAS
    M1 --> M2
    M3 --> M4
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Energy Head Distribution Along the Pipe

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1["Section 1: Total Head H1 = p1/ρg + v1²/2g + z1"] --> H2["Section 2: Total Head H2 = p2/ρg + v2²/2g + z2"]
    H2 --> H3["Section 3: Total Head H3 = p3/ρg + v3²/2g + z3"]
    H1 -. "Head loss hL1" .-> H2
    H2 -. "Head loss hL2" .-> H3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** In a **real (viscous) fluid**, the total head $H$ decreases **progressively** in the direction of flow due to frictional losses. In an **ideal fluid**, the heads would be equal at all sections (horizontal dashed line).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State Bernoulli's equation. List the assumptions made in its derivation.**

**Model Answer:**

Bernoulli's equation states that for a **steady, incompressible, inviscid fluid** flowing along a streamline, the sum of pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head is constant:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{v^{2}}{2g} + z &= \text{constant}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Assumptions:**
1. **Steady flow** — flow parameters do not vary with time.
2. **Incompressible fluid** — $\rho$ is constant.
3. **Inviscid (frictionless)** — no viscous shear stress.
4. **Flow along a streamline** — equation applies between two points on the same streamline.
5. **No energy addition or extraction** — no pump, turbine, or heat transfer between sections.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between static pressure, dynamic pressure, and total pressure. How is each measured?**

**Model Answer:**

| Type of Pressure | Definition | Measurement Instrument |
|------------------|------------|------------------------|
| **Static Pressure** ($p$) | Pressure exerted by fluid at rest; pressure felt by a wall-parallel surface | **Piezometer tap** (wall tap parallel to flow) |
| **Dynamic Pressure** ($\tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^{2}$) | Pressure due to velocity of fluid; exists only when fluid is moving | Cannot be measured directly; calculated from $v = Q/A$ |
| **Total Pressure / Stagnation Pressure** ($p_0$) | Sum of static and dynamic pressure ($p + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^{2}$) | **Pitot tube** (faces the flow directly) |

The **piezometric pressure** is $p + \rho g z$ (static + elevation component).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never** write the Bernoulli equation without dividing by $\rho g$ — always express in **head form (metres of fluid)**, not in pressure (Pa).
> 2. **Always compute velocity at each section** using continuity — do not assume $v_1 = v_2$ even if the pipe looks uniform.
> 3. **Watch the units**: diameter must be in **metres** (mm values must be converted: 25 mm = 0.025 m).
> 4. In the experiment, **do not confuse the piezometer reading with total head** — the velocity head must be **added** to the piezometric head to obtain the total head.
> 5. **Head loss** $h_L$ should be reported as a small positive value (typically 0.5–5% of total head) — very large losses indicate an experimental error.
> 6. **Use $g = 9.81$ m/s²** (not 9.8 or 10) unless specified otherwise.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Derive Bernoulli's equation for the steady flow of an ideal fluid along a streamline, starting from Euler's equation of motion. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Water flows through a horizontal pipe of diameter 60 mm at section 1 and 30 mm at section 2. The discharge is 4 litres/second. The pressures at sections 1 and 2 are 120 kN/m² and 60 kN/m² respectively. Verify Bernoulli's theorem and calculate the head loss between the two sections. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

The complete derivation is as shown in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The examiner expects the following checkpoints:

1. Free-body diagram of fluid element along streamline (1 mark)
2. Application of Newton's second law → Euler's equation (2 marks)
3. Use of chain rule: $dv/dt = v \, dv/ds$ (1 mark)
4. Integration for incompressible flow (1 mark)
5. Listing of 5 assumptions (2 marks)

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $d_1 = 60$ mm = 0.06 m, $d_2 = 30$ mm = 0.03 m, $Q = 4$ L/s = $4 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s, $p_1 = 120$ kN/m² = $120 \times 10^{3}$ N/m², $p_2 = 60$ kN/m² = $60 \times 10^{3}$ N/m², horizontal pipe.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional areas:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= \frac{\pi}{4} (0.06)^{2} = 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^{2} \\
A_2 &= \frac{\pi}{4} (0.03)^{2} = 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Velocities (continuity):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_1 &= \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{2.827 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.415 \;\text{m/s} \\
v_2 &= \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{7.069 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.659 \;\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Pressure heads:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{p_1}{\rho g} &= \frac{120 \times 10^{3}}{1000 \times 9.81} = 12.232 \;\text{m} \\
\frac{p_2}{\rho g} &= \frac{60 \times 10^{3}}{1000 \times 9.81} = 6.116 \;\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Velocity heads:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{v_1^{2}}{2g} &= \frac{(1.415)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.1020 \;\text{m} \\
\frac{v_2^{2}}{2g} &= \frac{(5.659)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81} = 1.6321 \;\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Total heads (horizontal pipe → $z_1 = z_2 = 0$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_1 &= 12.232 + 0.1020 + 0 = 12.334 \;\text{m} \\
H_2 &= 6.116 + 1.6321 + 0 = 7.748 \;\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Head loss and verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_L &= H_1 - H_2 = 12.334 - 7.748 = 4.586 \;\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Bernoulli's equation in **modified form** $H_1 = H_2 + h_L$ is **verified**, with the head loss $h_L = 4.586$ m. The loss is significant in this problem because the diameter ratio is large (2:1), creating substantial frictional and minor losses due to flow separation at the contraction.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the experimental setup used to verify Bernoulli's theorem. List the observations recorded and the formulae used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a Bernoulli's apparatus, the discharge measured is 1.2 L/s. The diameters at sections 1, 2, and 3 are 50 mm, 25 mm, and 50 mm respectively. The piezometric heads at these sections (measured from a common datum) are 4.2 m, 3.6 m, and 3.9 m respectively. Calculate the total head at each section and comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Description (7 marks):**

The setup consists of:

1. **Sump tank** (storage of water).
2. **Centrifugal pump** to lift water to the test section.
3. **Stabilising tank** with overflow arrangement to ensure constant head supply.
4. **Test pipe** with **variable cross-section** (larger at inlet → smaller at throat → larger at outlet, like a venturi shape).
5. **Piezometer taps** at three (or more) sections, connected to a **differential U-tube manometer** containing mercury (or carbon tetrachloride) as manometric fluid.
6. **Control valve** at the downstream end to vary the flow rate.
7. **Collecting tank** with a graduated scale to measure discharge volume, and a **stopwatch** to record time.

**Observations recorded:**
- Diameter at each section ($d_1, d_2, d_3$)
- Manometer deflection ($h$)
- Volume of water collected ($V$) in collecting tank
- Time taken ($t$)

**Formulae used:**

- Discharge: $Q = V/t$
- Area: $A = \pi d^{2}/4$
- Velocity: $v = Q/A$
- Piezometric head: $\dfrac{p}{\rho g} + z$ (from manometer)
- Velocity head: $v^{2}/2g$
- Total head: $H = \dfrac{p}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v^{2}}{2g} + z$
- Head loss: $h_L = H_1 - H_2$

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $Q = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s, $d_1 = d_3 = 0.050$ m, $d_2 = 0.025$ m, piezometric heads: $P_1 = 4.2$ m, $P_2 = 3.6$ m, $P_3 = 3.9$ m (where $P_i = p_i/\rho g + z_i$).

**Step 1 — Areas:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 = A_3 &= \frac{\pi}{4} (0.050)^{2} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^{2} \\
A_2 &= \frac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^{2} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Velocities:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_1 = v_3 &= \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.6114 \;\text{m/s} \\
v_2 &= \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.4445 \;\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Velocity heads:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{v_1^{2}}{2g} = \frac{v_3^{2}}{2g} &= \frac{(0.6114)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.0191 \;\text{m} \\
\frac{v_2^{2}}{2g} &= \frac{(2.4445)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.3046 \;\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total heads:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_1 &= P_1 + \frac{v_1^{2}}{2g} = 4.2 + 0.0191 = 4.219 \;\text{m} \\
H_2 &= P_2 + \frac{v_2^{2}}{2g} = 3.6 + 0.3046 = 3.905 \;\text{m} \\
H_3 &= P_3 + \frac{v_3^{2}}{2g} = 3.9 + 0.0191 = 3.919 \;\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Head losses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{L1-2} &= H_1 - H_2 = 4.219 - 3.905 = 0.314 \;\text{m} \\
h_{L2-3} &= H_2 - H_3 = 3.905 - 3.919 = -0.014 \;\text{m} \;\;(\approx 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comment:** The total head decreases from section 1 → section 2 by about 0.314 m, representing the **converging-section loss** (contraction). Between section 2 and section 3 (diverging), the head is nearly constant (slight experimental error). The **theoretical (ideal) Bernoulli** prediction is that $H_1 \approx H_2 \approx H_3$; the deviation here is due to **frictional and minor losses**, confirming the **modified Bernoulli equation** $H_1 = H_2 + h_L$. The result **qualitatively verifies** Bernoulli's theorem.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Verification of Bernoulli's Equation**

### Conceptual Recap
- **Bernoulli's equation** is a statement of **conservation of mechanical energy** per unit weight of fluid along a streamline.
- The total head $H = p/\rho g + v^{2}/2g + z$ comprises **pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head**.
- For an **ideal fluid**, $H$ is constant; for a **real fluid**, $H$ decreases in the flow direction by an amount equal to the **head loss $h_L$**.

### Experimental Setup
- A **variable-area test pipe** (venturi-shaped) with **piezometer taps** at multiple sections.
- A **differential U-tube manometer** (mercury) measures the **piezometric head difference**.
- A **collecting tank** + **stopwatch** is used to measure the **volumetric discharge $Q$**.

### Key Formulae to Memorise
- $Q = V/t = A \cdot v$ (continuity)
- $A = \pi d^{2}/4$
- $H = p/\rho g + v^{2}/2g + z$
- $h_L = H_1 - H_2$
- Piezometric head from manometer: $\Delta(p/\rho g) = h \left( \rho_m / \rho_w - 1 \right)$

### Calculation Steps (in Order)
1. Measure discharge $Q = V/t$ (using collecting tank + stopwatch).
2. Compute area at each section: $A_i = \pi d_i^{2}/4$.
3. Compute velocity at each section: $v_i = Q/A_i$.
4. Compute pressure head at each section: $p_i/\rho g$ (or read piezometric head from manometer).
5. Compute velocity head: $v_i^{2}/2g$.
6. Compute total head: $H_i = p_i/\rho g + v_i^{2}/2g + z_i$.
7. Compare total heads — they should be **approximately equal** (with small $h_L$ for real fluids).

### Common Sources of Error
- **Air bubbles** trapped in manometer → give false readings (always bleed the manometer before taking readings).
- **Fluctuating supply head** → use a stabilising tank with overflow.
- **Inaccurate timing** of discharge collection → use a large collecting tank and longer time.
- **Misaligned pipe tappings** → ensure tappings are perpendicular to flow and at the same datum.
- **Temperature variations** → affects $\rho$ and $\gamma$ slightly (use 1000 kg/m³ for water at room temperature).

### Key Engineering Applications
- **Venturi meter, orifice meter, nozzle meter** (flow measurement)
- **Pitot-static tube** (aircraft airspeed indicator)
- **Carburettor, atomiser, sprayer** (low-pressure → high-velocity principle)
- **Draft tube** in reaction turbines (recovers pressure at turbine exit)

### Exam-Day Reminders
- Use $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³.
- Convert all diameters to **metres** before computing area.
- Express the final answer in **head form (metres of fluid)**, not in pressure units.
- Show **all steps** with explicit units — partial credit is given for correct intermediate steps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of coefficient of discharge and calibration of orifice meter, venturimeter, and nozzle meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL407 - FLUID MECHANICS, OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS & HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of coefficient of discharge and calibration of orifice meter, venturimeter, and nozzle meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flow Measurement Devices (Obstruction-Type Meters):**
> These devices measure the discharge (flow rate) through a closed conduit/pipeline by introducing a deliberate constriction. The resulting drop in pressure (or piezometric head) across the constriction is mathematically related to the discharge using **Bernoulli's Equation** and the **Continuity Equation**.

### The Three Meters under Study:

**1. Orifice Meter**
> [!NOTE]
> A thin, flat circular plate (sharp-edged orifice plate) with a precisely machined concentric hole is clamped between two flanges of a pipe. The plate thickness is negligible compared to pipe diameter. The flow contracts sharply after the orifice forming a **vena contracta**.

**2. Venturimeter**
> [!NOTE]
> A convergent-divergent pipe assembly with a smoothly shaped throat. The inlet has a cone angle of **≈ 21°** (convergent) and outlet has a divergent angle of **≈ 5° to 7°** to minimize frictional losses. Throat diameter is typically **0.4 to 0.7 times** the pipe diameter.

**3. Nozzle Meter**
> [!NOTE]
> A streamlined convergent profile (bell-shaped nozzle) attached inside the pipe. The nozzle profile follows the shape of a free jet. Unlike orifice, there is no sharp edge — the flow converges smoothly with minimal separation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a Highway Toll Booth Analogy:**
> Picture water flowing through a wide highway. Suddenly, we install a narrower gate. Cars must squeeze through this gate, accelerating as they pass. Immediately downstream, traffic spreads out again. This acceleration causes a "pressure drop" — measured by the difference between the calm water levels upstream and at the narrow point.
>
> - **Orifice meter** = Like a sharp-edged sudden bottleneck (the water jet contracts dramatically).
> - **Venturimeter** = Like a smooth, funnel-shaped ramp (water glides through without losing energy).
> - **Nozzle meter** = Like a polished, curved nozzle (similar to venturi but shorter and cheaper).
>
> By measuring how much the "pressure" drops, we can calculate how fast water is moving — just like toll booth cameras count cars!

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²**
- **Density of water (ρ):** **1000 kg/m³** (at 4°C); **998 kg/m³** (at 20°C)
- **Specific weight of water (γ):** **9810 N/m³**
- **Standard manometer fluid:** Mercury (Hg), with **S.H.G = 13.6**
- **Typical Cd values** (in actual practice):
  - Orifice meter: **0.60 – 0.65**
  - Venturimeter: **0.95 – 0.99**
  - Nozzle meter: **0.95 – 0.98**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The closer **Cd** is to **1.0**, the more ideal (lossless) the meter is. The orifice meter has the lowest Cd due to high energy losses at the sharp edge.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Bernoulli's Equation

Applying Bernoulli's equation between section (1) — upstream of meter, and section (2) — at the throat/orifice vena contracta:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

Assuming the pipe is horizontal (**z₁ = z₂**) and neglecting losses temporarily (**h_L = 0**):

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} = h$$

This **h** is the **piezometric head difference** (measured by manometer).

---

## 2.2 Applying Continuity Equation

From **Conservation of Mass** for incompressible flow:

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2$$

---

## 2.3 The Derivation of Theoretical Discharge

Substituting V₁ in Bernoulli's equation and solving for V₂:

$$h = \frac{V_2^2 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 V_2^2}{2g} = \frac{V_2^2}{2g}\left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]$$

$$V_2 = \frac{\sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}$$

The **theoretical discharge** is:

$$Q_{th} = A_2 V_2 = \frac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}$$

---

## 2.4 Coefficient of Discharge (Cd)

The **actual discharge** is always less than theoretical due to frictional losses, turbulence, and vena contracta effects. So:

$$\boxed{C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}}$$

Therefore, the **actual discharge equation** for all three meters is:

$$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$

---

## 2.5 Differential Head 'h' Using a U-Tube Manometer

When a differential **U-tube mercury manometer** is used:

$$h = \frac{x \left(\frac{S_{Hg}}{S_w} - 1\right)}{1} = x\left(\frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1\right)$$

Where **x** = manometer deflection (in m), **S_Hg = 13.6** for mercury.

> [!TIP]
> A differential manometer is preferred over piezometers because it magnifies small pressure differences and gives accurate readings.

---

## 2.6 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---------|----------------|------|
| 1 | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \sqrt{2gh}$ | Theoretical discharge | m³/s |
| 2 | $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot Q_{th}$ | Actual discharge | m³/s |
| 3 | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | Coefficient of discharge | dimensionless |
| 4 | $V_2 = \dfrac{\sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$ | Velocity at throat | m/s |
| 5 | $h = x\left(\dfrac{S_{Hg}}{S_w} - 1\right)$ | Differential head (manometer) | m |
| 6 | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | Area of pipe / orifice | m² |
| 7 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{VD}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless |
| 8 | $Q_{act} = A_{tank} \cdot \dfrac{dh}{dt}$ | Volumetric method (calibration tank) | m³/s |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These meters are extensively used in:
> - **Water treatment plants** — measuring raw water intake
> - **Oil and gas pipelines** — custody transfer metering
> - **Steam and air distribution** — boiler feed systems
> - **Chemical plants** — process control
> - **Power plants** — cooling water circulation
> - **HVAC systems** — building automation

Calibration ensures the meter gives **accurate, traceable readings** which are legally binding in commercial transactions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Discharge Equation (Bernoulli + Continuity)

### STEP 1: State the assumptions

- Steady, incompressible, irrotational flow
- No energy loss (ideal case, to be corrected by Cd later)
- Pipe is horizontal
- Manometric fluid is mercury with S.H.G = 13.6

### STEP 2: Apply Bernoulli's Equation between Section (1) and Section (2)

$$\frac{P_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\gamma} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

### STEP 3: Simplify using horizontal pipe condition

Since z₁ = z₂, the elevation terms cancel:

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\gamma} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$

### STEP 4: Define pressure head difference as h

$$h = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\gamma}$$

Therefore:

$$h = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$

### STEP 5: Apply Continuity Equation

For incompressible steady flow:

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$

Solve for V₁:

$$V_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} \cdot V_2$$

### STEP 6: Substitute V₁ into Bernoulli's Equation

$$h = \frac{V_2^2 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 \cdot V_2^2}{2g}$$

$$h = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} \left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]$$

### STEP 7: Solve for V₂

$$V_2^2 = \frac{2gh}{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}$$

$$V_2 = \frac{\sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}$$

### STEP 8: Multiply V₂ by A₂ to get Qth

$$Q_{th} = A_2 V_2 = \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}$$

### STEP 9: Multiply numerator and denominator by A₁

$$Q_{th} = \frac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}$$

### STEP 10: Introduce Coefficient of Discharge for actual flow

$$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the universal discharge equation for all three meters** (orifice, venturi, nozzle). The only difference is the value of **Cd**, which depends on the geometry.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Pattern)

> **Given:**
> - Pipe diameter, D₁ = **50 mm** = 0.05 m
> - Orifice diameter, D₂ = **25 mm** = 0.025 m
> - Differential head, h = **0.20 m** of water
> - Actual discharge measured in calibration tank = **0.85 × 10⁻³ m³/s**

**Find:** Coefficient of discharge (Cd)

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Calculate areas

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.05)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the ratio term

$$\frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} = \frac{(1.9635 \times 10^{-3})(4.909 \times 10^{-4})}{\sqrt{(1.9635 \times 10^{-3})^2 - (4.909 \times 10^{-4})^2}}$$

Numerator:

$$= 9.638 \times 10^{-7}$$

Denominator (inside sqrt):

$$= 3.855 \times 10^{-6} - 2.410 \times 10^{-7} = 3.614 \times 10^{-6}$$

Square root:

$$= 1.901 \times 10^{-3}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} = \frac{9.638 \times 10^{-7}}{1.901 \times 10^{-3}} = 5.07 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate √(2gh)

$$\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.20} = \sqrt{3.924} = 1.981 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate Qth

$$Q_{th} = 5.07 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.981 = 1.004 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate Cd

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{0.85 \times 10^{-3}}{1.004 \times 10^{-3}} = \mathbf{0.847}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A value of **Cd ≈ 0.85** is reasonable for an orifice meter if flow is well-developed. For ideal laboratory conditions with sharp-edged orifice, expect **0.60 – 0.65**.

---

## 3.3 Calibration Procedure — Analytical Matrix

| Step | Action | Instrument Used | Output Recorded |
|------|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Note pipe diameter D₁ & meter throat diameter D₂ | Vernier caliper | D₁, D₂ in metres |
| 2 | Start pump; let flow stabilize | Centrifugal pump | — |
| 3 | Adjust flow valve to set flow rate | Sluice valve | — |
| 4 | Read manometer deflection x | Differential manometer | x in m |
| 5 | Collect water in calibration tank for time t | Stopwatch + tank | Rise (R), time (t) |
| 6 | Calculate actual discharge | Volumetric method | Q_act = A_tank × R/t |
| 7 | Compute h from manometer reading | Equation h = x(S_Hg/S_w − 1) | h in m of water |
| 8 | Compute Q_th from formula | Universal equation | Q_th in m³/s |
| 9 | Compute Cd = Q_act / Q_th | Calculator | Cd |
| 10 | Repeat for 5–6 different flow rates | — | Data table |
| 11 | Plot Q_act vs √h (graph) | Graph paper | Straight line through origin |

> [!TIP]
> The calibration curve **Q_act vs √h** should be a **straight line passing through the origin**. Its slope is **Cd × (A₁A₂)/√(A₁² − A₂²) × √(2g)**, allowing determination of Cd graphically.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
