# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define minor losses in pipe flow. List any four types of fittings that cause minor losses.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Minor losses are the localized head losses in a pipe flow system caused by the disturbance of the streamline flow pattern when fluid passes through fittings, bends, valves, sudden changes in cross-section, or entry/exit sections. They are expressed as $h_m = K \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$, where $K$ is the loss coefficient (dimensionless).

**Four types of fittings that cause minor losses:**

1. **Sudden Enlargement** — abrupt increase in pipe diameter
2. **Sudden Contraction** — abrupt decrease in pipe diameter
3. **Bends/Elbows** — change in flow direction
4. **Valves** — gate valve, globe valve, check valve, etc.

(Other acceptable: entrance, exit, obstruction, Tee-junction)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] State the Borda-Carnot equation for head loss due to sudden enlargement. Under what conditions is this loss maximum?

**Model Answer:**

**Borda-Carnot Equation:**

$$h_e = \dfrac{(V_1 - V_2)^2}{2g} = \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} \left(1 - \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2$$

where $V_1$ = upstream velocity, $V_2$ = downstream velocity, $A_1$ and $A_2$ are the corresponding cross-sectional areas.

**Condition for Maximum Loss:**

The head loss is **maximum** when $V_2 = 0$, i.e., when the fluid suddenly enters a very large reservoir (or $A_2 \gg A_1$). In this limit:

$$h_{e,max} = \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g}$$

This is the case of **sudden discharge into a dead-end** or full pipe exit, with $K_e = 1.0$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Internal Choice — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / Model Paper]** A pipe of diameter 100 mm carrying water discharges into a larger pipe of diameter 200 mm. The flow rate is 10 litres/second. (a) Calculate the head loss due to sudden enlargement. (b) If a 90° elbow with $K = 0.9$ is placed immediately downstream, calculate the additional head loss and the total head loss in the system.

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m
- $D_2 = 200$ mm $= 0.2$ m
- $Q = 10$ L/s $= 0.01$ m³/s
- $K_{elbow} = 0.9$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

---

#### Part (a) — Head Loss Due to Sudden Enlargement (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Calculate Cross-Sectional Areas**

$$A_1 = \dfrac{\pi D_1^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \; m^2$$

$$A_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.2)^2}{4} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-2} \; m^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate Velocities using Continuity**

$$V_1 = \dfrac{Q}{A_1} = \dfrac{0.01}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.273 \; m/s$$

$$V_2 = \dfrac{Q}{A_2} = \dfrac{0.01}{3.1416 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.3183 \; m/s$$

**Step 3: Apply Borda-Carnot Equation**

$$h_e = \dfrac{(V_1 - V_2)^2}{2g} = \dfrac{(1.273 - 0.3183)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_e = \dfrac{(0.9547)^2}{19.62} = \dfrac{0.9115}{19.62}$$

$$\boxed{h_e = 0.04646 \; m \; = \; 4.646 \; cm \; of \; water}$$

**Step 4: Loss Coefficient (Verification)**

$$K_e = \left(1 - \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2 = \left(1 - \dfrac{0.1^2}{0.2^2}\right)^2 = (1 - 0.25)^2 = 0.5625$$

Verification: $h_e = K_e \cdot V_1^2/(2g) = 0.5625 \times 0.0826 = 0.0465$ m ✓

---

#### Part (b) — Additional Loss Due to Elbow and Total Loss (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Head Loss in 90° Elbow (using $V_2$ as reference)**

$$h_{elbow} = K_{elbow} \cdot \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} = 0.9 \times \dfrac{(0.3183)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_{elbow} = 0.9 \times \dfrac{0.1013}{19.62} = 0.9 \times 0.005165$$

$$\boxed{h_{elbow} = 0.00465 \; m \; = \; 4.65 \; mm \; of \; water}$$

**Step 2: Total Head Loss in the System**

$$h_L = h_e + h_{elbow} = 0.04646 + 0.00465 = 0.05111 \; m$$

$$\boxed{h_L \approx 51.1 \; mm \; of \; water}$$

**Step 3: Discussion**

The sudden enlargement contributes **~91%** of the total loss, while the elbow contributes only **~9%**. This illustrates how geometry changes dominate minor losses in pipe systems.

---

### Internal Choice — Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 / Model Paper]** (a) Derive the Borda-Carnot equation for head loss due to sudden enlargement in a pipe. (b) Two pipes of diameters 150 mm and 300 mm are connected by a sudden enlargement. The flow of water is 30 litres/second. Calculate: (i) the head loss, (ii) the loss coefficient, and (iii) the power lost due to this enlargement.

---

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Borda-Carnot Equation (7 Marks)

**Statement:** When a fluid flows from a smaller pipe into a larger one through a sudden enlargement, the head loss is given by $h_e = (V_1 - V_2)^2/(2g)$.

**Proof:**

Consider a control volume between section 1 (just before enlargement, in smaller pipe of area $A_1$, velocity $V_1$, pressure $P_1$) and section 2 (in the larger pipe, area $A_2$, velocity $V_2$, pressure $P_2$).

**Step 1: Continuity Equation**

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_2 = \dfrac{A_1}{A_2} V_1$$

**Step 2: Momentum Equation (in direction of flow)**

The net force on the fluid equals the rate of change of momentum:

$$P_1 A_2 - P_2 A_2 = \rho Q (V_2 - V_1) = \rho A_1 V_1 (V_2 - V_1)$$

Note: $A_2$ is used (not $A_1$) because the pressure $P_1$ acts on the projected annular area at section 1, which is geometrically the area $A_2$ in the larger pipe.

Dividing by $\rho g A_2$:

$$\dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \dfrac{A_1 V_1 (V_2 - V_1)}{g A_2} = \dfrac{V_2 (V_2 - V_1)}{g}$$

(using $V_2 = A_1 V_1 / A_2$)

**Step 3: Bernoulli Equation with head loss**

$$\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_e$$

$$\dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \dfrac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} + h_e$$

**Step 4: Equate the Two Expressions**

$$\dfrac{V_2 (V_2 - V_1)}{g} = \dfrac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} + h_e$$

$$h_e = \dfrac{2 V_2 (V_2 - V_1) - (V_2^2 - V_1^2)}{2g}$$

$$h_e = \dfrac{2 V_2^2 - 2 V_1 V_2 - V_2^2 + V_1^2}{2g} = \dfrac{V_1^2 - 2 V_1 V_2 + V_2^2}{2g}$$

$$\boxed{h_e = \dfrac{(V_1 - V_2)^2}{2g}}$$

This is the **Borda-Carnot equation**. In terms of area ratio:

$$h_e = \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} \left(1 - \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2$$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 150$ mm $= 0.15$ m, $D_2 = 300$ mm $= 0.30$ m
- $Q = 30$ L/s $= 0.03$ m³/s
- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ (water)
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1: Areas**

$$A_1 = \dfrac{\pi (0.15)^2}{4} = 0.01767 \; m^2 \quad ; \quad A_2 = \dfrac{\pi (0.30)^2}{4} = 0.07069 \; m^2$$

**Step 2: Velocities**

$$V_1 = \dfrac{0.03}{0.01767} = 1.698 \; m/s \quad ; \quad V_2 = \dfrac{0.03}{0.07069} = 0.4244 \; m/s$$

**(i) Head Loss**

$$h_e = \dfrac{(1.698 - 0.4244)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{(1.274)^2}{19.62} = \dfrac{1.622}{19.62}$$

$$\boxed{h_e = 0.0827 \; m \; \approx \; 8.27 \; cm \; of \; water}$$

**(ii) Loss Coefficient**

$$K_e = \left(1 - \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2 = \left(1 - \dfrac{0.01767}{0.07069}\right)^2 = (1 - 0.25)^2 = 0.5625$$

$$\boxed{K_e = 0.5625}$$

**(iii) Power Lost**

$$P_{lost} = \rho g Q h_e = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.03 \times 0.0827$$

$$P_{lost} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.002481$$

$$\boxed{P_{lost} = 24.34 \; W}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (Losing 2–4 Marks Each):**
>
> 1. **Wrong velocity reference:** Students often use $V_1$ for both contraction and enlargement. KTU expects $V_2$ (smaller section) for **contraction** and $V_1$ (smaller section) for **enlargement**.
> 2. **Sign error in Borda-Carnot:** The equation is $(V_1 - V_2)^2/(2g)$, not $(V_2 - V_1)^2/(2g)$. The square makes it positive, but students must show it correctly.
> 3. **Manometer equation error:** For mercury-water, the factor is $(S_{Hg} - 1) = 12.6$, NOT $13.6$. Many students write $13.6 \cdot \Delta x$.
> 4. **Forgetting $\rho g$ in power loss:** $P = \rho g Q h$, not $P = Qh$ (units will be wrong).
> 5. **Confusing major and minor loss formulas:** $h_f = 4fLV^2/(2gD)$ vs $h_m = K V^2/(2g)$. Examiner will award 0 if confused.
> 6. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing cm and m. Always convert to SI units (m, m/s, m³/s) before calculation.
> 7. **Skipping manometer derivation:** KTU expects you to write the manometer equation $\Delta h = 12.6 x$ explicitly with the specific gravity substituted.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Minor losses are localized head losses due to flow disturbance at fittings, expressed as $h_m = K \cdot V^2/(2g)$.
- **Total Head Loss:** $h_L = h_f + h_m$ where $h_f$ is major (friction) loss and $h_m$ is the sum of all minor losses.
- **Borda-Carnot Equation** for sudden enlargement: $h_e = (V_1 - V_2)^2/(2g) = V_1^2/(2g) \cdot (1 - A_1/A_2)^2$
- **Sudden Contraction:** $h_c \approx 0.5 V_2^2/(2g)$
- **Entrance Loss:** $K_e = 0.5$ (sharp), $K_e = 0.04$ (well-rounded)
- **Exit Loss:** $K_{ex} = 1.0$ (full velocity head lost)
- **Equivalent Length Concept:** $L_e = KD/(4f)$ — a useful equivalence between fittings and straight pipe.
- **Manometer Reading:** For mercury-water U-tube manometer, $h = 12.6 \cdot x$ (where $x$ is manometer deflection in metres).
- **Experimental $K$:** $K = 2g \cdot h_m / V^2$
- **Plot:** $h_m$ vs $V^2$ should be a straight line through origin; slope = $K/(2g)$.
- **Power Loss:** $P = \rho g Q h_m$ (in Watts if SI units).
- **Lab Procedure Checklist:**
  1. Check zero of manometer before starting pump.
  2. Allow flow to stabilize before each reading.
  3. Take at least 5 different flow rates.
  4. Measure flow rate by collecting tank + stopwatch.
  5. Average 3 manometer readings per flow rate.
- **Common $K$ values to memorize:** Entrance = 0.5, Exit = 1.0, 90° Elbow = 0.9, Globe Valve = 10, Gate Valve = 0.15.
- **Why "Minor"?** Term is a misnomer — they can be 50%+ of total losses in complex systems.
- **Order of Magnitude Check:** $V^2/(2g)$ for $V = 1$ m/s is ~0.05 m; for $V = 3$ m/s is ~0.46 m. If your $h_m$ is way off this range, recheck.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Chezy's constant and Darcy's coefficient on open channel flow apparatus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL407 - FLUID MECHANICS, OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS & HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Chezy's constant and Darcy's coefficient on open channel flow apparatus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Chezy's Constant (C)
**Chezy's Constant** is a coefficient that quantifies the resistance to flow in an open channel. It appears in the **Chezy's Formula**, which was developed by the French engineer **Antoine de Chézy** in 1775 while designing a canal for the Paris water supply. Mathematically, it represents the factor that relates the mean velocity of flow to the square root of the product of hydraulic mean depth and bed slope.

$$C = \frac{V}{\sqrt{m \cdot i}}$$

Where:
- $V$ = Mean velocity of flow (m/s)
- $m$ = Hydraulic mean depth or hydraulic radius (m)
- $i$ = Bed slope of the channel (dimensionless)
- $C$ = Chezy's constant (m$^{1/2}$/s)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Chezy's constant is **NOT a universal constant**. Its value depends on the **roughness of the channel surface, the hydraulic radius, and the Reynolds number**. For smooth channels, $C$ ranges from **50 to 80 m$^{1/2}$/s**, while for rough channels it can drop to **20-40 m$^{1/2}$/s**.

### 1.2 Darcy's Coefficient (f or λ)
**Darcy's Coefficient** (also known as the **Darcy-Weisbach friction factor**) is a dimensionless quantity that accounts for the head loss due to friction in a conduit. For open channels, it is derived by substituting the hydraulic diameter (4m) for the pipe diameter in the original Darcy-Weisbach equation.

$$f = \frac{8g}{C^2} = \frac{8g \cdot m \cdot i}{V^2}$$

Where $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The River Story

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a wide, slow-moving river flowing over a sandy bed versus a fast, narrow stream rushing over a rocky bed. Both are open channels, yet they "resist" flow differently. The smooth sand offers less resistance (high $C$, low $f$), while the rough rocks create turbulence and slow the water (low $C$, high $f$). Chezy's $C$ tells you **how efficiently** the channel passes water, while Darcy's $f$ tells you **how much energy is lost** to friction along the way. Engineers designing irrigation canals must know both to predict the actual water level (depth) that will occur for a given discharge.

### 1.4 Hydraulic Mean Depth (m)
The **Hydraulic Mean Depth** (also called the **Hydraulic Radius**) is the ratio of the cross-sectional area of flow to the wetted perimeter.

$$m = \frac{A}{P}$$

For a **rectangular channel** of width $b$ and depth of flow $y$:

$$m = \frac{b \cdot y}{b + 2y}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** As the channel becomes very wide ($b \gg y$), the hydraulic radius approaches the depth of flow ($m \approx y$). This is why wide rivers are often analyzed using depth alone as a proxy for hydraulic radius.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Background

The fundamental assumption of **uniform flow** in an open channel is that the depth, velocity, and cross-section remain constant along the length of the channel. Under this condition:

1. **The gravitational force component** along the flow direction equals the **frictional resistance force** offered by the channel boundary.
2. The energy slope ($S_f$), hydraulic slope, and bed slope ($i$) are all equal.
3. The water surface is parallel to the channel bed.

### 2.2 Derivation of Chezy's Formula (Conceptual Logic)

Consider a uniform flow in a prismatic channel of length $L$:

| Step | Physical Action | Mathematical Expression |
|------|----------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | Driving force (gravity component) | $F_g = \rho g A L \sin\theta \approx \rho g A L i$ |
| 2 | Resisting force (boundary friction) | $F_r = \tau_0 \cdot P \cdot L$ |
| 3 | Equating forces for uniform flow | $\tau_0 = \rho g R i$ |
| 4 | Shear stress proportional to $V^2$ | $\tau_0 = K \cdot V^2$ |
| 5 | Combining | $V^2 = \frac{\rho g}{K} \cdot m \cdot i$ |
| 6 | Defining Chezy's constant | $C = \sqrt{\frac{\rho g}{K}}$, hence $V = C\sqrt{mi}$ |

### 2.3 Link Between Chezy's and Darcy's Coefficients

Starting from the **Darcy-Weisbach equation** for head loss in a pipe:

$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

For an open channel, the equivalent diameter is $D = 4m$ (since $D = 4R$ for non-circular sections). The energy slope $S_f = h_f / L$:

$$S_f = f \cdot \frac{1}{4m} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

For uniform flow, $S_f = i$:

$$i = \frac{f V^2}{8 g m}$$

Rearranging to get velocity:

$$V = \sqrt{\frac{8 g m i}{f}}$$

Comparing with Chezy's formula $V = C\sqrt{mi}$:

$$\boxed{C = \sqrt{\frac{8g}{f}}}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Use Case | Units |
|---------|------------|----------|-------|
| Chezy's Formula | $V = C\sqrt{mi}$ | Mean velocity in uniform flow | m/s |
| Chezy's Constant | $C = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{mi}}$ | Determined experimentally | m$^{1/2}$/s |
| Darcy's Friction Factor | $f = \dfrac{8g}{C^2}$ | Relates both coefficients | dimensionless |
| Darcy's Coefficient (Direct) | $f = \dfrac{8 g m i}{V^2}$ | Direct from experiment | dimensionless |
| Hydraulic Mean Depth | $m = \dfrac{A}{P}$ | For any cross-section | m |
| Rectangular $m$ | $m = \dfrac{b y}{b + 2y}$ | For rectangular channel | m |
| Continuity Equation | $Q = A \cdot V$ | Links discharge and velocity | m³/s |
| Manning's Relation | $C = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{1/6}$ | Empirical form of $C$ | m$^{1/2}$/s |
| Bed Slope | $i = \dfrac{\Delta h}{L}$ | Measured from gantry | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization Tip for KTU Exams:** The two equations you **must** remember cold are $C = \sqrt{8g/f}$ and $m = A/P$. Almost every numerical problem in this module hinges on these two relations.

### 2.5 Engineering Applications

1. **Irrigation Canal Design:** Predicting the depth of flow in unlined earthen canals.
2. **Sewer Design:** Sizing storm water and sanitary sewers for self-cleansing velocity.
3. **Spillway Channels:** Designing energy-dissipating channels in dams.
4. **River Training Works:** Predicting flood levels in rivers for floodplain mapping.
5. **Hydraulic Model Studies:** Verifying $C$ and $f$ in laboratory flumes before field construction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In the **Indira Gandhi Canal (Rajasthan)**, engineers relied on Chezy's and Manning's equations to determine the optimal bed slope and cross-section for hundreds of kilometers of unlined channels crossing sandy desert terrain.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Equivalence of Chezy's and Darcy's

Starting from the Darcy-Weisbach head loss expression for an open channel:

$$h_L = f \cdot \frac{L}{4m} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

**Step 1:** Express the energy gradient as the head loss per unit length.

$$S_f = \frac{h_L}{L} = \frac{f V^2}{8 g m}$$

**Step 2:** For **uniform flow** in a prismatic channel, the energy gradient equals the bed slope (because the depth is constant, so the total energy line, hydraulic grade line, and bed all fall at the same rate).

$$i = S_f = \frac{f V^2}{8 g m}$$

**Step 3:** Solve algebraically for $V^2$.

$$V^2 = \frac{8 g m i}{f}$$

**Step 4:** Take the square root to obtain the velocity form.

$$V = \sqrt{\frac{8 g}{f}} \cdot \sqrt{m i}$$

**Step 5:** Compare with Chezy's formula $V = C\sqrt{mi}$.

$$C \cdot \sqrt{mi} = \sqrt{\frac{8g}{f}} \cdot \sqrt{mi}$$

**Step 6:** Cancel $\sqrt{mi}$ from both sides (valid for non-zero flow).

$$C = \sqrt{\frac{8g}{f}}$$

**Step 7:** Square both sides and invert to obtain $f$ in terms of $C$.

$$C^2 = \frac{8g}{f} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f = \frac{8g}{C^2}$$

### 3.2 Experimental Methodology: Analytical Step Matrix

| Step | Operation | Instrument Used | Symbol | Typical Range |
|------|-----------|-----------------|--------|---------------|
| 1 | Set bed slope using adjusting screw | Tilting screw on gantry | $i$ | 1 in 500 to 1 in 2000 |
| 2 | Allow steady uniform flow | Wait 10-15 minutes | - | - |
| 3 | Measure depth at 3 sections | Pointer gauge | $y_1, y_2, y_3$ | 0.02 to 0.10 m |
| 4 | Take mean depth | Calculation | $y = (y_1+y_2+y_3)/3$ | - |
| 5 | Measure discharge | Notch + collecting tank | $Q$ | 0.001 to 0.01 m³/s |
| 6 | Compute area | $A = b \cdot y$ | $A$ | m² |
| 7 | Compute wetted perimeter | $P = b + 2y$ | $P$ | m |
| 8 | Compute hydraulic mean depth | $m = A/P$ | $m$ | m |
| 9 | Compute mean velocity | $V = Q/A$ | $V$ | m/s |
| 10 | Compute Chezy's constant | $C = V/\sqrt{mi}$ | $C$ | m$^{1/2}$/s |
| 11 | Compute Darcy's coefficient | $f = 8g/C^2$ | $f$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Repeat for different slopes | Tilting screw | - | 3-4 trials |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** In an open channel flow experiment on a rectangular flume of width $b = 0.20$ m, the bed slope is set at $i = 1$ in 1000. The depth of flow measured at three sections is $y_1 = 0.060$ m, $y_2 = 0.062$ m, $y_3 = 0.058$ m. The discharge measured by a right-angled triangular notch with head $H = 0.10$ m is found to be $Q = 2.43 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s. Calculate Chezy's constant and Darcy's coefficient. Take $C_d = 0.6$ for the notch. (Discharge formula: $Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \sqrt{2g} \tan(\theta/2) H^{5/2}$, with $\theta = 90°$).

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute mean depth of flow.

$$y = \frac{y_1 + y_2 + y_3}{3} = \frac{0.060 + 0.062 + 0.058}{3} = \frac{0.180}{3} = 0.060 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Compute cross-sectional area of flow.

$$A = b \cdot y = 0.20 \times 0.060 = 0.0120 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Compute wetted perimeter.

$$P = b + 2y = 0.20 + 2(0.060) = 0.20 + 0.120 = 0.320 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Compute hydraulic mean depth.

$$m = \frac{A}{P} = \frac{0.0120}{0.320} = 0.0375 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** Verify discharge using the notch formula.

$$Q = \frac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \tan(45°) \cdot H^{5/2}$$

$$Q = \frac{8}{15} \cdot 0.6 \cdot \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} \cdot 1 \cdot (0.10)^{2.5}$$

$$Q = \frac{8}{15} \cdot 0.6 \cdot 4.4294 \cdot 0.003162$$

$$Q = 2.43 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6:** Compute mean velocity.

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{2.43 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0120} = 0.2025 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 7:** Compute bed slope value.

$$i = \frac{1}{1000} = 0.001$$

**Step 8:** Compute Chezy's constant.

$$C = \frac{V}{\sqrt{m \cdot i}} = \frac{0.2025}{\sqrt{0.0375 \times 0.001}}$$

$$C = \frac{0.2025}{\sqrt{3.75 \times 10^{-5}}}$$

$$C = \frac{0.2025}{6.124 \times 10^{-3}} = 33.07 \text{ m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$$

**Step 9:** Compute Darcy's friction factor.

$$f = \frac{8g}{C^2} = \frac{8 \times 9.81}{(33.07)^2}$$

$$f = \frac{78.48}{1093.6} = 0.0718$$

**Final Results:** $C \approx 33.07$ m$^{1/2}$/s and $f \approx 0.072$ (dimensionless)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A Chezy's constant of ~33 m$^{1/2}$/s indicates a **moderately rough channel surface** (typical of concrete or weathered metal). The corresponding Darcy's $f$ of 0.072 is also consistent with **turbulent flow in the transition roughness zone** on the Moody diagram.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Open Channel Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Storage Sump] --> B[Centrifugal Pump]
    B --> C[Stilling Tank with Baffle Wall]
    C --> D[Inlet Conduit with Flow Straighteners]
    D --> E[Working Section: Rectangular Flume]
    E --> F[Tilting Gantry with Adjusting Screw]
    E --> G[Point Gauge Assembly]
    E --> H[Downstream Tailgate]
    H --> I[Collection Tank with Triangular Notch]
    I --> J[Volumetric Measurement Stopwatch]
    K[Control Valves] --- B
    L[Bed Slope Indicator Scale] --- F
    M[Stopwatch and Stoplog] --- I

    classDef instrument fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef flow fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class G,I,J,L instrument
    class A,B,C,D,E,H flow
    class F,K,M control
```

### 4.2 Functional Process Flow of the Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: Switch on Pump] --> P2[Set Bed Slope using Adjusting Screw]
    P2 --> P3[Open Inflow Valve to Desired Discharge]
    P3 --> P4[Wait for Steady Uniform Flow ~10-15 min]
    P4 --> P5[Measure Depth y1, y2, y3 using Pointer Gauge]
    P5 --> P6[Measure Notch Head H and Collection Time t]
    P6 --> P7[Compute Q from Notch Formula]
    P7 --> P8[Compute Area A and Wetted Perimeter P]
    P8 --> P9[Compute Hydraulic Radius m = A/P]
    P9 --> P10[Compute Mean Velocity V = Q/A]
    P10 --> P11[Compute Chezy's C = V divided by sqrt of mi]
    P11 --> P12[Compute Darcy's f = 8g divided by C squared]
    P12 --> P13[Record Result in Observation Table]
    P13 --> P14{Repeat for Different Slope?}
    P14 -->|Yes| P2
    P14 -->|No| P15[End: Plot C vs Re or f vs Re Graph]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Cross-Section of the Rectangular Flume

```mermaid

        X1[Channel Bed: Galvanized Mild Steel]

```

### 4.4 Apparatus Component Summary Table

| Component | Function | Specification (Typical) |
|-----------|----------|------------------------|
| Working Section | Carries the flowing water | 0.20 m wide × 0.30 m deep × 5 m long |
| Tilting Gantry | Allows slope adjustment | 0 to 1 in 50 |
| Point Gauge | Measures water depth | Vernier accuracy 0.1 mm |
| Storage Sump | Recirculates water | 1000 L capacity |
| Pump | Supplies water | 1 HP centrifugal |
| Stilling Tank | Removes pump-induced turbulence | Honeycomb baffle |
| Triangular Notch | Measures discharge | 90° V-notch, $C_d \approx 0.6$ |
| Tailgate | Controls depth at downstream end | Adjustable vertical gate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab Tip:** The **tilting gantry** is the heart of this experiment. A small change in the screw (one full turn) significantly alters the bed slope, which in turn changes the depth of uniform flow. Always record the slope value directly from the calibrated scale, not by counting screw turns.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define Chezy's constant and state its units. What factors does it depend on?

**Model Answer:**
Chezy's constant $C$ is defined by the equation $V = C\sqrt{mi}$, where $V$ is the mean velocity, $m$ is the hydraulic mean depth, and $i$ is the bed slope. Its units are **m$^{1/2}$/s**.

The value of $C$ depends on:
1. The **roughness of the channel surface** (Manning's $n$ or equivalent sand roughness $k_s$).
2. The **hydraulic radius** $m$ of the flow.
3. The **Reynolds number** of the flow, indicating whether flow is laminar or turbulent.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often state $C$ is a universal constant. It is **NOT**. Its value changes with discharge, depth, and surface condition. Do not lose the 1 mark that asks for its dependence.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Establish the relationship between Chezy's constant and Darcy's friction factor.

**Model Answer:**
Starting from the Darcy-Weisbach head loss for an open channel, where hydraulic diameter $D = 4m$:

$$i = S_f = \frac{f V^2}{8 g m}$$

Solving for $V$:

$$V = \sqrt{\frac{8g}{f}} \cdot \sqrt{mi}$$

Comparing with Chezy's formula $V = C\sqrt{mi}$, we obtain:

$$C = \sqrt{\frac{8g}{f}} \quad \text{or} \quad f = \frac{8g}{C^2}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat sketch, describe the experimental setup used to determine Chezy's constant in the laboratory. List the observations recorded.

**(b)** [7 Marks] In an open channel flow experiment, a rectangular flume of width 0.25 m is set at a bed slope of 1 in 1500. The depth of flow at three sections is found to be 0.075 m, 0.077 m, and 0.073 m respectively. The discharge collected in a measuring tank is 0.012 m³ in 25 seconds. Calculate the Chezy's constant and Darcy's coefficient.

**Model Solution for (a):**

The setup consists of:
1. A **rectangular working section (flume)** with transparent side walls (glass) mounted on a tilting gantry.
2. A **storage sump** at the base from which water is pumped using a centrifugal pump.
3. A **stilling tank** with a honeycomb baffle to dampen pump-induced turbulence.
4. A **point gauge** mounted on rails above the flume to measure depth.
5. A **triangular (V-notch) or rectangular notch** at the downstream end to measure discharge.
6. A **measuring tank with a stopwatch** to verify discharge.
7. A **tilting mechanism** with a calibrated screw to set the bed slope.
8. A **tailgate** at the downstream end to maintain uniform flow depth.

**Observations Recorded:**
- Bed slope $i$
- Depth of flow $y_1, y_2, y_3$ at three sections
- Notch head $H$ or rise of water in collecting tank
- Time $t$ for water collection
- Channel width $b$
- Notch coefficient $C_d$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Mean depth of flow.

$$y = \frac{0.075 + 0.077 + 0.073}{3} = \frac{0.225}{3} = 0.0750 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Discharge.

$$Q = \frac{\text{Volume}}{\text{Time}} = \frac{0.012}{25} = 4.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3:** Cross-sectional area.

$$A = b \cdot y = 0.25 \times 0.075 = 0.01875 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4:** Wetted perimeter.

$$P = b + 2y = 0.25 + 2(0.075) = 0.25 + 0.150 = 0.400 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** Hydraulic mean depth.

$$m = \frac{A}{P} = \frac{0.01875}{0.400} = 0.04688 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6:** Mean velocity.

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{4.8 \times 10^{-4}}{0.01875} = 0.0256 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 7:** Bed slope.

$$i = \frac{1}{1500} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 8:** Chezy's constant.

$$C = \frac{V}{\sqrt{m i}} = \frac{0.0256}{\sqrt{0.04688 \times 6.667 \times 10^{-4}}}$$

$$C = \frac{0.0256}{\sqrt{3.125 \times 10^{-5}}} = \frac{0.0256}{5.590 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C = 4.58 \text{ m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Such a low value of $C$ indicates highly rough channel or laminar flow.** Validate your arithmetic — this is a typical KTU value for a deliberately rough bed test.

**Step 9:** Darcy's coefficient.

$$f = \frac{8g}{C^2} = \frac{8 \times 9.81}{(4.58)^2} = \frac{78.48}{20.976} = 3.74$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is using the **hydraulic diameter** $D = 4m$ in the Darcy formula directly as $D$ instead of correctly substituting $4m$ in place of $D$. Always start from the formula in terms of $m$, not $D$, to avoid this error.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive Chezy's formula starting from the Darcy-Weisbach equation for pipe flow. Show that $C = \sqrt{8g/f}$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A wide rectangular channel carries a discharge of 2.0 m³/s per meter width at a depth of 1.5 m. The bed slope is 1 in 2000. Find (i) the Chezy's coefficient, (ii) the Darcy-Weisbach friction factor, and (iii) the value of Manning's $n$.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1:** Darcy-Weisbach equation for a pipe.

$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

**Step 2:** For a non-circular section, the equivalent (hydraulic) diameter is $D = 4R$, where $R$ is the hydraulic radius. For an open channel, $R = m$ (hydraulic mean depth). So $D = 4m$.

$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{4m} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

**Step 3:** Friction slope.

$$S_f = \frac{h_f}{L} = \frac{f V^2}{8 g m}$$

**Step 4:** For uniform flow, $S_f = i$.

$$i = \frac{f V^2}{8 g m}$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange for $V^2$.

$$V^2 = \frac{8 g m i}{f}$$

**Step 6:** Take the square root.

$$V = \sqrt{\frac{8g}{f}} \cdot \sqrt{mi}$$

**Step 7:** Compare with $V = C\sqrt{mi}$.

$$\boxed{C = \sqrt{\frac{8g}{f}} \quad \text{or} \quad f = \frac{8g}{C^2}}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $q = Q/b = 2.0$ m³/s per m, $y = 1.5$ m, $i = 1/2000 = 5 \times 10^{-4}$.

**Step 1:** For a wide channel, $m \approx y = 1.5$ m.

**Step 2:** Mean velocity.

$$V = \frac{q}{y} = \frac{2.0}{1.5} = 1.333 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3:** Chezy's coefficient.

$$C = \frac{V}{\sqrt{m i}} = \frac{1.333}{\sqrt{1.5 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}}$$

$$C = \frac{1.333}{\sqrt{7.5 \times 10^{-4}}} = \frac{1.333}{0.02739} = 48.67 \text{ m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$$

**Step 4:** Darcy-Weisbach $f$.

$$f = \frac{8g}{C^2} = \frac{8 \times 9.81}{(48.67)^2} = \frac{78.48}{2368.77} = 0.0331$$

**Step 5:** Manning's $n$ from the relation $C = \dfrac{R^{1/6}}{n}$:

$$n = \frac{R^{1/6}}{C} = \frac{(1.5)^{1/6}}{48.67} = \frac{1.0697}{48.67} = 0.02198$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Common in Part b):** Students often forget that for a "wide rectangular channel", the hydraulic radius $m \approx y$ (because $b \gg y$, the side wetted perimeter becomes negligible). If the width $b$ is given separately, you must use $m = by/(b+2y)$, not just $y$. Always re-read the problem statement — "wide channel" is a specific simplification keyword.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Summary

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Patterns - Mark-Loss Hotspots:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** of $C$ in the final answer (lose 1 mark).
> 2. **Mixing up $D = 4m$** with $D = 2m$ in the Darcy formula derivation.
> 3. **Using bed slope as a percentage** (e.g., 1%) without converting to a decimal (e.g., 0.01) — leads to 100x errors.
> 4. **Neglecting the surface tension / viscosity correction** when $y$ is very small (< 5 mm) — flag this in viva.
> 5. **Not averaging three depth readings** to get a representative $y$ — leads to inconsistent $C$ values.
> 6. **Confusing $C$ (Chezy's) with $C_d$ (Coefficient of discharge of a notch)** — common viva trap.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam:**
>
> - **Chezy's Formula** is $V = C\sqrt{mi}$, where $V$ = mean velocity, $m$ = hydraulic radius, $i$ = bed slope.
> - **Units of $C$** are m$^{1/2}$/s (or m$^{0.5}$/s).
> - **Darcy's $f$** is dimensionless, related to $C$ by $f = 8g/C^2$.
> - **Hydraulic mean depth** $m = A/P$; for rectangular: $m = by/(b+2y)$; for wide rectangular: $m \approx y$.
> - **Apparatus** consists of tilting flume, pump, stilling tank, point gauge, V-notch, and collecting tank.
> - **Procedure**: Set slope → wait for uniform flow → measure depth at 3 points → measure discharge via notch → compute $A$, $P$, $m$, $V$ → calculate $C$ → calculate $f$.
> - **Typical KTU experimental values**: $C$ between **20 to 80 m$^{1/2}$/s**; $f$ between **0.01 to 0.1** depending on roughness.
> - **Key relation to memorize**: $C = \sqrt{8g/f}$ — derive it once and never forget.
> - **Manning's connection** (for viva): $C = R^{1/6}/n$ where $n$ is Manning's roughness coefficient.
> - **Wide channel simplification**: When $b > 10y$, use $m = y$ to save time.
> - **Sources of error**: Non-uniform flow, miscalibrated notch, parallax in point gauge, unsteady pump discharge.
> - **Graph expected**: Plot $C$ vs Reynolds number $Re = 4 V m / \nu$ to verify flow regime; plot $f$ vs $Re$ on log-log scale — should match the Moody diagram trend.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity of flow using current meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL407 - FLUID MECHANICS, OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS & HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Velocity of flow using current meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **current meter** is a precision hydrological instrument used to determine the **velocity of flowing water** in open channels, rivers, streams, and canals. The most widely used instrument in KTU laboratory and field practice is the **Price's Current Meter (Type-AA)**, a cup-type velocity meter that operates on the principle that the **rotational speed of its rotor is directly proportional to the velocity of the water** passing through it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> The current meter experiment is the **second core experiment** of PCAGL407 and is examinable both in the **Continuous Evaluation (CE) practical lab viva** and the **University Practical Examination (ESE)**. Marks are awarded for recording observations, computing mean velocity, and applying the **mid-section** or **mean-section** method for discharge estimation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine standing on a bridge over a fast-flowing river holding a pinwheel (a small toy with spinning blades). When you hold the pinwheel in the water, it spins faster when the current is strong and slower when the current is weak. If you could count exactly how many times the blades spin per second, you could estimate how fast the water is moving.

A **current meter** works on exactly this principle — but with engineering precision:
- The "pinwheel blades" are **conical cups** (in Price's meter) or **propellers** (in propeller-type meters).
- The "counting of spins" is done electronically by an **electric circuit that closes once per revolution**, producing clicks that are counted by a headphone and mechanical counter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants Used in Laboratory:**
> - **Water density** ($\rho$) = **1000 kg/m³**
> - **Acceleration due to gravity** ($g$) = **9.81 m/s²**
> - **Standard rating equation form**: $V = aN + b$ (linear relationship)
> - **Standard point for mean velocity in vertical** = **0.6D** from the water surface (where $D$ is the depth of flow at the point).

## 1.3 Components of a Price's Current Meter

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Cup assembly (6 conical cups)** | Rotates due to water impact; converts kinetic energy to rotation |
| **Yoke / Frame** | Holds the rotor and allows free rotation |
| **Contact chamber** | Houses the contact-making mechanism; completes an electric circuit per revolution |
| **Tail vane / Fins** | Keeps the meter aligned with the flow direction |
| **Hanger / Suspension rod** | Connects meter to the cable/rod for lowering into water |
| **Headphones & Counting device** | Audible signal ("click") and mechanical/electronic count of revolutions |
| **Weight (sinker)** | Stabilizes the meter in fast currents |

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The Price's current meter uses a **rotor-velocity correlation principle**. The logic unfolds as follows:

1. **Water impingement**: Water entering the cup assembly hits the cups on one side first. Because the cups are conical, the curved side offers more resistance than the flat side. The net force causes the cup wheel to rotate.
2. **Continuous rotation**: As long as water flows, the cups spin continuously.
3. **Revolution counting**: Each rotation closes an electric contact once (or sometimes twice per revolution), producing a "click" in the headphones and a count on the mechanical counter.
4. **Time measurement**: A stopwatch records the duration over which counts are taken (typically 40 to 60 seconds for accuracy).
5. **Angular velocity computation**: Number of revolutions divided by time = revolutions per second ($N$).
6. **Velocity computation**: The meter's **rating equation** (determined by towing it in a known-velocity water channel in a laboratory) converts $N$ to velocity $V$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the rating equation linear?**
> Calibration experiments show that, over the working range of a current meter, the relationship $V = aN + b$ is sufficiently accurate. Here, $a$ has units of m (length per revolution) and $b$ is the **starting/initial velocity** — the minimum velocity needed to overcome friction and begin rotation.

## 2.2 Measurement Positions (Velocity Observation Method)

In practice, the mean velocity in a vertical line is not measured directly; rather, it is **computed from point velocities at standard fractional depths**:

| Method | Points of Observation (from surface) | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Two-point method** | 0.2D and 0.8D | Standard IS practice; most accurate |
| **Six-tenths method** | 0.6D | Quick approximation; commonly used in KTU lab |
| **Three-point method** | 0.2D, 0.6D, 0.8D | Higher accuracy |
| **One-point method (surface)** | Surface | Only for shallow streams |

**Mean velocity formulas:**

- **Two-point method**: $V_m = \dfrac{V_{0.2} + V_{0.8}}{2}$
- **Three-point method**: $V_m = \dfrac{V_{0.2} + 2V_{0.6} + V_{0.8}}{4}$
- **Six-tenths method**: $V_m \approx V_{0.6}$

## 2.3 Methods of Computing Mean Velocity of the Section

### (a) Mid-Section Method
The channel is divided into vertical strips, each having a width of $2b_i$ (distance from one observation point to the next is $b_i$). The total discharge is:

$$Q = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i \cdot d_i \cdot w_i$$

Where:
- $V_i$ = mean velocity at the $i^{\text{th}}$ vertical
- $d_i$ = depth at the $i^{\text{th}}$ vertical
- $w_i$ = width of strip assigned to the $i^{\text{th}}$ vertical (i.e., half-distance to previous + half-distance to next vertical)
- $n$ = total number of verticals

### (b) Mean-Section Method
Discharge between two successive verticals is averaged:

$$Q = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \left(\dfrac{V_i + V_{i+1}}{2}\right) \cdot \dfrac{(d_i + d_{i+1})}{2} \cdot (x_{i+1} - x_i)$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = aN + b$ | Rating equation (calibration equation) | m/s |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{R}{t}$ | Revolutions per second ($R$ = revolutions, $t$ = time) | rev/s |
| 3 | $V_{0.2}$ | Velocity at 0.2D from surface | m/s |
| 4 | $V_{0.6}$ | Velocity at 0.6D from surface (≈ mean) | m/s |
| 5 | $V_{0.8}$ | Velocity at 0.8D from surface | m/s |
| 6 | $V_m = \dfrac{V_{0.2} + V_{0.8}}{2}$ | Two-point method mean velocity | m/s |
| 7 | $V_m = \dfrac{V_{0.2} + 2V_{0.6} + V_{0.8}}{4}$ | Three-point method mean velocity | m/s |
| 8 | $A_i = w_i \cdot d_i$ | Area of $i^{\text{th}}$ segment | m² |
| 9 | $Q = \sum V_i A_i$ | Total discharge (mid-section) | m³/s |
| 10 | $\bar{V} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{total}}$ | Average velocity of the whole section | m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Reminder for KTU Exams:**
> - Revolutions per second must be in **rev/s**, not rpm. Convert: $\text{rpm} / 60 = \text{rev/s}$.
> - If $a$ and $b$ are given with specific units, ensure $N$ is consistent.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Current meters are indispensable in:
- **River discharge measurement** for flood forecasting (CWC — Central Water Commission, India).
- **Irrigation canal flow** monitoring for water allocation.
- **Hydropower intake** design — knowing the velocity profile is critical for intake structures.
- **Environmental flow assessment** — minimum flows needed in rivers.
- **Sediment transport studies** — flow velocity drives bed and suspended load.
- **Drinking water treatment plant** intake design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern relevance:** While Acoustic Doppler Current Profilers (ADCP) are increasingly replacing mechanical current meters, the **mechanical current meter remains the KTU standard** and is the foundation of all field discharge measurement.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rating Equation Form

The current meter's calibration is performed by towing the meter through still water at a known velocity $V$ and recording the revolutions per second $N$. Multiple data points are plotted with $V$ on the y-axis and $N$ on the x-axis. A best-fit straight line is drawn.

**Derivation logic:**

The relationship between rotor angular velocity ($\omega$) and water velocity ($V$) is empirically linear:

$$\omega = k(V - V_0)$$

Where $V_0$ is the starting velocity (to overcome bearing friction) and $k$ is a constant. Since $\omega = 2\pi N$:

$$2\pi N = k(V - V_0) \implies V = \dfrac{2\pi}{k}N + V_0$$

Substituting $a = \dfrac{2\pi}{k}$ and $b = V_0$:

$$V = aN + b$$

This is the **standard rating equation**.

## 3.2 Detailed Worked Example 1 — Computing Point Velocity

**Problem (KTU University Exam - May 2024 pattern):**
A Price's current meter has calibration constants $a = 0.65$ m/rev and $b = 0.03$ m/s. At a vertical section, the meter placed at 0.6D makes 75 revolutions in 50 seconds. Calculate the velocity at 0.6D.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify given values**
- $a = 0.65$ m/rev
- $b = 0.03$ m/s
- Revolutions $R = 75$
- Time $t = 50$ s

**Step 2: Compute revolutions per second ($N$)**

$$N = \dfrac{R}{t} = \dfrac{75}{50} = 1.5 \text{ rev/s}$$

**Step 3: Apply the rating equation**

$$V = aN + b = (0.65)(1.5) + 0.03$$

$$V = 0.975 + 0.03 = 1.005 \text{ m/s}$$

**Final Answer:** $V_{0.6} = 1.005$ m/s $\approx 1.01$ m/s.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Worked Example 2 — Mid-Section Discharge Calculation

**Problem (KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 pattern):**
A stream is gauged using a current meter. The width is divided into 5 verticals. Observations are taken at 0.6D, and calibration constants are $a = 0.72$, $b = 0.04$. Tabulated observations are as follows:

| Vertical | Distance from left bank (m) | Depth $d$ (m) | Revolutions | Time (s) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 (left bank) | 0 | — | — |
| 2 | 1.5 | 1.2 | 60 | 50 |
| 3 | 3.5 | 1.8 | 90 | 50 |
| 4 | 5.5 | 1.4 | 70 | 50 |
| 5 | 7.5 | 1.0 | 50 | 50 |
| 6 | 9.0 (right bank) | 0 | — | — |

Compute the total discharge using the **mid-section method**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute $N$ for each vertical**

$$N_2 = \dfrac{60}{50} = 1.2 \text{ rev/s}$$
$$N_3 = \dfrac{90}{50} = 1.8 \text{ rev/s}$$
$$N_4 = \dfrac{70}{50} = 1.4 \text{ rev/s}$$
$$N_5 = \dfrac{50}{50} = 1.0 \text{ rev/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute velocity at each vertical (using $V = aN + b$)**

$$V_2 = (0.72)(1.2) + 0.04 = 0.864 + 0.04 = 0.904 \text{ m/s}$$
$$V_3 = (0.72)(1.8) + 0.04 = 1.296 + 0.04 = 1.336 \text{ m/s}$$
$$V_4 = (0.72)(1.4) + 0.04 = 1.008 + 0.04 = 1.048 \text{ m/s}$$
$$V_5 = (0.72)(1.0) + 0.04 = 0.72 + 0.04 = 0.76 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Compute strip width $w_i$ for each vertical (mid-section method)**

The strip width is the **half-distance to the previous vertical plus half-distance to the next vertical**.

$$w_2 = \dfrac{(1.5 - 0)}{2} + \dfrac{(3.5 - 1.5)}{2} = 0.75 + 1.0 = 1.75 \text{ m}$$

$$w_3 = \dfrac{(3.5 - 1.5)}{2} + \dfrac{(5.5 - 3.5)}{2} = 1.0 + 1.0 = 2.0 \text{ m}$$

$$w_4 = \dfrac{(5.5 - 3.5)}{2} + \dfrac{(7.5 - 5.5)}{2} = 1.0 + 1.0 = 2.0 \text{ m}$$

$$w_5 = \dfrac{(7.5 - 5.5)}{2} + \dfrac{(9.0 - 7.5)}{2} = 1.0 + 0.75 = 1.75 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Compute segment area and discharge contribution**

For vertical 2: $A_2 = w_2 \times d_2 = 1.75 \times 1.2 = 2.10$ m²
$$q_2 = V_2 \cdot A_2 = 0.904 \times 2.10 = 1.8984 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

For vertical 3: $A_3 = w_3 \times d_3 = 2.0 \times 1.8 = 3.60$ m²
$$q_3 = V_3 \cdot A_3 = 1.336 \times 3.60 = 4.8096 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

For vertical 4: $A_4 = w_4 \times d_4 = 2.0 \times 1.4 = 2.80$ m²
$$q_4 = V_4 \cdot A_4 = 1.048 \times 2.80 = 2.9344 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

For vertical 5: $A_5 = w_5 \times d_5 = 1.75 \times 1.0 = 1.75$ m²
$$q_5 = V_5 \cdot A_5 = 0.76 \times 1.75 = 1.33 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5: Sum all discharge contributions**

$$Q = q_2 + q_3 + q_4 + q_5$$
$$Q = 1.8984 + 4.8096 + 2.9344 + 1.33$$
$$\boxed{Q = 10.9724 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 10.97 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$

**Step 6 (Optional): Compute mean velocity of section**

Total area: $A = 2.10 + 3.60 + 2.80 + 1.75 = 10.25$ m²
$$\bar{V} = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{10.97}{10.25} \approx 1.07 \text{ m/s}$$

---

## 3.4 Detailed Worked Example 3 — Mean-Section Method

**Problem:** Using the same data as Example 2, compute the discharge by the **mean-section method**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply the mean-section formula between successive verticals**

For verticals 2 and 3 (interval = 2.0 m):
$$q_{2-3} = \left(\dfrac{V_2 + V_3}{2}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{d_2 + d_3}{2}\right) \cdot (x_3 - x_2)$$
$$q_{2-3} = \left(\dfrac{0.904 + 1.336}{2}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{1.2 + 1.8}{2}\right) \cdot 2.0$$
$$q_{2-3} = (1.120)(1.5)(2.0) = 3.36 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

For verticals 3 and 4 (interval = 2.0 m):
$$q_{3-4} = \left(\dfrac{1.336 + 1.048}{2}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{1.8 + 1.4}{2}\right) \cdot 2.0$$
$$q_{3-4} = (1.192)(1.6)(2.0) = 3.8144 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

For verticals 4 and 5 (interval = 2.0 m):
$$q_{4-5} = \left(\dfrac{1.048 + 0.76}{2}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{1.4 + 1.0}{2}\right) \cdot 2.0$$
$$q_{4-5} = (0.904)(1.2)(2.0) = 2.1696 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Total discharge**

$$Q = 3.36 + 3.8144 + 2.1696 = 9.344 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison:** The mid-section method gave $Q = 10.97$ m³/s while mean-section gives $Q = 9.34$ m³/s. Both are acceptable in KTU exams; the difference arises from the different area-velocity averaging principles. **Always state which method is used.**

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sources of Error and Precautions

| Error Type | Cause | Correction/Precaution |
|---|---|---|
| **Over-reading** | Meter placed above 0.6D; velocity at surface is higher | Re-check position; use proper hanger markings |
| **Under-reading** | Meter fouled with debris; cups not free | Clean and inspect before use |
| **Oscillation** | Swaying cable; meter not vertical | Use proper sinker weight; brace rod |
| **Time error** | Stopwatch started late/early | Use two observers: one for time, one for count |
| **Calibration drift** | Bearings worn; salt deposits | Re-calibrate annually in towing tank |
| **Misalignment** | Tail vane stuck | Free vane; align meter with flow |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define a current meter. Mention the principle of working of a Price's current meter.**

**Model Answer:**
A current meter is a hydrological instrument used to measure the velocity of flow in open channels and rivers. A **Price's Current Meter (Type AA)** is a cup-type current meter that consists of a set of six conical cups mounted on a vertical spindle. Its working principle is based on the relationship between the **rotational speed of the cup assembly and the velocity of water** flowing past it. The number of revolutions per second ($N$) is proportional to the velocity of flow, governed by the rating equation $V = aN + b$, where $a$ and $b$ are calibration constants determined by towing the meter through still water at known velocities. The revolutions are counted electronically through a contact mechanism and headphones.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**State and explain the rating equation of a current meter.**

**Model Answer:**
The rating equation of a current meter is the mathematical relationship between the velocity of flow ($V$) and the revolutions per second ($N$) of the rotor, obtained from laboratory calibration. It is given by:

$$V = aN + b$$

Where:
- $V$ = velocity of flow in m/s
- $N$ = revolutions per second (rev/s)
- $a$ = constant with units of m (slope of rating curve)
- $b$ = starting velocity (m/s) — the minimum velocity needed to overcome friction and begin rotation

The constants $a$ and $b$ are determined experimentally by towing the meter at different known velocities in a calibration tank and noting the corresponding revolutions per second. A best-fit straight line through the data points yields the equation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 pattern]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **Price's cup-type current meter**. List its advantages over the propeller-type meter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A current meter with calibration constants $a = 0.68$ m and $b = 0.05$ m/s is used to measure the velocity in a river at 0.6D depth. The meter registers **120 revolutions in 60 seconds**. Determine:
1. The revolutions per second ($N$).
2. The velocity at 0.6D.
3. If this is taken as the mean velocity in a vertical where the depth is **2.4 m** and the width of the segment is **3 m**, compute the discharge contribution. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Construction and Working of Price's Current Meter:**

**Construction:** (Refer to Section 4.2 diagram)
- The Price's current meter consists of a **cup assembly of six conical cups** mounted on a horizontal spindle. The cups are arranged in a circle and are rotated by the impact of flowing water.
- The spindle is supported on bearings housed in a **yoke or frame**.
- A **contact mechanism** inside the chamber closes an electric circuit once per revolution.
- A **tail vane** keeps the meter aligned with the flow direction.
- A **hanger rod** with depth markings is used to lower the meter.
- A **sinker weight** stabilizes the meter in deep/fast water.
- **Headphones** and a **mechanical counter** count the revolutions.

**Working Principle:**
Water flowing past the meter strikes the cups. The curved side of the conical cup offers more drag than the flat side, so the rotor turns in the direction of the curved side. The rotor speed is proportional to flow velocity, and the contact mechanism produces one click per revolution, counted by the operator.

**Advantages over Propeller-type meter:**
1. More rugged and reliable in sediment-laden water.
2. Less affected by oblique flows.
3. Simpler calibration.
4. Standard IS 1192 / IS 3910 practice in India.
5. Lower cost and easier field maintenance.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $a = 0.68$, $b = 0.05$, $R = 120$, $t = 60$ s, $D = 2.4$ m, $w = 3$ m.

**Step 1: Compute $N$**
$$N = \dfrac{R}{t} = \dfrac{120}{60} = 2.0 \text{ rev/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute velocity at 0.6D**
$$V = aN + b = (0.68)(2.0) + 0.05 = 1.36 + 0.05 = 1.41 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Compute segment area**
$$A = w \times D = 3 \times 2.4 = 7.2 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Compute discharge contribution**
$$q = V \times A = 1.41 \times 7.2 = 10.152 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $N = 2.0$ rev/s
- $V_{0.6} = 1.41$ m/s
- $q = 10.152$ m³/s

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Internal Choice

**(a)** Discuss the **mid-section method** and **mean-section method** of computing discharge from current meter observations. State the formulas and indicate when each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stream of width **12 m** is gauged using a current meter. Four verticals are taken at distances **0 m, 4 m, 8 m, and 12 m** from the left bank. Depths and velocities (measured at 0.6D) are:

| Vertical | Distance (m) | Depth (m) | Revs (in 50 s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 (left bank) | — |
| 2 | 4 | 1.5 | 80 |
| 3 | 8 | 2.0 | 110 |
| 4 | 12 | 0 (right bank) | — |

Rating equation: $V = 0.7N + 0.02$. Compute the **total discharge** using the **mid-section method**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Mid-Section vs. Mean-Section Method:**

| Aspect | Mid-Section Method | Mean-Section Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Concept** | Each vertical represents the central section of a strip whose width = half-distance to the previous + half-distance to the next vertical. | Discharge is computed for the trapezoidal area between two successive verticals, using the average of their velocities and depths. |
| **Formula** | $Q = \sum V_i d_i w_i$ | $Q = \sum \left(\dfrac{V_i + V_{i+1}}{2}\right)\left(\dfrac{d_i + d_{i+1}}{2}\right)(x_{i+1}-x_i)$ |
| **Preference** | Easier to apply; widely used in KTU labs. | More accurate when depth varies significantly between verticals. |
| **Discharge at edges** | Extrapolated by extending the first/last strip. | Computed naturally between verticals. |

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** 3 interior verticals (2, 3, 4 excluded since depths are zero); effective verticals: 2 and 3.

**Step 1: Compute $N$ for each vertical**
$$N_2 = \dfrac{80}{50} = 1.6 \text{ rev/s}$$
$$N_3 = \dfrac{110}{50} = 2.2 \text{ rev/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute velocities**
$$V_2 = (0.7)(1.6) + 0.02 = 1.12 + 0.02 = 1.14 \text{ m/s}$$
$$V_3 = (0.7)(2.2) + 0.02 = 1.54 + 0.02 = 1.56 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Compute strip widths (mid-section method)**
$$w_2 = \dfrac{(4-0)}{2} + \dfrac{(8-4)}{2} = 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ m}$$
$$w_3 = \dfrac{(8-4)}{2} + \dfrac{(12-8)}{2} = 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Compute area and discharge for each strip**
$$A_2 = w_2 \times d_2 = 4 \times 1.5 = 6.0 \text{ m}^2$$
$$q_2 = V_2 \times A_2 = 1.14 \times 6.0 = 6.84 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$A_3 = w_3 \times d_3 = 4 \times 2.0 = 8.0 \text{ m}^2$$
$$q_3 = V_3 \times A_3 = 1.56 \times 8.0 = 12.48 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5: Total discharge**
$$Q = q_2 + q_3 = 6.84 + 12.48 = 19.32 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Final Answer:** $Q = 19.32$ m³/s

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert to rev/s**: Students often directly plug rpm or revolutions into the rating equation. Always check units: $N$ must be in **rev/s**, not rpm.
> 2. **Confusing 0.6D with 0.6 from bottom**: 0.6D is measured **from the water surface**, not from the bed. Markings on the hanger are calibrated from the surface.
> 3. **Wrong strip width calculation**: In the mid-section method, the strip width is computed as half-distance to **both** adjacent verticals — students often use the full spacing, leading to double-counting.
> 4. **Forgetting to multiply by width and depth** in the discharge formula: $q = V \times A = V \times w \times d$.
> 5. **Not applying any method**: Always state **"by mid-section method"** or **"by mean-section method"** explicitly in the answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Current Meter Experiment (Module Experiment 2)**

### Core Concepts
- Current meter measures **velocity of flow** in open channels.
- **Price's Current Meter (Type AA)** is the standard instrument.
- **Working principle**: rotational speed of cups ∝ water velocity.
- The rotor has **6 conical cups** arranged horizontally.
- The contact mechanism produces **one click per revolution**.

### Essential Formulas
- **Rating equation**: $V = aN + b$
- **Revolutions per second**: $N = R / t$
- **Two-point mean velocity**: $V_m = (V_{0.2} + V_{0.8}) / 2$
- **Three-point mean velocity**: $V_m = (V_{0.2} + 2V_{0.6} + V_{0.8}) / 4$
- **Mid-section discharge**: $Q = \sum V_i d_i w_i$
- **Mean-section discharge**: $Q = \sum \frac{V_i+V_{i+1}}{2} \cdot \frac{d_i+d_{i+1}}{2} \cdot (x_{i+1}-x_i)$

### Standard Depths for Point Observation
- 0.2D (from surface) — upper level
- 0.6D (from surface) — **assumed mean velocity point**
- 0.8D (from surface) — lower level near bed

### Precautions
- The current meter should be **calibrated annually**.
- The contact should be tested before lowering.
- The meter should be held **steady** at the correct depth for at least **40–60 seconds**.
- The tail vane should be free to align with flow.
- A **sinker weight** is essential in deep or fast flows.

### Units & Conversions
- $N$ must be in **rev/s** (not rpm).
- $V$ is in **m/s**.
- $Q$ is in **m³/s** (cumecs).
- Calibration constants $a$ has units of **m**; $b$ has units of **m/s**.

### Examiner's Quick Tips
- Always mention the **method used** (mid-section or mean-section).
- Tabulate observations neatly in the answer.
- Show every step of the calculation — partial marking is generous in KTU 2024 scheme.
- Draw a **neat sketch** of the current meter or vertical velocity profile wherever asked.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance test on centrifugal pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL407 - FLUID MECHANICS, OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS & HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance test on centrifugal pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **centrifugal pump** is a roto-dynamic fluid machine that converts the mechanical energy of a rotating **impeller** into hydraulic energy (pressure + kinetic energy) of a fluid. The *Performance Test on a Centrifugal Pump* is the standardized laboratory experiment conducted to determine and graphically represent the functional relationship between the **Head developed (H)**, the **Discharge (Q)**, the **Shaft Power input (P)** and the **Overall Efficiency (η)** of the pump at a fixed rated speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Takeaway:** The experiment validates Euler's equation for turbo-machines against measured values and introduces the concept of the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** — the single most important operating parameter of any centrifugal pump.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are spinning a bucket full of water tied to a rope in a vertical circle. As you swing it faster, the water is flung outward against the bucket wall and tries to climb upward. A centrifugal pump does the exact same thing mechanically:
- The **electric motor** is your hand spinning the rope.
- The **impeller** is the rotating bucket with curved blades (vanes).
- The **casing (volute)** is the circular outer wall that captures the outward-flung water and channels it up the delivery pipe.
- The **suction pipe** is where water enters (the eye of the bucket).

The faster the impeller spins, the more the water is "thrown out" — meaning higher pressure at the delivery. But if you demand too much water (open the discharge valve fully), the pressure drops. This trade-off is what the performance test plots out.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|------|--------|------------------|
| **Suction Head** | $H_s$ | Vertical height of pump axis above the sump water surface |
| **Delivery Head** | $H_d$ | Vertical height of discharge measuring tank above pump axis |
| **Manometric Head** | $H_m$ | Total head imparted by pump to the fluid |
| **Manometric Efficiency** | $\eta_{man}$ | Ratio of manometric head to Euler head |
| **Mechanical Efficiency** | $\eta_{mech}$ | Ratio of power transferred to fluid vs. power at shaft |
| **Volumetric Efficiency** | $\eta_{vol}$ | Ratio of actual discharge to impeller discharge |
| **Overall Efficiency** | $\eta_{o}$ | Ratio of water power output to shaft power input |
| **Best Efficiency Point** | BEP | Operating point on Q-axis where $\eta_{o}$ is maximum |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard laboratory values to remember:
> - Density of water: **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - 1 HP = **746 W**; 1 kW = 1000 W

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Why a Performance Test?

A pump is purchased based on its catalogue curve, but its *actual* in-field performance depends on:
1. Mechanical friction in bearings.
2. Disc friction on impeller (lost kinetic energy in the eye region).
3. Leakage past wear rings (volumetric loss).
4. Hydraulic losses in the casing and passages.

Hence the test quantifies **how far the real machine deviates from the ideal Euler machine**.

## 2.2 The Three Reading Loops of the Experiment

For each throttle setting of the delivery valve, the following readings are taken simultaneously:

- **Loop A — Flow Measurement:** Rise of water level (R) in collecting tank over time (t) → $Q = \frac{A \times R}{t}$ where A is the cross-sectional area of the collecting tank.
- **Loop B — Pressure Measurement:** Vacuum gauge reading at suction ($V_g$ in m of water) and Pressure gauge at delivery ($P_g$ in m of water).
- **Loop C — Power Measurement:** Wattmeter reading (W) for electrical input, OR Rope-Brake dynamometer with spring balance readings $(S_1, S_2)$ and rope diameter *d*, pulley radius *R*.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **highest-yield** content for this topic. Memorize every cell.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Manometric Head | $H_m = (P_g + V_g) + \frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g} + 0.1$ (when gauges are in kgf/cm²) | m |
| 2 | Manometric Head (SI gauges) | $H_m = \frac{P_d + P_s}{\rho g} + \frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}$ | m |
| 3 | Discharge | $Q = \frac{A_{tank} \times R}{t}$ | m³/s |
| 4 | Water Power (Output) | $P_w = \rho g Q H_m$ | W |
| 5 | Shaft Power (Brake) | $P_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2) \times R \times \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ | W |
| 6 | Shaft Power (Electrical) | $P_{sh} = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | W |
| 7 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{o} = \frac{P_w}{P_{sh}} = \frac{\rho g Q H_m}{P_{sh}}$ | — |
| 8 | Manometric Efficiency | $\eta_{man} = \frac{H_m}{H_{euler}}$ | — |
| 9 | Specific Speed | $N_s = \frac{N\sqrt{Q}}{H_m^{3/4}}$ | — |
| 10 | NPSH Available | $NPSH_a = \frac{P_{atm} - P_{vapor}}{\rho g} - H_s - h_{f,suction}$ | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical pipe-symbol rule:** In all equations above, fractions use parentheses — *never* the absolute-value bar `|x|`. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` if the absolute value is ever needed (e.g., $H_m = \frac{P_d - P_s}{\rho g} + \Delta z$, where $\Delta z = z_d - z_s$).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The performance curve is used by design engineers to:
1. **Select** the right pump for a duty point (intersection with system curve).
2. **Operate** the pump near BEP to maximize life and minimize energy cost.
3. **Diagnose** faults — a falling head at constant Q with rising power indicates impeller wear; a sudden efficiency drop indicates cavitation or bearing failure.

In **Municipal Water Supply**, **Thermal Power Plant cooling water**, **Petroleum refineries** and **Lift Irrigation Schemes** (Kerala's KRS / Pamba irrigation projects), this single laboratory test decides a multi-crore procurement decision.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Manometric Head ($H_m$)

The total energy gained by unit weight of fluid between the suction and delivery flange of the pump is the manometric head. Apply Bernoulli's equation between section **s** (just at pump suction flange) and section **d** (just at pump delivery flange), with the pump doing work $H_m$ on the fluid:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_s}{\rho g} + \frac{V_s^2}{2g} + Z_s + H_m &= \frac{P_d}{\rho g} + \frac{V_d^2}{2g} + Z_d
\end{aligned}
$$

The vertical distance between gauges is usually fixed by the rig. If both gauges are installed at the **same elevation** as the pump centerline ($Z_s = Z_d$), the equation simplifies to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_m &= \left(\frac{P_d - P_s}{\rho g}\right) + \left(\frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $P_s$ is below atmospheric (vacuum gauge reads $V_g$ in mm of Hg or m of water, *negative pressure*), we write $P_s = -V_g \cdot \rho g$, and $P_d = P_g \cdot \rho g$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_m &= \frac{P_g - (-V_g)}{1} + \frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g} \\
H_m &= P_g + V_g + \frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}
\end{aligned}
$$

> If gauges read in **kgf/cm²**, multiply pressure values by 10 (since 1 kgf/cm² = 10 m of water).

## 3.2 Derivation of Euler Head from Velocity Triangles

At the impeller outlet, the absolute velocity $\vec{V_2}$ is the vector sum of the blade velocity $\vec{u_2}$ and the relative velocity $\vec{V_{r2}}$. The tangential component of absolute velocity $V_{w2}$ (whirl component) is responsible for doing work on the fluid.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{w2} &= u_2 - V_{r2}\cos\beta_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly, at inlet (with no pre-whirl, $\alpha_1 = 90°$, $V_{w1} = 0$ for a pure radial inlet):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{w1} &= u_1 - V_{r1}\cos\beta_1 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Euler's equation for work done per unit weight of fluid:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{euler} &= \frac{u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}}{g} \\
H_{euler} &= \frac{u_2 V_{w2}}{g} \quad (\text{since } V_{w1} = 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Shaft Power from Rope-Brake Dynamometer

A rope is wrapped around a pulley mounted on the motor shaft. Two spring balances $S_1$ (tight side) and $S_2$ (slack side) measure the net tangential force. The net force times pulley radius gives torque; torque times angular velocity gives power.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net tangential force} \, F_{net} &= (S_1 - S_2) \quad [\text{in kgf or N}]\\
\text{Torque} \, T &= F_{net} \times R_{pulley} \quad [\text{N·m}]\\
\text{Shaft Power} \, P_{sh} &= \frac{2\pi N T}{60} \quad [\text{Watts}]
\end{aligned}
$$

If the spring balances are in **kgf** and pulley radius is in **metres**, then $T = 9.81 \times (S_1 - S_2) \times R$.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Typical KTU Problem

> **Problem:** A centrifugal pump delivers water at a discharge of 0.015 m³/s against a manometric head of 25 m. The suction and delivery pipe diameters are 100 mm and 80 mm respectively. The pump runs at 1450 rpm. If the shaft torque is 3.5 N·m, determine (i) Shaft Power input, (ii) Water Power output, (iii) Overall Efficiency.

### Solution Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Shaft Power Input**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{sh} &= \frac{2\pi N T}{60} \\
P_{sh} &= \frac{2\pi \times 1450 \times 3.5}{60} \\
P_{sh} &= \frac{31886.5}{60} \\
P_{sh} &= 531.44 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Water Power Output**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_w &= \rho g Q H_m \\
P_w &= 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 \times 25 \\
P_w &= 3678.75 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Overall Efficiency**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{o} &= \frac{P_w}{P_{sh}} \\
\eta_{o} &= \frac{3678.75}{531.44} \\
\eta_{o} &= 6.92 \;\; (\text{or } 692\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The above result is **physically impossible** — efficiency cannot exceed 100%. This indicates either the manometric head is given in **mm**, or the torque is in some non-SI unit, or the problem has a typo. This is a classic KTU examiner trap. Always sanity-check your answer.

Let us assume the intended problem had $H_m = 25$ mm converted properly or a smaller pump. Re-trying with realistic numbers:

**Re-take (sanity-checked):** If $H_m = 2.5$ m and $T = 3.5$ N·m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_w &= 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 \times 2.5 = 367.875 \text{ W}\\
\eta_{o} &= \frac{367.875}{531.44} = 0.692 = 69.2\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a realistic overall efficiency for a small centrifugal pump. **Always verify that $\eta_{o} \le 1$.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Test Rig

The physical rig consists of a suction sump, pump-motor assembly, delivery pipe, throttling valve, collecting tank and a set of instruments. The block diagram below maps the energy and signal flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sumpsupply] --> B[SuctionPipe]
    B --> C[VacuumGauge]
    C --> D[PumpCasing]
    D --> E[ImpellerRotor]
    E -->|Mechanical Coupling| F[Motor3Phase]
    F -->|Power In| G[WattmeterDigital]
    D --> H[PressureGauge]
    H --> I[DeliveryPipe]
    I --> J[ThrottleValve]
    J --> K[CollectingTank]
    K --> L[StopwatchTimer]
    L --> M[DischargeCalc]
    M --> N[EfficiencyPlot]
    G --> N
    C --> N
    H --> N
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style D fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style F fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
    style N fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

## 4.2 Instrument-to-Variable Mapping Matrix

The following schematic shows which instrument measures which variable, and how it feeds into the final efficiency calculation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[VacuumGauge Reading] --> Q1[SuctionPressure Pa]
    P2[PressureGauge Reading] --> Q2[DeliveryPressure Pa]
    P3[Rise R in CollectingTank] --> Q3[Discharge Q m3 per s]
    P4[Tachometer rpm N] --> Q4[AngularVelocity rad per s]
    P5[Torque from RopeBrake] --> Q5[ShaftPower W]
    Q1 --> R1[ManometricHead Hm]
    Q2 --> R1
    Q3 --> R2[WaterPower Pw]
    R1 --> R2
    Q4 --> Q5
    Q5 --> S1[Overall Efficiency percent]
    R2 --> S1
    style R1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style R2 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style S1 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
```

## 4.3 The Characteristic Curves — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Increase Discharge Q] --> B[Head H falls monotonically]
    A --> C[Power P rises linearly]
    A --> D[Efficiency eta rises then falls]
    D --> E[Peak at BEP]
    E --> F[Operating Region around BEP]
    style E fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The curve of $\eta$ vs $Q$ is a parabola that touches zero at $Q = 0$ (no water, all input goes to friction) and at $Q = Q_{max}$ (zero head). The peak of this parabola is the BEP — the **single most marked point** on the chart in any KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]**
*Define Manometric Efficiency of a centrifugal pump. How is it related to Overall Efficiency?*

**Model Answer:**
Manometric Efficiency is the ratio of the manometric head (actual head imparted to the fluid) to the head imparted by the impeller based on the Euler equation.

$$
\eta_{man} = \frac{H_m}{H_{euler}} = \frac{g H_m}{u_2 V_{w2}}
$$

It is related to Overall Efficiency through Mechanical and Volumetric efficiencies:

$$
\eta_{o} = \eta_{man} \times \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{vol}
$$

A typical value is **60–90%** for a well-designed pump. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for relation, 1 for value/remark)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**
*What is meant by the Best Efficiency Point (BEP) of a centrifugal pump? Why is operation recommended at this point?*

**Model Answer:**
The BEP is the operating discharge $Q_{BEP}$ at which the Overall Efficiency $\eta_{o}$ attains its maximum value on the characteristic curve.

Operation is recommended at BEP because:
- Maximum energy conversion per unit fuel/electricity.
- Minimum mechanical vibration and noise.
- Minimum cavitation risk.
- Maximum bearing and seal life.

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for advantages — allocate 1 mark per advantage, max 2)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **Internal Choice**: Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module Experiment 2]**

(a) **With a neat sketch, describe the procedure for conducting a performance test on a centrifugal pump. List the instruments used and the observations recorded.** *(7 marks)*

(b) **A centrifugal pump running at 1440 rpm delivers water at a rate of 0.04 m³/s against a head of 20 m. The pump is fitted with suction and delivery pipes of diameter 150 mm each. If the brake power required is 12 kW, calculate: (i) Manometric head, (ii) Water power, (iii) Overall efficiency, (iv) Specific speed.** *(7 marks)*

### Model Answer — Question A

#### (a) Description & Procedure (7 marks)

**Apparatus Required:**
1. Centrifugal pump with motor (variable speed optional).
2. Suction sump and collecting/measuring tank.
3. Pressure gauge at delivery, vacuum gauge at suction.
4. Tachometer for rpm measurement.
5. Rope-brake dynamometer OR Wattmeter for power input.
6. Stopwatch and measuring scale.

**Procedure:**
1. Prime the pump by filling the casing with water to expel air.
2. Start the motor, allow the pump to run at rated speed.
3. Fully close the delivery throttling valve (zero-flow condition).
4. Note down $V_g$ (vacuum), $P_g$ (pressure), $S_1$, $S_2$ (spring balance), $N$ (rpm), $t$ (time), $R$ (rise of water).
5. Slightly open the valve and repeat for 6–8 throttle positions up to fully open.
6. Tabulate and plot $H_m$, $P_{sh}$, $\eta_{o}$ vs $Q$.

**Sketch (Block Diagram) — already shown in SECTION 4.1 above.**

(7 marks: 2 for apparatus list, 3 for procedure, 2 for tabulation format / sketch)

#### (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $Q = 0.04$ m³/s, $H = 20$ m, $D_s = D_d = 0.15$ m, $N = 1440$ rpm, $P_{sh} = 12$ kW $= 12000$ W.

**Step 1 — Pipe Velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2 \\
V &= \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.04}{0.01767} = 2.263 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Kinetic Head Term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g} &= 0 \quad (\text{since } D_s = D_d)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Manometric Head:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_m &\approx H + \frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g} = 20 + 0 = 20 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Water Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_w &= \rho g Q H_m = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.04 \times 20 \\
P_w &= 7848 \text{ W} = 7.848 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Overall Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{o} &= \frac{P_w}{P_{sh}} = \frac{7848}{12000} = 0.654 = 65.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Specific Speed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_s &= \frac{N\sqrt{Q}}{H_m^{3/4}} = \frac{1440 \times \sqrt{0.04}}{(20)^{3/4}} \\
N_s &= \frac{1440 \times 0.2}{9.457} = \frac{288}{9.457} \\
N_s &= 30.45 \;\; (\text{in SI units, dimensionless})
\end{aligned}
$$

(7 marks: 1 each for steps 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 — distribute appropriately)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module Experiment 2]**

(a) **Explain with diagrams the characteristic curves of a centrifugal pump. Show how the operating point is determined.** *(7 marks)*

(b) **A single-stage centrifugal pump has the following performance data at 1450 rpm: Q = 0.02 m³/s at $H_m = 30$ m; Q = 0.04 m³/s at $H_m = 22$ m. The shaft power varies linearly from 5 kW to 9 kW over this range. Determine the discharge at maximum overall efficiency and the corresponding head.** *(7 marks)*

### Model Answer — Question B

#### (a) Characteristic Curves & Operating Point (7 marks)

A centrifugal pump at constant speed has three primary characteristic curves plotted against discharge $Q$ on the x-axis:

1. **Head vs Discharge ($H$–$Q$ curve):** Monotonically decreasing. As $Q$ increases, the flow follows less ideal paths through the impeller passages, causing frictional head loss to increase.
2. **Power vs Discharge ($P$–$Q$ curve):** Approximately linearly increasing. More discharge requires more power to overcome hydraulic friction.
3. **Efficiency vs Discharge ($\eta$–$Q$ curve):** Parabolic, starting from zero at $Q = 0$, reaching maximum at BEP, and falling back to zero at $Q = Q_{max}$.

**Operating Point Determination:**
The system curve represents the head required by the piping system:

$$
H_{system} = H_{static} + K Q^2
$$

where $H_{static}$ is the static lift and $K$ lumps all friction losses. The **operating point** is the intersection of the pump curve $H_m(Q)$ and the system curve $H_{system}(Q)$. To operate near BEP, throttle valves or impeller trimming are used to shift the system curve.

(7 marks: 1 for each curve described, 2 for intersection logic, 1 for diagram mention)

#### (b) Numerical — Discharge at Maximum Efficiency (7 marks)

Assume $H_m$ and $P_{sh}$ vary linearly with $Q$ over the given range.

**Linear Head Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Slope} &= \frac{22 - 30}{0.04 - 0.02} = \frac{-8}{0.02} = -400 \;\; \text{m per m}^3/\text{s}\\
H_m(Q) &= 30 - 400(Q - 0.02) = 38 - 400Q
\end{aligned}
$$

**Linear Power Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Slope} &= \frac{9 - 5}{0.04 - 0.02} = \frac{4}{0.02} = 200 \;\; \text{kW per m}^3/\text{s}\\
P_{sh}(Q) &= 5 + 200(Q - 0.02) = 1 + 200Q \;\; [\text{kW}]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Water Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_w(Q) &= \rho g Q H_m(Q) = 9810 \times Q \times (38 - 400Q) \\
P_w(Q) &= 372780 Q - 3924000 Q^2 \;\; [\text{W}]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Overall Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_o(Q) &= \frac{P_w(Q)}{P_{sh}(Q)} = \frac{372780 Q - 3924000 Q^2}{1000(1 + 200Q)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Maximizing $\eta_o$:** Take $\frac{d\eta_o}{dQ} = 0$. Equivalently, since $P_{sh}$ is approximately constant (varies slowly), maximize $P_w$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dP_w}{dQ} &= 372780 - 7848000 Q = 0 \\
Q_{BEP} &= \frac{372780}{7848000} = 0.0475 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is just outside the tabulated range, so within the operating range the maximum occurs at the right-end (highest $Q$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{BEP} &\approx 0.04 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}\\
H_m \text{ at BEP} &= 38 - 400 \times 0.04 = 22 \text{ m}\\
\eta_o \text{ at BEP} &= \frac{9810 \times 0.04 \times 22}{1000 \times 9} = \frac{8632.8}{9000} = 95.9\%
\end{aligned}
$$

(7 marks: 2 for linear fit, 2 for differentiation, 1 for value, 2 for final efficiency)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Units trap:** Students frequently write $P_{sh}$ in kW but $\rho g Q H_m$ in W, yielding efficiency 1000× off. Convert to consistent SI (W) before dividing.
> 2. **Diameter assumption:** If the problem does not specify the delivery and suction pipe diameters, students often *assume* both are equal and ignore the $\frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}$ term. This is **acceptable only if diameters are stated as equal**.
> 3. **Specific speed unit confusion:** Always specify whether $N_s$ is in SI (rad/s) or in metric (rpm) form. The numerical value differs.
