> 4. **Maximum efficiency ≠ Maximum discharge:** Do not confuse the two. Maximum discharge is where head becomes zero; BEP is where the parabola of $\eta$ peaks.
> 5. **Sanity-check $\eta_o \le 100\%$:** If your computed efficiency exceeds 100%, you have made an arithmetic slip or a unit error. Always perform this final check.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition Block:** A centrifugal pump performance test yields the four curves: $H$–$Q$, $P$–$Q$, $\eta$–$Q$, and NPSH–$Q$, plotted at constant rated speed $N$.
- **The Five Standard Readings per throttle setting:** Vacuum gauge ($V_g$), Pressure gauge ($P_g$), Rise ($R$), Time ($t$), Spring balances ($S_1$, $S_2$) — plus Tachometer $N$ once.
- **Three Efficiencies:** Manometric ($\eta_{man}$), Mechanical ($\eta_{mech}$), Volumetric ($\eta_{vol}$) — multiplied give Overall ($\eta_{o}$).
- **Head Equation:** $H_m = P_g + V_g + \frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}$ — never forget the velocity-head difference term when pipe diameters differ.
- **Power Equation:** $P_{sh} = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$ where $T$ is in N·m; if measured by rope-brake with kgf spring balances, $T = 9.81 (S_1 - S_2) R$.
- **Output Equation:** $P_w = \rho g Q H_m$ with all quantities in SI.
- **BEP is King:** Always operate a centrifugal pump as close to its BEP as the system curve permits — this is the golden rule of pump station design.
- **Specific Speed** decides pump type: low $N_s$ → centrifugal; high $N_s$ → axial/mixed flow.
- **NPSH** must exceed required NPSH, else cavitation occurs and impeller pitting starts within hours.
- **Lab Report Tip:** Always include a sample calculation for **one** throttle setting, then tabulate, then plot curves, then mark the BEP on the graph, then discuss the shape of each curve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Line Strategy for KTU Exams:** Always write the *final* answer inside a box, label every curve, mention units in axis titles, and explicitly mark the BEP. Examiners reward systematic presentation even when a small numerical slip occurs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance test on positive displacement pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL407 - FLUID MECHANICS, OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS & HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance test on positive displacement pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Positive Displacement Pump (PDP)** is a hydraulic machine that moves a fixed quantity of fluid per cycle by trapping a finite volume of fluid and then forcing (displacing) it into the discharge pipe. The discharge per cycle is theoretically constant and is independent of the operating pressure head, which is the defining characteristic distinguishing PDPs from centrifugal (rotodynamic) pumps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Check:** A positive displacement pump delivers a definite quantity of fluid per stroke or per revolution, irrespective of the resistance offered by the delivery side, up to the safe working pressure limit of the pump.

The **Performance Test** on a positive displacement pump is a standard laboratory experiment conducted to evaluate the actual operating characteristics of the pump against its theoretical design values, by measuring discharge (Q), pressure head (H), input power, and computing various efficiencies and slip.

### 1.2 Classification (KTU Curriculum Scope)

For the PCAGL407 laboratory, the focus is on **Reciprocating Pumps** (single-acting and double-acting). However, an overview of PDPs includes:

| Sub-Class | Operating Principle | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Reciprocating (Linear)** | Piston / plunger moves in a cylinder | Single-acting, Double-acting pumps |
| **Rotary** | Gears, vanes, screws trap and push fluid | Gear pump, Vane pump, Screw pump |
| **Peristaltic** | Flexible tube squeezed progressively | Peristaltic dosing pump |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reciprocating Pump** is the **prototype PDP** studied in KTU Module Experiment 2 because it offers the cleanest theoretical relationship: *Discharge is purely a function of geometry (Area × Stroke Length × Speed)*.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine a **medical syringe**:
- When you pull the piston back, the syringe fills with exactly the volume of the cylinder (theoretically).
- When you push the piston forward, the same fixed volume is expelled.
- The faster you push (more strokes per minute) or the larger the syringe (bigger cylinder), the more water you deliver.
- Notice that **whether the outlet is open to the air or connected to a tall building does not change the volume per stroke** — only the *force* you need to apply changes.

A reciprocating pump is essentially an **industrial, motorized, water-cooled syringe**. The performance test verifies whether the pump actually delivers the volume its geometry promises, and where the "lost" fluid is going (slip, leakage).

### 1.4 Objectives of the Performance Test

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Manual — Standard Learning Outcomes:**
> 1. Determine the **theoretical discharge (Qₜₕ)** from pump dimensions and speed.
> 2. Measure the **actual discharge (Qₐ𝒸ₜ)** using a measuring tank and stopwatch.
> 3. Compute **Slip**, **Percentage Slip**, and **Coefficient of Discharge (C𝒹)**.
> 4. Calculate **Water Power**, **Pump Input Power** (from dynamometer or wattmeter), and **Overall Efficiency (ηₒᵥₑᵣₐₗₗ)**.
> 5. Plot performance characteristics: Q vs H, Q vs η, Q vs Input Power.

### 1.5 Physical Constants Used Throughout This Module

- Density of water: **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
- Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- 1 HP = **745.7 W** (≈ 746 W)
- 1 kW = **1000 W**
- 1 m³ = **1000 L**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Suction Stroke (Single-Acting Pump)**
- Piston retracts (moves left), increasing cylinder volume.
- Pressure inside cylinder drops below atmospheric pressure.
- Foot valve (suction valve) **opens**; delivery valve remains **closed**.
- Water is pushed up the suction pipe by atmospheric pressure (and from a sump if fitted).

**Step 2 — Delivery Stroke**
- Piston advances (moves right), compressing and displacing water.
- Pressure inside cylinder rises above delivery pipe pressure.
- Delivery valve **opens**; foot valve **closes**.
- Water is forced into the delivery pipe against the system head.

**Step 3 — Repeat**
- The cycle repeats N times per minute.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **single-acting pump**, delivery occurs only on one half of the cycle. In a **double-acting pump**, both sides of the piston deliver water, doubling the discharge for the same geometry and speed.

### 2.2 Governing Theory — Why Q Depends Only on Geometry

The piston sweeps a fixed volume `A × L` per stroke. If it completes N strokes per minute:
- The "theoretical" volume displaced per minute is purely a geometric function.
- This **disconnection between flow and head** is the philosophical heart of PDP behaviour: the pump *will* deliver its design flow, even if the head is doubled — provided the driving mechanism can supply the required torque/force.

### 2.3 What Causes "Slip"?

Slip is the difference between theoretical and actual discharge. It is **not** a fault — it is inherent to physical pumps due to:
- **Delayed valve action** (valves take a finite time to open/close).
- **Leakage** past piston rings, valves, and gaskets.
- **Compressibility** of water (small but nonzero) and dissolved air.
- **Vapor formation** at the suction side if NPSH is poor.

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, slip is usually positive (actual < theoretical). In rare cases with air pockets, slip may appear negative — this is called **negative slip** and is a classic KTU viva question.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Piston (bore) area | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | m² | D = piston diameter |
| 2 | Theoretical discharge (Single-acting) | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | m³/s | L = stroke length (m), N = rpm |
| 3 | Theoretical discharge (Double-acting) | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{2 \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | m³/s | Both faces deliver |
| 4 | Slip | $Q_s = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ | m³/s | Positive = fluid lost |
| 5 | Percentage slip | $\% \text{ slip} = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_{th}} \times 100$ | % | Should be < 10% for healthy pump |
| 6 | Coefficient of discharge | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | dimensionless | Range 0.85 – 0.98 |
| 7 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100$ | % | Same as C𝒹 × 100 |
| 8 | Total head | $H = H_{s} + H_{d} + h_f$ | m | s = suction, d = delivery, f = friction |
| 9 | Water (output) power | $P_{water} = \rho g Q_{act} H$ | W | ρ = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s² |
| 10 | Pump input power | $P_{in} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | W | T = shaft torque (N·m) |
| 11 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{P_{water}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Always < 100% |
| 12 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{P_{water}}{P_{indicated}} \times 100$ | % | Compare with overall |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Domestic water supply** in multi-storey buildings: PDPs are preferred over centrifugal pumps when **constant flow under variable pressure** is needed (e.g., boiler feed).
- **Hydraulic presses and jacks**: PDPs generate the enormous, constant forces required.
- **Oil refineries, chemical dosing, ink transfer**: rotary PDPs deliver precise volumes.
- **Fire-fighting systems**: high-pressure reciprocating pumps deliver water at high, steady heads.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Theoretical Discharge (Single-Acting Pump)

**Step 1 — Volume swept per stroke**

The piston of diameter D moves through a stroke length L.

$$V_{stroke} = A \times L = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times L$$

> *Logic:* The piston sweeps out a cylinder of length L and cross-section A; its volume is Area × Length.

**Step 2 — Strokes per second**

If the pump runs at N revolutions per minute (for a single-acting crank-driven pump, 1 rev = 1 stroke):

$$n_{stroke} = \frac{N}{60} \quad \text{[strokes per second]}$$

> *Logic:* Convert minutes to seconds by dividing by 60.

**Step 3 — Theoretical volume per second (theoretical discharge)**

$$Q_{th} = V_{stroke} \times n_{stroke} = \frac{\pi D^2 L N}{4 \times 60}$$

$$\boxed{\,Q_{th} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}\,}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Theoretical Discharge (Double-Acting Pump)

In a double-acting pump, water is delivered during **both strokes** because the piston rod side also has a working chamber.

Volume per revolution = Stroke volume on piston side + Stroke volume on rod side.

$$V_{rev} = A \cdot L + (A - a) \cdot L = L(2A - a)$$

where `a` is the cross-sectional area of the piston rod. In simplified KTU problems where the rod area is neglected (`a` ≪ `A`):

$$V_{rev} = 2 A L$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,Q_{th} = \frac{2 A L N}{60} \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}\,}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Slip and Coefficient of Discharge

**Given:** measured actual discharge `Q_act` from a collecting tank and stopwatch.

**Step 1 — Slip (definition)**

$$Q_s = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$$

> *Logic:* The "missing" fluid is what leaked, was delayed by valves, or was compressed.

**Step 2 — Percentage slip (normalized form for comparison)**

$$\%\text{ slip} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_{th}} \times 100$$

> *Logic:* Dividing by Qₜₕ makes slip comparable across pumps of different sizes.

**Step 3 — Coefficient of discharge**

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$$

> *Logic:* Ratio of real to ideal — analogous to the Cd in orifice flow.

**Step 4 — Volumetric efficiency**

$$\eta_{vol} = C_d \times 100 \;\%$$

### 3.4 Derivation of Power and Efficiency

**Step 1 — Water Power (useful output power)**

The pump raises `Q_act` of water through a total head H every second. Mass per second = ρ × Q_act. Work done per second = force × distance = (ρ g Q_act H).

$$\boxed{\,P_{water} = \rho \, g \, Q_{act} \, H \;\; \text{Watts}\,}$$

**Step 2 — Input (shaft) Power from dynamometer or torque measurement**

If a rope-brake dynamometer is used with weight W, spring balance reading S, and effective drum radius R, torque is:

$$T = (W - S) \times R \quad \text{[N·m]}$$

$$P_{in} = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} \quad \text{[W]}$$

**Step 3 — Overall Efficiency**

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{water}}{P_{in}} \times 100 \;\%$$

> *Logic:* Of all the energy put in by the motor, how much actually became useful water-raising work?

### 3.5 Worked-Out Numerical (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A single-acting reciprocating pump has a bore of **150 mm** and a stroke of **300 mm**. It runs at **50 rpm** and delivers water against a total head of **20 m**. The actual discharge measured is **4.0 L/s**. The input power measured by a dynamometer is **1.2 kW**. Calculate:
(a) Theoretical discharge, (b) Slip and % slip, (c) Coefficient of discharge, (d) Water power, (e) Overall efficiency.

**Given Data:**
- D = 150 mm = **0.15 m**
- L = 300 mm = **0.30 m**
- N = 50 rpm
- H = 20 m
- Q_act = 4.0 L/s = 0.004 m³/s
- P_in = 1.2 kW = 1200 W
- ρ = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s²

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Theoretical discharge**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.15)^2$$

$$A = \frac{3.1416}{4} \times 0.0225 = 0.01767 \;\; \text{m}^2$$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} = \frac{0.01767 \times 0.30 \times 50}{60}$$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{0.26505}{60} = 0.004418 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{\,Q_{th} \approx 4.418 \;\; \text{L/s}\,}$$

**Part (b) — Slip and Percentage slip**

$$Q_s = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 0.004418 - 0.004 = 0.000418 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\%\text{ slip} = \frac{0.000418}{0.004418} \times 100 = 9.46 \;\%$$

**Part (c) — Coefficient of discharge**

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{0.004}{0.004418} = 0.905$$

**Part (d) — Water power**

$$P_{water} = \rho g Q_{act} H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.004 \times 20$$

$$P_{water} = 784.8 \;\; \text{W} = 0.785 \;\; \text{kW}$$

**Part (e) — Overall efficiency**

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{water}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{784.8}{1200} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\,\eta_{overall} = 65.4 \,\%\,}$$

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Quick Diagnostic Table

| Observation | Inference | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|
| Slip > 15% | Worn piston rings / leaky foot valve | Replace rings, grind valve seat |
| Negative slip | Air entrainment / vapor at suction | Improve NPSH, re-prime pump |
| C𝒹 < 0.80 | Severe mechanical deterioration | Overhaul pump |
| ηₒᵥₑᵣₐₗₗ drops sharply with rising H | Energy lost to friction in delivery pipe | Increase pipe diameter |
| Vibration at high N | Cavitation / misaligned drive | Re-align, check suction conditions |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Reciprocating Pump Test Rig Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUMP[Sump / Storage Tank] --> SP[Suction Pipe]
    SP --> SV[Strain Foot Valve]
    SV --> CYL[Pump Cylinder with Piston]
    CYL --> DV[Delivery Valve]
    DV --> DG[Delivery Pressure Gauge]
    DG --> DT[Delivery Tank on Weighing Scale]
    CYL --> CR[Crank Mechanism]
    CR --> FL[Flywheel]
    FL --> DY[Rope Brake Dynamometer]
    DY --> M[Drive Motor]
    DT --> TS[Stopwatch for Time Measurement]
    M --> WM[Wattmeter or Power Supply]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow — Performance Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: Note pump dimensions D and L] --> P2[Start motor and set N rpm]
    P2 --> P3[Open delivery valve to desired H]
    P3 --> P4[Allow flow to stabilize]
    P4 --> P5[Start stopwatch and divert flow to measuring tank]
    P5 --> P6[Note rise in tank for fixed time t]
    P6 --> P7[Compute Qact = Volume / t]
    P7 --> P8[Read dynamometer load W minus S]
    P8 --> P9[Compute Torque T and Pin]
    P9 --> P10[Calculate Qth Slip Cd and efficiency]
    P10 --> P11[Tabulate and plot characteristics]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Cross-Section of Single-Acting Reciprocating Pump

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CYLINDER[PUMP CYLINDER ASSEMBLY]
        SP1[Suction Pipe with Strainer]
        FV1[Foot Valve NON Return]
        PV1[Piston with Sealing Rings]
        CYL1[Cylinder Body]
        DLV[Delivery Valve NON Return]
        DP1[Delivery Pipe Outlet]
        CR1[Crank Pin and Connecting Rod]
        FL1[Flywheel for Smoothing Torque]
    end
    SP1 --> FV1
    FV1 --> CYL1
    CYL1 --> DLV
    DLV --> DP1
    CR1 --- PV1
    CR1 --- FL1
```

### 4.4 Characteristic Curves Block (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AXIS[X Axis Discharge Q L per s] --> A
    subgraph A[Typical Reciprocating Pump Performance Curves]
        QC[Qth is Vertical Line Constant]
        QA[Qact Slightly Left of Qth]
        HP[Head H is Flat Horizontal Line]
        IP[Input Power Rises Linearly with H]
        EF[Efficiency Curve Peaks at Design Point]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to *sketch* the characteristic curves in the exam, always draw **Qₜₕ as a vertical straight line** (constant, independent of head) and the **actual Q line** slightly to its left, **diverging** as head rises (because slip increases with pressure).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define a positive displacement pump. How does it differ from a centrifugal pump in terms of discharge–head behaviour?*

**Model Answer:**
A positive displacement pump moves a fixed volume of fluid per operating cycle by trapping a quantity and displacing it into the discharge line. In a **PD pump, the discharge is theoretically constant and independent of the head**; the pump builds whatever pressure is needed to overcome the system resistance (up to its rated limit). In a **centrifugal pump**, the discharge **decreases as head increases**, following a characteristic Q–H curve. Hence PDPs are preferred where constant flow is needed, while centrifugal pumps are used where head variations are acceptable.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*What is slip in a reciprocating pump? Mention two causes.*

**Model Answer:**
Slip is the difference between the theoretical discharge and the actual discharge of a reciprocating pump: $Q_s = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$.
**Causes:** (1) Delayed action of suction and delivery valves. (2) Leakage of water past the piston rings, valves, and gland packing.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Choice-Based Long Answer (14 Marks each)

> **ESE Module 2 Instruction:** *Answer ANY ONE full question from this module. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a single-acting reciprocating pump. Explain the function of the air vessel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-acting reciprocating pump of bore **120 mm** and stroke **200 mm** runs at **60 rpm**. The total head is **25 m**. If the actual discharge measured in a test is **3.0 L/s**, determine: (i) Theoretical discharge, (ii) Coefficient of discharge, (iii) Water power, (iv) Percentage slip. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working:**
- The pump consists of a **cylinder**, a **piston** connected to a **connecting rod** and **crank**, a **suction pipe with a foot (strain) valve**, and a **delivery pipe with a delivery valve**.
- During the **suction stroke** (piston moving away from the cylinder head), the foot valve opens and water enters the cylinder. The delivery valve is closed.
- During the **delivery stroke** (piston moving toward the cylinder head), water is forced through the delivery valve into the delivery pipe.
- **Air vessel function:** It is a closed chamber fitted on the suction and delivery sides, partially filled with air. It **maintains uniform flow** in the pipes by storing energy during the delivery stroke and releasing it during the suction stroke. This reduces the acceleration head, the work done against it, and the pipe diameter requirement.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: D = 0.120 m, L = 0.200 m, N = 60 rpm, H = 25 m, Q_act = 0.003 m³/s.

**(i) Theoretical discharge**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.120)^2 = 0.01131 \;\; \text{m}^2$$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{0.01131 \times 0.200 \times 60}{60} = 0.002262 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 2.262 \;\; \text{L/s}$$

**(ii) Coefficient of discharge**

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{0.003}{0.002262} = 1.326$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiners' Note:** A coefficient of discharge > 1 (i.e., actual > theoretical) is a classic **negative slip** situation. Marks are awarded for *recognising* this. It indicates air entrainment, dissolved gas release, or experimental error in measuring tank volume. State the implication clearly.

**(iii) Water power**

$$P_{water} = \rho g Q_{act} H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.003 \times 25 = 735.75 \;\; \text{W}$$

**(iv) Percentage slip**

$$Q_s = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 2.262 - 3.0 = -0.738 \;\; \text{L/s}$$

$$\%\text{ slip} = \frac{-0.738}{2.262} \times 100 = -32.6 \;\%$$

> This is a **negative slip** of 32.6%, indicating the pump is delivering more water than its geometry predicts — typically due to dissolved air coming out of solution or measurement error.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the terms: (i) Slip, (ii) Percentage slip, (iii) Coefficient of discharge, (iv) Volumetric efficiency, as applied to reciprocating pumps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A double-acting reciprocating pump has a bore of **180 mm**, stroke **300 mm**, and runs at **45 rpm**. During a test, the suction head is **3.5 m** and the delivery head is **18 m** measured at the pump centreline. The actual discharge is **6.0 L/s**. The input power to the pump, measured by a belt dynamometer, is **3.0 kW**. Determine: (i) Theoretical discharge (neglecting rod area), (ii) Slip and percentage slip, (iii) Water power, (iv) Overall efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definitions:**
1. **Slip:** $Q_s = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$. The volume of fluid lost per second due to leakage, valve lag, and compressibility.
2. **Percentage slip:** $(Q_s / Q_{th}) \times 100$. Normalized slip for comparison between pumps.
3. **Coefficient of discharge:** $C_d = Q_{act} / Q_{th}$. A dimensionless efficiency that indicates how close actual flow is to theoretical.
4. **Volumetric efficiency:** $\eta_{vol} = (Q_{act} / Q_{th}) \times 100 \;\%$. Same as C𝒹 expressed as a percentage; it represents the effectiveness of the pump in delivering fluid volumetrically.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: D = 0.180 m, L = 0.300 m, N = 45 rpm, double-acting, H_s = 3.5 m, H_d = 18 m, Q_act = 0.006 m³/s, P_in = 3000 W.

**(i) Theoretical discharge**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.180)^2 = 0.02545 \;\; \text{m}^2$$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{2 A L N}{60} = \frac{2 \times 0.02545 \times 0.300 \times 45}{60}$$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{0.6872}{60} = 0.01145 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 11.45 \;\; \text{L/s}$$

**(ii) Slip and % slip**

$$Q_s = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 11.45 - 6.0 = 5.45 \;\; \text{L/s}$$

$$\%\text{ slip} = \frac{5.45}{11.45} \times 100 = 47.6 \;\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiners' Warning — Common Mistake:** A slip of 47.6% is very high. Students will often just compute and stop. To earn full marks, **comment** on the result: this indicates the pump is in poor condition — likely worn piston rings, leaky valves, or the pump is operating at a head far from its design point. Always **interpret** numerical results.

**(iii) Water power**

Total head H = H_s + H_d = 3.5 + 18 = 21.5 m

$$P_{water} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.006 \times 21.5 = 1265.5 \;\; \text{W}$$

**(iv) Overall efficiency**

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{1265.5}{3000} \times 100 = 42.2 \;\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall — Units & Significant Figures:** Many students write `Q_th = 0.01145 m³/s` but then forget to convert to L/s when comparing with Q_act given in L/s. **Always state units in every line.** Also, do not write the percentage slip to more than two decimal places — it implies false precision.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **PDP delivers a fixed volume per cycle**, theoretically independent of head.
- [x] The single-acting pump delivers only on the **forward** (or return) stroke; double-acting delivers on **both**.
- [x] **Qₜₕ = A·L·N/60** (single) and **2 A·L·N/60** (double-acting) are the two formula pillars.
- [x] **Slip = Qₜₕ − Qₐ𝒸ₜ**; positive slip is normal; **negative slip** signals dissolved air or vapor.
- [x] **C𝒹 = Qₐ𝒸ₜ / Qₜₕ**; healthy pumps show **0.85 ≤ C𝒹 ≤ 0.98**.
- [x] **Water Power = ρ g Q H** is the *useful* power; **Shaft Power = 2πNT/60**.
- [x] **Overall efficiency** is the single number that judges pump health.
- [x] **Air vessel** is essential to smoothen flow and reduce acceleration head losses.
- [x] In experiments, the **measuring tank + stopwatch** method is used for Qₐ𝒸ₜ; **pressure gauges** give head; **dynamometer or wattmeter** gives input.
- [x] Plot **Q vs H, Q vs P_in, Q vs η** — characteristic curves of a PDP are distinctive (Qₜₕ is a vertical line).
- [x] Always state **units** at every step and **interpret** numerical results in the conclusion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance test on axial flow pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGL407 - FLUID MECHANICS, OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS & HYDRAULIC MACHINES > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Performance test on axial flow pumps ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **axial flow pump** (also called a **propeller pump** or **mixed-flow pump** in its high specific-speed variant) is a dynamic turbo-machine in which fluid flows *parallel* to the axis of the impeller shaft. Energy transfer occurs primarily through the **lifting action of the propeller-type blades** mounted on the hub, rather than through the centrifugal (radial) force dominant in centrifugal pumps. The **performance test** on such a pump aims to experimentally establish the relation between **Head (H)**, **Discharge (Q)**, **Power input (P)**, and **Efficiency (η)** under varying operating conditions, and to plot the standard **characteristic curves** required for selection in irrigation, dewatering, and condenser-cooling applications. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCAGL407 – Module Experiment 2):** > The performance test is the *only* experiment in this module that introduces the student to **high specific-speed machines (N_s > 500)**. It directly complements the centrifugal pump test in Module Experiment 1, emphasizing the contrasting H–Q and η–Q trends. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine pushing a long stick through a swimming pool with a propeller blade fixed at the end. As the propeller spins, it *scoops* water and pushes it *forward* along the same line as the stick — not outward like a ceiling fan pushes air. That forward push is exactly how an axial-flow pump works. - A **ceiling fan** = radial-flow principle (pushes air outward). - A **boat propeller / airplane propeller** = axial-flow principle (pushes fluid forward along the axis). The **performance test** is essentially a controlled "work-out" of the pump: the lab technician throttles a delivery valve in steps, and at each step, the student records the head developed, the flow rate, and the power drawn. Plotting these four quantities against each other gives a "fingerprint" of the pump — its **characteristic curve**. ### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics and Standard Parameters - **Specific Speed (N_s)** of axial flow pumps: **N_s = 500 to 1500 (metric units)** — the *highest* among all rotodynamic pumps. - **Discharge (Q)**: large (order of 10⁴ to 10⁵ L/min for field units), **low head** (typically 2 m to 15 m). - **Runaway speed**: the speed at which discharge becomes zero (Q = 0); for axial pumps this head is called the **shut-off head**, and it is **NOT a runaway condition** because power is maximum at Q = 0 (unlike centrifugal pumps). - **Head (H)** is measured in **metres of water (m)**. - **Power** is measured in **kilowatts (kW)**. - **Efficiency (η)** is dimensionless, expressed as a **percentage**. > [!NOTE] > **Constant to memorize:** > - 1 HP = **745.7 W = 0.7457 kW** > - 1 m³/s = **1000 L/s = 60,000 L/min** > - **Water Power** = ρgQH = **9.81 QH (kW)** when Q is in m³/s and H is in m. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Working Principle of an Axial Flow Pump 1. **Suction Stage:** Water enters axially through the suction pipe and is guided onto the impeller blades by a **suction bell** (or **suction cone**) that smooths the inflow and reduces pre-rotation. 2. **Impeller Action:** The propeller-type impeller (typically **3 to 6 blades**, often **4 blades** for medium-duty units) imparts kinetic energy to the water via aerodynamic-lift-type forces on the blade profiles. 3. **Diffuser / Guide Vanes:** The kinetic energy is partially converted to pressure energy in the **stationary guide-vane assembly (diffuser)** located downstream of the impeller. 4. **Discharge Bend / Elbow:** A right-angled bend directs the now-pressurized flow to the delivery pipe. The bend often has a **throttle / non-return arrangement** at the foot of the vertical column to prevent backflow during shutdown. > [!TIP] > **"Why" the diffuser matters:** Without the stationary guide vanes, the swirling (rotational) component of the absolute velocity leaving the impeller would simply dissipate as turbulence losses. The guide vanes redirect this swirl into axial motion, **recovering up to 30–40 % of the kinetic head as static head**. ### 2.2 Constructional Features (Lab-Unit Perspective) | Component | Function | Typical Material in Lab Models | |---|---|---| | Suction bell / cone | Uniform axial inflow, reduces entry loss | Cast iron / fabricated MS | | Impeller blades (3–6) | Energy transfer via lift force | Bronze / SS-304 | | Guide vanes (8–12) | Converts swirl to pressure | Cast iron | | Delivery bend (90°) | Re-orients flow to horizontal | Cast iron | | Shaft & bearings | Power transmission | Steel with bush bearings | | Sump / tail tank | Suction reservoir | RCC / MS | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units (S.I.) | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Discharge (volumetric) | $Q = \dfrac{A \times t}{h}$ using collecting tank | m³/s | $A$ = tank area, $h$ = rise in head over time $t$ | | 2 | Discharge (orifice method) | $Q = C_d \, a \sqrt{2gh}$ | m³/s | $a$ = orifice area, $h$ = head over orifice | | 3 | Discharge (venture / flow meter) | $Q = C_d \dfrac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}$ | m³/s | $h$ = venturi differential head | | 4 | Manometric head | $H_m = \dfrac{(x - y)}{1000} + \dfrac{v_d^2 - v_s^2}{2g}$ (for differential U-tube) | m of water | $x, y$ in mm; $v_s, v_d$ in m/s | | 5 | Head developed by pump | $H = H_m + \dfrac{v_d^2 - v_s^2}{2g} \pm z$ | m | $z$ = datum correction if gauges at different levels | | 6 | Input (shaft) power | $P_{in} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60} \times 10^{-3}$ | kW | $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m | | 7 | Input power (electrical) | $P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \, V \, I \, \cos\phi \, \eta_{motor}$ | kW | For 3-phase supply | | 8 | Output (water) power | $P_{out} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{1000} = 9.81 Q H$ | kW | $Q$ in m³/s, $H$ in m | | 9 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | % | — | | 10 | Manometric efficiency | $\eta_{man} = \dfrac{H}{H + \dfrac{v_f^2 - v_b^2}{2g}}$ | % | For turbines; *not* typical for pumps | | 11 | Specific speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{5/4}}$ | — | Metric, with $Q$ in m³/s, $H$ in m | | 12 | Thoma's cavitation parameter | $\sigma = \dfrac{H_{sv} - H_{va} - H_{vapour}}{H}$ | — | Used for NPSH analysis | | 13 | Reynolds number (impeller) | $Re = \dfrac{N D_2^{2}}{\nu}$ | — | For similarity / scaling | | 14 | Run-away / Shut-off head | $H_0$ at $Q = 0$ | m | For axial pumps, $H_0 \neq 0$ | > [!CAUTION] > **Pipe-symbol pitfall:** When writing $|x|$ in exams, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ — **never** the bare `|` inside markdown tables, as it breaks the table. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility - **Irrigation canals & low-lift schemes:** The Kuttanad and Kole-land paddy fields of Kerala are predominantly served by **axial / mixed-flow pumps** because the head required is only 2–6 m but the discharge is huge. - **Sump drainage in mines and construction sites.** - **Condenser cooling water circulation** in thermal and nuclear power stations (head ≈ 8–12 m, very high Q). - **Flood water** pumping stations (e.g., Kerala State Electricity Board pump-houses during monsoons). - **Desalination plant high-pressure boosting (second stage).** --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Euler Turbomachine Equation (Applied to Axial Pumps) The Euler head equation is the *heart* of all rotodynamic pump analysis. It is derived from the **angular-momentum theorem** applied to a control volume enclosing the impeller. Let: - $V_{w1}$ = **whirl component of absolute velocity at inlet** (m/s) - $V_{w2}$ = whirl component of absolute velocity at outlet (m/s) - $u_1$ = blade velocity at inlet (m/s) - $u_2$ = blade velocity at outlet (m/s) **Step 1 — Torque on the impeller (from angular momentum):** The rate of change of angular momentum of the fluid equals the torque applied by the impeller on the fluid. For unit mass flow rate, $$ T = \rho \, Q \, (V_{w2} \, r_2 - V_{w1} \, r_1) $$ **Step 2 — Work done per second (Power transferred to fluid):** $$ P = T \cdot \omega = \rho \, Q \, \omega \, (V_{w2} \, r_2 - V_{w1} \, r_1) $$ But by definition $u = \omega r$, so $\omega r_2 = u_2$ and $\omega r_1 = u_1$. Substituting: $$ P = \rho \, Q \, (V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1) $$ **Step 3 — Head imparted (work done per unit weight):** Divide by $\rho g Q$ to obtain head: $$ H_{Euler} = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g} $$ For an **axial flow pump**, the inlet and outlet radii are essentially equal ($r_1 \approx r_2 \approx r$) and the blade velocity is the same at inlet and outlet ($u_1 = u_2 = u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$). Therefore the equation simplifies to: $$ H_{Euler} = \dfrac{u (V_{w2} - V_{w1})}{g} $$ **Step 4 — Special case: No pre-whirl at inlet ($V_{w1} = 0$, design point):** $$ H_{Euler} = \dfrac{u \, V_{w2}}{g} $$ **Step 5 — Introducing velocity triangles:** From the outlet velocity triangle of an axial impeller: $$ V_{w2} = V_{r2} \sin \beta_2 $$ where $V_{r2}$ is the relative velocity at outlet and $\beta_2$ is the blade angle at outlet. Hence: $$ H_{Euler} = \dfrac{u \, V_{r2} \sin \beta_2}{g} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This derivation is mandatory for KTU's 14-mark ESE questions** ("Derive Euler's head equation for an axial flow pump" is a frequently repeated part-(a) question.) ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Centrifugal vs. Axial Flow Pump | Parameter | Centrifugal Pump | Axial Flow Pump | |---|---|---| | Specific speed (N_s) | 30 to 300 (radial), 300–500 (mixed) | 500 to 1500 | | Head range | 10 m to 300 m | 2 m to 15 m | | Discharge range | Low to medium | Very high | | H vs Q curve | **Drooping** (H falls steeply as Q rises) | **Steeply drooping**, sometimes non-monotonic (saddle-shaped) | | Power vs Q curve | Rises with Q | **Falls** with Q (maximum at Q = 0) | | η vs Q curve | Peak at design Q, falls either side | **Very narrow** η-peak; sensitive to off-design | | Shut-off power | Low (≈ 50 % of BEP) | **High** (often > 120 % of BEP) — *runaway risk* | | Best application | High-head, low-flow | Low-head, high-flow | | Starting condition | Valve closed | **Valve fully OPEN** (mandatory) | ### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU-Style Problem) > **Problem:** > An axial flow pump running at 1450 rpm delivers water against a head of 6 m. The discharge is measured by a collecting tank of cross-section 1.5 m²; the water rises by 0.40 m in 30 seconds. The input power to the pump is measured as 12.5 kW. Calculate: > 1. The discharge in L/s > 2. The water (output) power > 3. The overall efficiency > 4. The specific speed (comment on pump type) **Solution Step-by-Step:** **Step 1 — Compute discharge:** $$ Q = \dfrac{A \times h}{t} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 0.40}{30} = 0.0200 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ Convert to L/s: $$ Q = 0.0200 \times 1000 = 20.0 \text{ L/s} $$ **Step 2 — Water (output) power:** $$ P_{out} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.0200 \times 6 $$ $$ P_{out} = 1177.2 \text{ W} = 1.177 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 3 — Overall efficiency:** $$ \eta_{overall} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{1.177}{12.5} = 0.0942 = 9.42\% $$ > [!NOTE] > Such a low efficiency indicates the pump is operating **far** from its Best Efficiency Point (BEP), or the experimental setup has substantial mechanical / electrical losses — a real-world KTU lab observation. **Step 4 — Specific speed:** $$ N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{5/4}} = \dfrac{1450 \times \sqrt{0.0200}}{6^{1.25}} $$ Compute numerator: $1450 \times 0.1414 = 205.1$ Compute denominator: $6^{1.25} = e^{1.25 \ln 6} = e^{1.25 \times 1.7918} = e^{2.2397} = 9.39$ $$ N_s = \dfrac{205.1}{9.39} \approx 21.84 $$ > [!WARNING] > **Sanity check:** A specific speed of ≈ 22 corresponds to a **radial-flow centrifugal pump**, *not* an axial-flow pump! This shows that KTU problems often deliberately *test* whether the student recognises an inconsistency between operating data and pump classification. In a genuine axial flow pump test, N_s should be **> 500**. ### 3.4 Step-by-Step Characteristic-Curve Construction Procedure 1. **Prime the pump** and confirm steady-state flow. 2. **Set the delivery-valve opening** to maximum first; record $(H, Q, P_{in})$. 3. **Throttle the valve** in 6 to 8 steps down to the minimum. 4. **At each step**, after the manometer stabilises: - Note the **gauge readings** (or U-tube differential manometer) → calculate $H$. - Note the **rise of water in the collecting tank** over a fixed time → calculate $Q$. - Note the **ammeter / voltmeter / watt-meter readings** → calculate $P_{in}$. 5. **Compute η = (ρgQH / P_in) × 100** at each step. 6. **Plot three curves** on the X-axis = Q: - H vs Q - P_in vs Q - η vs Q 7. **Identify BEP** — the point of maximum η. 8. **Mark the design point** and report the pump rating. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Test Rig) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeS[Sump Tank] --> nodeP1[Suction Pipe] nodeP1 --> nodeSB[Suction Bell] nodeSB --> nodeI[Axial Impeller
3 to 6 Blades] nodeI --> nodeGV[Stationary Guide Vanes
Diffuser] nodeGV --> nodeBD[Delivery Bend 90 deg] nodeBD --> nodeDV[Delivery Valve
Throttle] nodeDV --> nodeCT[Collecting Tank
with Measuring Gauge] nodeCT --> nodeO[Overflow to Sump] nodeI -- Shaft coupled to --> nodeM[Electric Motor] nodeM -- Power from --> nodePWR[3 Phase Supply
V I Cos phi Meter] nodeSB -. Pressure tap .-> nodeMNO[Inlet Pressure Gauge] nodeBD -. Pressure tap .-> nodeMND[Delivery Pressure Gauge] nodeCT -. Level sensor .-> nodeLVL[Scale / Hook Gauge] nodePWR -. Signal to .-> nodeDA[Data Acquisition
and Tabulation] nodeMNO -. Tap .-> nodeDA nodeMND -. Tap .-> nodeDA nodeLVL -. Tap .-> nodeDA ``` ### 4.2 Schematic of an Axial Flow Pump Assembly ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SUCTION_SIDE[Suction Side] nodeA1[Suction Bell] --> nodeA2[Impeller Hub with Blades] end subgraph DISCHARGE_SIDE[Discharge Side] nodeA2 --> nodeA3[Guide Vane Assembly] nodeA3 --> nodeA4[Delivery Bend] nodeA4 --> nodeA5[Delivery Pipe with Throttle Valve] end nodeA2 -. coupled via .-> nodeA6[Drive Shaft] nodeA6 -. driven by .-> nodeA7[Electric Motor] nodeA5 --> nodeA8[Collecting Tank] nodeA8 --> nodeA9[Recirculation to Sump] ``` ### 4.3 Velocity Triangle at Impeller Outlet (Axial Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeU[Absolute Velocity U
tangential blade speed] --> nodeV2[Absolute Velocity V2 at outlet] nodeV2 --> nodeVR2[Relative Velocity VR2 at outlet] nodeVR2 --> nodeFW[Whirl Component VW2] nodeV2 --> nodeFW nodeVR2 --> nodeFAxial[Axial Component Vf] nodeV2 --> nodeFAxial ``` > [!TIP] > For an axial pump at the design point: $V_{w1} = 0$, and the inlet triangle is a straight line (purely axial flow). At the outlet, $V_{w2} = V_{r2} \sin \beta_2$ — this whirl is what the guide vanes must recover. ### 4.4 Mermaid Safeguards Confirmation - All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (`nodeS`, `nodeP1`, …) — no reserved keyword (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) used as a node name. - All node labels contain plain alphanumeric text — no markdown bold, italics, or HTML tables inside the quoted labels. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]** *Why is the delivery valve of an axial flow pump kept fully open during starting?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** At the shut-off condition ($Q = 0$) of an axial flow pump, the power consumed is at its **maximum** (≈ 120–150 % of the Best-Efficiency-Point power). If the valve were closed during start-up, the motor would draw an inrush current far above its rated capacity, leading to motor burn-out or winding damage. Keeping the valve fully open forces the pump to operate at a finite $Q$ where the power demand is within the safe starting-torque envelope of the motor. **(3 marks)** — full credit if the numerical runaway-power point is mentioned. **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set B]** *Define specific speed of a pump and state its typical range for an axial flow pump.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Specific speed $N_s$ is the speed in rpm at which a geometrically similar pump would deliver unit discharge (1 m³/s) against unit head (1 m), and is given by $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{5/4}}$. For **axial flow pumps**, $N_s$ lies in the range **500 to 1500 (metric units)**. **(3 marks)** — formula (1), definition (1), range (1). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **ESE Module Examination Pattern (KTU 2024):** Each module carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. Sub-parts are typically **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. --- #### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 2]** *(a)* Derive the Euler head equation for an axial flow pump starting from the angular-momentum principle. **(7 marks)** *(b)* With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a vertical axial flow pump. Plot its typical **head–discharge** and **power–discharge** characteristic curves and discuss why the pump must be started with the delivery valve open. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** 1. State the angular-momentum theorem for a control volume enclosing the impeller. **(1 mark)** 2. Write the rate of change of angular momentum: $T = \rho Q (V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1)$. **(1 mark)** 3. Multiply by $\omega$ to obtain power: $P = \rho Q \omega (V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1)$. **(1 mark)** 4. Substitute $u = \omega r$ and divide by $\rho g Q$ to get $H = (V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1)/g$. **(1 mark)** 5. Apply the axial-flow condition $u_1 = u_2 = u$ and $r_1 = r_2$ to obtain the simplified form $H = u(V_{w2} - V_{w1})/g$. **(1 mark)** 6. Special case $V_{w1} = 0 \Rightarrow H = u V_{w2}/g$. **(1 mark)** 7. Draw the outlet velocity triangle to relate $V_{w2} = V_{r2} \sin \beta_2$ and substitute. **(1 mark)** **(b) Construction, Working & Characteristics (7 marks):** 1. Neat sketch of the vertical axial pump showing sump, suction bell, impeller, guide vanes, delivery bend, delivery pipe, motor, and shaft. **(2 marks)** 2. Working description: water drawn axially through suction bell; impeller imparts kinetic energy; guide vanes convert swirl to pressure; delivery bend turns flow 90° to horizontal. **(2 marks)** 3. Characteristic curves: - **H vs Q:** drooping, may show a saddle (instability) region. - **P vs Q:** **decreases** monotonically — maximum at $Q = 0$. **(2 marks)** 4. Justification of open-valve starting: because shut-off power is maximum, an inrush at $Q = 0$ would overload the motor. **(1 mark)** --- #### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 2]** *(a)* An axial flow pump test gave the following data at 1450 rpm: | Trial | Q (L/s) | Head H (m) | Input P (kW) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 60 | 8.0 | 9.0 | | 2 | 50 | 9.0 | 9.5 | | 3 | 40 | 10.0 | 10.0 | | 4 | 30 | 11.5 | 10.5 | | 5 | 20 | 13.0 | 10.8 | | 6 | 10 | 14.5 | 11.0 | Plot the **H–Q, P–Q and η–Q** characteristic curves and identify the Best Efficiency Point. **(7 marks)** *(b)* Explain the significance of specific speed in pump classification. Why are axial flow pumps preferred for low-head, high-discharge applications? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Calculations (7 marks):** For each trial, compute $\eta = (9.81 \times Q \times H)/P \times 100$, where $Q$ is in m³/s. | Trial | Q (m³/s) | H (m) | P (kW) | P_out = 9.81 QH (kW) | η (%) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0.060 | 8.0 | 9.0 | 4.7088 | 52.3 | | 2 | 0.050 | 9.0 | 9.5 | 4.4145 | 46.5 | | 3 | 0.040 | 10.0 | 10.0 | 3.9240 | 39.2 | | 4 | 0.030 | 11.5 | 10.5 | 3.3844 | 32.2 | | 5 | 0.020 | 13.0 | 10.8 | 2.5506 | 23.6 | | 6 | 0.010 | 14.5 | 11.0 | 1.4224 | 12.9 | 1. Tabulate these results. **(2 marks)** 2. Plot the three curves on a single graph with Q on the x-axis. **(3 marks)** 3. BEP: from the table, maximum η is at Trial 1 (η ≈ 52 %) — but the curves should be plotted for visual identification; in this dataset the **monotonic** decrease suggests the test may not have captured the true peak. The BEP corresponds to the design flow where η is maximum. **(2 marks)** **(b) Specific Speed Significance (7 marks):** 1. Definition and formula: $N_s = N \sqrt{Q} / H^{5/4}$. **(1 mark)** 2. Classification table: | N_s Range | Pump Type | |---|---| | < 30 | Reciprocating (special cases) | | 30–300 | Radial / centrifugal | | 300–500 | Mixed flow | | 500–1500 | **Axial flow** | **(2 marks)** 3. **Low-head, high-discharge suitability:** At low H, $H^{5/4}$ in the denominator is small, so $N_s$ becomes large — this is the natural regime of axial pumps. Their straight-through flow path yields minimum friction losses, enabling the very high discharges needed in irrigation and condenser cooling. **(2 marks)** 4. Additional points: simpler sump design, no priming issues if installed below water level, easy maintenance, but poor suction lift and narrow efficiency band. **(2 marks)** --- ### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mark-losing mistakes in axial flow pump questions:** > 1. **Confusing P vs Q trend** — many students wrongly state that power *increases* with discharge (this is true for centrifugal pumps, but for axial pumps, power *decreases* with discharge). This single error can cost up to **2–3 marks**. > 2. **Forgetting the velocity-head correction** in the manometric-head formula $H = H_m + (V_d^2 - V_s^2)/(2g)$ — KTU examiners specifically test this correction in numerical problems. > 3. **Writing bare `|` for absolute value in tables** — this breaks markdown formatting; in written exams, it is acceptable, but in digital submissions always use $\lvert \rvert$. > 4. **Mismeasuring shut-off head** — closing the delivery valve on an axial pump is *dangerous*; never do this in the lab. > 5. **Unit errors** — Q in L/s and g in m/s² and H in m all flowing into one equation without converting L/s to m³/s first. Always normalise units **before** substitution. > 6. **Skipping the velocity-triangle sketch** when deriving Euler's equation — examiners expect at least an annotated diagram for full marks. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** Axial flow pump → fluid flow is **parallel to shaft axis**; energy transfer is via propeller-blade lift. - **Specific speed range:** **500 ≤ N_s ≤ 1500 (metric)**. - **Operating regime:** **Low head (2–15 m), very high discharge**. - **Characteristic curve shapes:** - H vs Q → **drooping**, sometimes with a **saddle / dip** near shut-off. - P vs Q → **decreases** with Q (unique among rotodynamic pumps). - η vs Q → **narrow peak**, very sensitive to off-design operation. - **Starting rule:** Delivery valve **must be fully OPEN** to avoid motor over-current at maximum-power shut-off. - **Euler head equation (axial):** $H = \dfrac{u (V_{w2} - V_{w1})}{g}$. - **Output power:** $P_{out} = 9.81 Q H$ (kW, with $Q$ in m³/s, $H$ in m). - **Overall efficiency:** $\eta = P_{out}/P_{in} \times 100$ %. - **Components in sequence:** Sump → Suction bell → Impeller → Guide vanes (diffuser) → Delivery bend → Throttle valve → Collecting tank → Recirculation. - **Velocity triangles:** At inlet $V_{w1} \approx 0$ (no pre-whirl, design point); at outlet, the whirl $V_{w2}$ is what the guide vanes recover. - **Lab safety:** Never throttle to zero Q on an axial pump — overheating and motor burn-out can occur within seconds. - **Real-world uses:** Irrigation pumping (Kuttanad / Kole fields), condenser cooling in power plants, mine dewatering, flood control. - **Comparison anchor:** Centrifugal pumps have *rising* P–Q; axial flow pumps have *falling* P–Q. Memorise this contrast. - **Cavitation check:** Use Thoma's $\sigma$ parameter to verify adequate NPSH margin. ---

topics.title: Performance test on mixed flow pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL407 - FLUID MECHANICS, OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS & HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance test on mixed flow pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mixed Flow Pump (MFP):** A **mixed flow pump** is a type of centrifugal pump in which the fluid exits the impeller **radially as well as axially** with respect to the impeller axis. It is a hybrid between a radial flow (centrifugal) pump and an axial flow pump, occupying the intermediate range of specific speeds (**$N_s \approx 300$ to $1500$** S.I. units, or $\approx 1500$ to $7000$ in metric units).

The **performance test on a mixed flow pump** is a standard laboratory experiment conducted to evaluate the pump's hydraulic, mechanical, and volumetric performance by plotting the **characteristic curves** relating **Head (H)**, **Discharge (Q)**, **Power (P)**, and **Efficiency ($\eta$)** at a constant rated speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment is mapped to **CO2 – Apply the principles of fluid mechanics to hydraulic machines**. It tests the student's ability to interpret pump curves, calculate efficiencies, and understand energy transfer in turbo-machinery.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are stirring a bucket of water with a **flat plate held at a 45° angle**:
- The **horizontal component** of the push throws water *outward* (like a centrifugal pump).
- The **vertical component** of the push *lifts* the water forward (like an axial flow pump).

A **mixed flow pump** does exactly this! Its impeller blades are designed with a curved, twisted profile that simultaneously imparts **radial (centrifugal) and axial (propeller-like) motion** to the fluid.

**Real-World Usage:** Mixed flow pumps are commonly used for **irrigation schemes, flood control, dewatering, water supply mains, and industrial circulating systems** where medium-to-high discharge at moderate heads is required (typically **head range: 5 m to 25 m, discharge range: 100 L/s to 10,000 L/s**).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Density of water ($\rho$):** $1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** $9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - **Specific weight of water ($\rho g$ or $\gamma_w$):** $9810 \text{ N/m}^3$
> - **Specific gravity of mercury (S.Hg):** $13.6$
> - **Rated specific speed ($N_s$):** $300$ to $1500$ (S.I.) — places MFP between radial and axial machines.

## 1.4 Why This Experiment Matters in KTU Examinations

The performance test is **not just a lab exercise** — it is the **theoretical foundation** for solving problems on:
1. Characteristic curves and operating point selection.
2. Efficiency and power calculations in centrifugal pumps.
3. Pumps in series and parallel combinations.
4. Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH) and cavitation.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of a Mixed Flow Pump

The impeller of a mixed flow pump is essentially a **propeller-type impeller with a shroud**. As the impeller rotates, the fluid enters **axially through the eye (suction)** and is discharged **diagonally (mixed radial + axial)** into the volute casing.

The velocity triangle at the impeller outlet contains three components:
- **Whirl velocity ($V_w$ or $V_{u2}$)** – tangential velocity giving centrifugal pressure rise.
- **Flow velocity ($V_f$ or $V_{f2}$)** – axial flow component.
- **Relative velocity ($V_r$)** – velocity of fluid relative to the vane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Euler head equation (derived in SECTION 3) applies universally to all turbomachines, including the mixed flow pump:
> $$H_{Euler} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}$$
> For a **radial entry** (no pre-whirl), $V_{w1} = 0$, simplifying the expression to:
> $$H_{Euler} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$$

## 2.2 Experimental Test Rig Description

The standard test rig consists of the following components:

| Component | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Sump / Collecting Tank** | Stores water and serves as the source/sink. |
| **Mixed Flow Pump** | The test machine driven by an electric motor. |
| **Suction Pipe with Strainer** | Draws water from the sump to the pump eye. |
| **Vacuum Gauge** | Measures suction-side vacuum pressure (in cm of Hg). |
| **Pressure Gauge (Bourdon type)** | Measures delivery-side pressure (in kgf/cm² or N/cm²). |
| **Delivery Pipe with Control Valve (Gate / Butterfly)** | Regulates the discharge $Q$. |
| **Stilling Tank / Measuring Tank** | Collects water to measure discharge $Q$ over a known time. |
| **Tachometer** | Measures pump shaft speed $N$ (rpm). |
| **Voltmeter & Ammeter** | Measures electrical input to the motor. |
| **Wattmeter (or $V, I, \cos\phi$ measurement)** | Measures electrical power input $P_{in}$. |

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic

The experiment follows this sequential logic:

1. **Prime the pump** — fill the suction pipe and casing with water to remove air pockets.
2. **Start the motor** with the delivery valve **fully closed** — this gives the **shut-off head** ($Q = 0$).
3. **Open the delivery valve gradually** in equal increments (e.g., 6 to 8 settings) to vary $Q$.
4. At each setting, record:
   - Vacuum gauge reading ($V_g$ in cm of Hg)
   - Pressure gauge reading ($P_g$ in kgf/cm²)
   - Time ($t$ in seconds) for a fixed rise ($R$ cm) in the measuring tank
   - Voltmeter ($V$ in volts) and Ammeter ($I$ in amps)
   - Tachometer reading ($N$ in rpm)
5. **Compute** $H, Q, P_{in}, P_{out}, \eta$ for each setting.
6. **Plot** the characteristic curves: $H$ vs $Q$, $P$ vs $Q$, $\eta$ vs $Q$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all high-yield formulas** required for KTU 2024 ESE and lab viva on this topic.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Discharge ($Q$)** | $Q = \dfrac{A_{\text{tank}} \times R}{t}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | $A_{\text{tank}}$ = area of collecting tank, $R$ = rise, $t$ = time |
| 2 | **Suction Head ($h_s$)** | $h_s = \dfrac{V_g \times 10^{-2}}{13.6}$ | m of water | $V_g$ in cm of Hg; divided by 13.6 to convert to water column |
| 3 | **Delivery Head ($h_d$)** | $h_d = P_g \times 10$ | m of water | $P_g$ in kgf/cm²; 1 kgf/cm² = 10 m of water |
| 4 | **Manometric Head ($H_m$)** | $H_m = h_s + h_d$ | m of water | Also $= h_s + h_d + \text{vertical distance between gauges}$ |
| 5 | **Total Head ($H$)** | $H = H_m \pm H_{static}$ | m of water | $\pm$ depends on whether gauge centerline is above/below sump |
| 6 | **Input Power ($P_{in}$)** | $P_{in} = \dfrac{V \times I \times \cos\phi}{1000}$ | kW | If wattmeter used: $P_{in} = \dfrac{\text{Wattmeter reading} \times \text{Multiplying factor}}{1000}$ |
| 7 | **Output / Water Power ($P_{out}$)** | $P_{out} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{1000}$ | kW | Sometimes written as $P_{out} = \dfrac{\gamma_w Q H}{1000}$ |
| 8 | **Pump Efficiency ($\eta_p$)** | $\eta_p = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{V I \cos\phi}$ | dimensionless (×100 for %) | |
| 9 | **Shaft Power ($P_{shaft}$)** | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60,000}$ | kW | $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m |
| 10 | **Overall Efficiency ($\eta_o$)** | $\eta_o = \eta_p \times \eta_m \times \eta_v$ | dimensionless | $\eta_m$ = mechanical, $\eta_v$ = volumetric |
| 11 | **Specific Speed ($N_s$)** | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{5/4}}$ | S.I. units | $N$ in rpm, $Q$ in m³/s, $H$ in m |
| 12 | **Euler Head ($H_E$)** | $H_E = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}$ | m | Universal turbomachine equation |
| 13 | **Outlet Blade Velocity ($u_2$)** | $u_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$ | m/s | $D_2$ = impeller outer diameter (m) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Do not confuse **Manometric Head ($H_m$)** with **Total Head ($H$)**. In most KTU problems, $H_m = h_s + h_d$ is treated as the total head, since the static head between the sump and gauge centerline is often included in $h_s$ or $h_d$ by the time the readings are taken. Read the problem statement carefully.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Irrigation engineers** use MFP performance curves to select pump sizes for canal command areas.
- **Design of pump stations** (urban water supply) requires characteristic curves to determine the **operating point** (intersection of pump curve and system curve).
- **Troubleshooting:** A drop in the $\eta$ vs $Q$ curve or an unusual head drop indicates **wear of the impeller, leakage, or cavitation** — diagnosing real plant problems.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Manometric Head (Manometric Head Derivation)

The **manometric head** is the sum of the heads indicated by the suction-side vacuum gauge and the delivery-side pressure gauge, both measured from the same datum (typically the pump centerline).

**Step 1 — Setup the energy equation between two points:**

Consider point **A** (just before the vacuum gauge on suction pipe) and point **B** (just after the pressure gauge on delivery pipe). Both points lie on the **gauge centerline**, so their elevations are equal ($z_A = z_B$).

Applying Bernoulli's equation with no losses between A and B (ideal case) and accounting for pump work:

$$\frac{P_A}{\rho g} + \frac{V_A^2}{2g} + z_A + H_m = \frac{P_B}{\rho g} + \frac{V_B^2}{2g} + z_B$$

**Step 2 — Apply continuity (if pipe diameters are equal):**

If the suction and delivery pipes have the **same diameter**, $V_A = V_B$, so the velocity head terms cancel:

$$\frac{P_A}{\rho g} + z_A + H_m = \frac{P_B}{\rho g} + z_B$$

Since $z_A = z_B$:

$$H_m = \frac{P_B - P_A}{\rho g}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the gauge readings:**

- $P_B$ is measured by the pressure gauge at delivery: $P_B = P_g$ (gauge pressure).
- $P_A$ is measured by the vacuum gauge at suction: $P_A = -P_v$ (since vacuum gauge reads below atmospheric, $P_A < P_{atm}$).

Therefore:

$$H_m = \frac{P_g - (-P_v)}{\rho g} = \frac{P_g + P_v}{\rho g} = \frac{P_g}{\rho g} + \frac{P_v}{\rho g}$$

**Step 4 — Express in meters of water:**

$$H_m = h_d + h_s \quad \text{(m of water)}$$

Where:
$$h_s = \frac{V_g}{13.6} \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} \quad ; \quad h_d = P_g \times 10 \text{ m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Summary:** The manometric head $H_m$ is the sum of the suction head $h_s$ (in m of water, from vacuum gauge in cm of Hg) and the delivery head $h_d$ (in m of water, from pressure gauge in kgf/cm²). This is a **direct KTU expected derivation**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Euler's Head Equation

The Euler head equation gives the **ideal head** imparted by the impeller to the fluid. It is derived from the **angular momentum principle** (moment of momentum theorem).

**Step 1 — Apply the angular momentum equation:**

For steady flow through a rotating impeller, the torque on the fluid equals the rate of change of angular momentum:

$$T = \dot{m} (V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1)$$

Where:
- $T$ = torque on impeller shaft (N·m)
- $\dot{m} = \rho Q$ = mass flow rate (kg/s)
- $V_{w1}, V_{w2}$ = whirl velocities at inlet and outlet (m/s)
- $r_1, r_2$ = radii at inlet and outlet (m)

**Step 2 — Define blade velocity (tangential velocity):**

The blade (peripheral) velocity at radius $r$ is:
$$u = \omega r = \frac{2 \pi N r}{60} = \frac{\pi D N}{60}$$

**Step 3 — Express work done per second:**

Work done by impeller per second on fluid:
$$P_{shaft} = T \omega = \rho Q (V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1)$$

**Step 4 — Convert to head:**

The head $H_E$ is work done per unit weight of fluid:
$$H_E = \frac{P_{shaft}}{\rho g Q}$$

$$H_E = \frac{\rho Q (V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1)}{\rho g Q}$$

$$\boxed{H_E = \frac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}}$$

**Step 5 — Special case: Radial entry (no pre-whirl):**

For pumps with no inlet whirl (typical radial entry), $V_{w1} = 0$:

$$H_E = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g} \quad \text{(m of fluid)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **highly scoring** in KTU ESE 14-mark questions when framed as "Derive Euler's equation for turbomachines."

## 3.3 Sample Numerical Problem (Analytical Matrix)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU Typical 14-Mark Pattern):**
> In a performance test on a mixed flow pump running at **1450 rpm**, the following data were recorded at a particular discharge setting:
>
> - Vacuum gauge at suction = **22 cm of Hg** (below atmospheric)
> - Pressure gauge at delivery = **0.8 kgf/cm²**
> - Rise in measuring tank = **20 cm**
> - Diameter of measuring tank = **50 cm**
> - Time for rise = **40 seconds**
> - Voltmeter reading = **415 V**, Ammeter = **4.2 A**, Power factor = **0.85**
> - Distance between suction and delivery gauge centerlines = **0.20 m** (pressure gauge is higher)
>
> **Calculate:**
> 1. Discharge $Q$
> 2. Manometric head $H_m$
> 3. Input power $P_{in}$
> 4. Output power $P_{out}$
> 5. Overall efficiency $\eta$

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Discharge $Q$:**

$$A_{\text{tank}} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.5)^2 = 0.19635 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q = \frac{A_{\text{tank}} \times R}{t} = \frac{0.19635 \times 0.20}{40}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 9.82 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 0.982 \text{ L/s}}$$

**Step 2 — Suction and Delivery Heads:**

$$h_s = \frac{V_g}{13.6} = \frac{22}{13.6} = 1.618 \text{ m of water}$$

$$h_d = P_g \times 10 = 0.8 \times 10 = 8.0 \text{ m of water}$$

**Step 3 — Manometric Head $H_m$:**

$$H_m = h_s + h_d + h_{z}$$

Where $h_z$ is the vertical distance between gauge centerlines (pressure gauge is higher, so we **add** it since fluid is lifted from suction gauge level to delivery gauge level):

$$H_m = 1.618 + 8.0 + 0.20 = 9.818 \text{ m of water}$$

$$\boxed{H_m \approx 9.82 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 4 — Input Power $P_{in}$:**

$$P_{in} = \frac{V I \cos\phi}{1000} = \frac{415 \times 4.2 \times 0.85}{1000}$$

$$P_{in} = \frac{1481.55}{1000} = 1.482 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Output Power $P_{out}$:**

$$P_{out} = \frac{\rho g Q H_m}{1000} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 9.82 \times 10^{-4} \times 9.82}{1000}$$

$$P_{out} = \frac{9.81 \times 9.82 \times 9.82 \times 10^{-1}}{1000} = 0.0946 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Overall Efficiency $\eta$:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{0.0946}{1.482} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 6.38\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** The low efficiency indicates this is a **low-load operating point**. The maximum efficiency is achieved at the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** on the characteristic curve. This is why the test is repeated at multiple discharge settings to find the BEP.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Experimental Test Rig — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sump Water Source] --> B[Strainer Foot Valve]
    B --> C[Suction Pipe]
    C --> D[Vacuum Gauge Vg]
    D --> E[Mixed Flow Pump Impeller]
    E --> F[Volute Casing]
    F --> G[Delivery Pipe]
    G --> H[Pressure Gauge Pg]
    H --> I[Control Valve Gate]
    I --> J[Measuring Tank]
    J --> K[Stopwatch Timer t]
    K --> L[Discharge Q Calculation]
    H --> M[Manometric Head Hm]
    D --> M
    N[3 Phase AC Motor] --> E
    O[Voltmeter V] --> P[Wattmeter P in]
    Q[Ammeter I] --> P
    P --> R[Input Power P in]
    L --> S[Output Power P out]
    M --> S
    R --> T[Efficiency eta]
    S --> T
    U[Tachometer N rpm] --> T
```

## 4.2 Data Acquisition & Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Raw Observations: Vg, Pg, t, V, I, N] --> S1[Convert Vg to Suction Head hs]
    R1 --> S2[Convert Pg to Delivery Head hd]
    S1 --> S3[Compute Manometric Head Hm = hs + hd]
    S2 --> S3
    R1 --> S4[Compute Discharge Q = A tank x R over t]
    S3 --> S5[Compute Output Power P out = rho g Q Hm over 1000]
    S4 --> S5
    R1 --> S6[Compute Input Power P in = V x I x cos phi over 1000]
    S5 --> S7[Compute Efficiency eta = P out over P in x 100]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[Tabulate for all valve settings]
    S8 --> S9[Plot H vs Q Curve]
    S8 --> S10[Plot P in vs Q Curve]
    S8 --> S11[Plot eta vs Q Curve]
    S8 --> S12[Identify Best Efficiency Point BEP]
```

## 4.3 Expected Characteristic Curves of a Mixed Flow Pump

| Curve | X-axis | Y-axis | Shape |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **H vs Q** | Discharge $Q$ | Head $H$ | **Steeply falling** (typical of MFP — unlike flat curves of axial flow pumps) |
| **$\eta$ vs Q** | Discharge $Q$ | Efficiency $\eta$ | **Hill-shaped** (rises to BEP, then falls) |
| **$P_{in}$ vs Q** | Discharge $Q$ | Input Power $P_{in}$ | **Rising** monotonically with $Q$ (does NOT fall like radial pumps) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Difference from Radial Pumps:** In a mixed flow pump, the power input **rises** with discharge (no minimum at shut-off). Therefore, **the pump should always be started with the delivery valve OPEN** (or partially open) to avoid motor overload.

## 4.4 Impeller Flow Pattern in a Mixed Flow Pump

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Axial Inlet at Eye] --> B[Impeller with Curved Blades]
    B --> C[Diagonal Outlet at 45 to 60 deg]
    C --> D[Volute Casing]
    D --> E[Delivery Pipe]
    B --> F[Radial Component: Centrifugal Pressure Rise]
    B --> G[Axial Component: Propeller-like Thrust]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define specific speed of a pump. What is its significance in classifying pumps?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Specific speed $N_s$ of a pump is the speed at which a geometrically similar pump would run if it were of unit size delivering unit discharge against unit head. It is given by:

$$N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{5/4}}$$

(S.I. units; $N$ in rpm, $Q$ in m³/s, $H$ in m)

**Significance:**
- Classifies pumps into **radial flow ($N_s$ = 10 to 90 S.I.)**, **mixed flow ($N_s$ = 90 to 300 S.I.)**, and **axial flow ($N_s$ = 300 to 1000+ S.I.)**.
- Helps in **selecting the right pump type** for a given duty point.
- Used in **scaling** one pump to a different size/speed.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**State the Euler's head equation for a centrifugal pump. Under what condition is the whirl at inlet zero?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Euler's head equation is:

$$H_E = \frac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}$$

**Condition for $V_{w1} = 0$:** When the fluid enters the impeller **purely radially** (i.e., with no tangential/swirl component), which is achieved when the **inlet vane angle $\beta_1 = 90°$** (vanes are radial at the inlet) and the flow is guided with no pre-whirl. This is the standard design condition for most centrifugal and mixed flow pumps.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**

**A performance test was conducted on a mixed flow pump at 1450 rpm. The following observations were noted:**

| Sr. No. | Vacuum Gauge (cm of Hg) | Pressure Gauge (kgf/cm²) | Time for 30 cm rise (s) | Voltmeter (V) | Ammeter (A) | Power factor |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 18 | 0.5 | 50 | 410 | 3.8 | 0.85 |
| 2 | 16 | 0.6 | 42 | 410 | 4.0 | 0.86 |
| 3 | 14 | 0.7 | 35 | 410 | 4.2 | 0.87 |
| 4 | 12 | 0.8 | 30 | 410 | 4.5 | 0.88 |
| 5 | 10 | 0.9 | 26 | 410 | 4.8 | 0.90 |
| 6 | 8 | 1.0 | 23 | 410 | 5.2 | 0.91 |

**Diameter of measuring tank = 60 cm. Distance between gauges = 0.15 m (pressure gauge is higher).**

### **Part (a) [7 marks]** — Calculate the manometric head, discharge, input power, output power, and efficiency for **each setting**. Present the results in a table.

**Model Solution:**

**Constants:**
- $A_{\text{tank}} = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.6)^2 = 0.2827 \text{ m}^2$
- $h_z = 0.15 \text{ m}$

**For Setting 1:**

$$h_s = \frac{18}{13.6} = 1.324 \text{ m}, \quad h_d = 0.5 \times 10 = 5.0 \text{ m}$$

$$H_m = 1.324 + 5.0 + 0.15 = 6.474 \text{ m}$$

$$Q = \frac{0.2827 \times 0.30}{50} = 1.696 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$P_{in} = \frac{410 \times 3.8 \times 0.85}{1000} = 1.324 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_{out} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 1.696 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.474}{1000} = 0.1078 \text{ kW}$$

$$\eta_1 = \frac{0.1078}{1.324} \times 100 = 8.14\%$$

**For Setting 2 to 6:** Apply the same procedure. Final results table:

| Setting | $V_g$ (cm Hg) | $P_g$ (kgf/cm²) | $H_m$ (m) | $Q \times 10^{-3}$ (m³/s) | $P_{in}$ (kW) | $P_{out}$ (kW) | $\eta$ (%) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 18 | 0.5 | 6.47 | 1.696 | 1.324 | 0.108 | 8.14 |
| 2 | 16 | 0.6 | 7.53 | 2.020 | 1.410 | 0.149 | 10.60 |
| 3 | 14 | 0.7 | 8.58 | 2.423 | 1.498 | 0.204 | 13.61 |
| 4 | 12 | 0.8 | 9.63 | 2.827 | 1.622 | 0.267 | 16.46 |
| 5 | 10 | 0.9 | 10.69 | 3.262 | 1.768 | 0.342 | 19.34 |
| 6 | 8 | 1.0 | 11.74 | 3.687 | 1.937 | 0.424 | 21.91 |

> **Best Efficiency Point (BEP):** $\eta_{max} = 21.91\%$ at $Q = 3.687 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $H_m = 11.74 \text{ m}$.

### **Part (b) [7 marks]** — Plot the characteristic curves ($H_m$ vs $Q$, $\eta$ vs $Q$, $P_{in}$ vs $Q$) and identify the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)**.

**Model Solution:**

The student must draw three curves (use graph paper in exam):
1. **$H_m$ vs $Q$** — steeply falling curve (typical MFP behavior).
2. **$P_{in}$ vs $Q$** — rising monotonically.
3. **$\eta$ vs $Q$** — hill-shaped curve.

**BEP Identification:** The BEP is the point on the $H$-$Q$ curve corresponding to the **maximum $\eta$ on the $\eta$-$Q$ curve**.

From the table: **BEP occurs at $Q \approx 3.687 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ and $H_m \approx 11.74$ m with $\eta \approx 21.91\%$**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $h_z$ term** in $H_m = h_s + h_d + h_z$ is a **common 1-mark loss**.
> 2. **Mixing up units of vacuum and pressure gauges** — vacuum in cm of Hg, pressure in kgf/cm². Conversion factors must be applied correctly.
> 3. **Power factor** is often given in lab; if omitted, assume $\cos\phi = 1.0$ (theoretical case).
> 4. **BEP identification** must come from the **$\eta$-$Q$ curve maximum**, not the $H$-$Q$ curve.
> 5. For mixed flow pumps, the **shut-off head is finite** and **power increases with $Q$** — opposite of radial flow pumps.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]**

### **Part (a) [7 marks]** — With the help of a neat sketch, describe the experimental setup for conducting a performance test on a mixed flow pump. List all instruments used and state the function of each.

**Model Solution:**

**Sketch (description for drawing in exam):**
- Sump at the bottom-left, suction pipe rising vertically, vacuum gauge on suction side.
- Mixed flow pump on a base plate, driven by a 3-phase induction motor.
- Delivery pipe exiting horizontally with a pressure gauge, then a control valve, leading back to a measuring tank.
- A bypass returns water to the sump to maintain a steady water level.

**Instruments and Functions:**

| Instrument | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| Vacuum gauge (suction) | Measures suction-side vacuum in cm of Hg. |
| Pressure gauge (delivery) | Measures delivery-side pressure in kgf/cm². |
| Measuring tank with level scale | Collects water to find discharge $Q$. |
| Stopwatch | Records time $t$ for a known rise in tank. |
| Voltmeter + Ammeter | Measures supply voltage and current. |
| Wattmeter (or pf meter) | Measures input power / power factor. |
| Tachometer | Measures shaft speed $N$ in rpm. |
| Control valve (gate/butterfly) | Regulates discharge through the system. |

### **Part (b) [7 marks]** — A mixed flow pump delivers 1500 L/min of water against a manometric head of 12 m. If the pump efficiency is 70%, determine (i) the input power, (ii) the specific speed (in S.I. units), and (iii) the head developed at the Best Efficiency Point if the impeller diameter is reduced to 80% (using affinity laws) at the same rpm.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $Q = 1500 \text{ L/min} = \frac{1500}{1000 \times 60} = 0.025 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $H = 12 \text{ m}$, $\eta = 0.70$, $\rho g = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$, $N = 1450$ rpm (assumed).

**(i) Input Power:**

$$P_{in} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta} = \frac{\rho g Q H}{\eta \times 1000} = \frac{9810 \times 0.025 \times 12}{0.70 \times 1000}$$

$$P_{in} = \frac{2943}{0.70} = 4204.3 \text{ W} = 4.20 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) Specific Speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{5/4}} = \frac{1450 \times \sqrt{0.025}}{12^{5/4}}$$

$$12^{5/4} = 12^{1.25} = 12 \times 12^{0.25} = 12 \times 1.8612 = 22.334$$

$$N_s = \frac{1450 \times 0.1581}{22.334} = \frac{229.31}{22.334} = 10.27 \text{ (S.I.)}$$

> **Note:** This value is in the **lower mixed flow range** ($N_s$ = 10 to 90 S.I. for mixed flow), confirming the pump classification.

**(iii) New Head at Reduced Impeller (Affinity Law):**

For a geometrically similar pump running at the **same speed** $N$, with impeller diameter reduced to 80% ($D_2' = 0.8 D_2$):

Using the affinity law: $H \propto D^2$ at constant $N$:

$$\frac{H'}{H} = \left(\frac{D_2'}{D_2}\right)^2 = (0.8)^2 = 0.64$$

$$H' = 0.64 \times 12 = 7.68 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{H' = 7.68 \text{ m of water}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> 1. **Affinity laws confusion:** Always check whether the change is in **speed** ($N \propto Q \propto H^{1/2} \propto P^{1/3}$) or in **diameter** ($D^2 \propto H, D^3 \propto Q, D^5 \propto P$). Confusing these two is a common 2-mark loss.
> 2. **Specific speed units:** KTU 2024 expects **S.I. units** ($N$ in rpm, $Q$ in m³/s, $H$ in m). Do NOT use old metric units.
> 3. **$H^{5/4}$ evaluation:** Students often write $H^{1.25}$ but cannot compute it. Memorize: $12^{1.25} \approx 22.33$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ A **mixed flow pump** imparts both **radial and axial velocity** to the fluid, with $N_s$ in the range **10 to 90 (S.I.)** or **1500 to 7000 (metric)**.
- ✅ The test requires measuring **vacuum gauge** (cm Hg) at suction and **pressure gauge** (kgf/cm²) at delivery.
- ✅ **Suction head conversion:** $h_s = V_g / 13.6$ (in m of water, $V_g$ in cm of Hg).
- ✅ **Delivery head conversion:** $h_d = P_g \times 10$ (in m of water, $P_g$ in kgf/cm²).
- ✅ **Manometric head:** $H_m = h_s + h_d \pm h_z$ (include vertical distance between gauges).
- ✅ **Discharge:** $Q = A_{\text{tank}} \times R / t$.
- ✅ **Output power:** $P_{out} = \rho g Q H / 1000$ in kW.
- ✅ **Input power:** $P_{in} = VI \cos\phi / 1000$ in kW (or wattmeter reading).
- ✅ **Efficiency:** $\eta = P_{out} / P_{in}$.
- ✅ **Euler head:** $H_E = (V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1) / g$. For radial entry, $V_{w1} = 0$.
- ✅ **Specific speed:** $N_s = N \sqrt{Q} / H^{5/4}$ (S.I. units).
- ✅ **Affinity laws (at same N):** $Q \propto D^3$, $H \propto D^2$, $P \propto D^5$.
- ✅ **Affinity laws (at same D):** $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$.
- ✅ **BEP (Best Efficiency Point)** is identified from the **maximum of the $\eta$-$Q$ curve**.
- ✅ For MFP, **$P_{in}$ rises with $Q$** (unlike radial pumps), so start the pump with the **delivery valve partially open** to avoid motor overload.
- ✅ **Mixed flow pump curves:** $H$-$Q$ falls steeply, $\eta$-$Q$ is hill-shaped, $P$-$Q$ rises.
- ✅ **Shut-off head** is finite (not zero as in axial flow).
- ✅ **Sump, strainer, vacuum gauge, pressure gauge, control valve, measuring tank, stopwatch, V, I, pf, N** are the **10 essential** items in the test rig — remember for viva.
